For patients experiencing statin myalgia, a 3-month trial at 200 mg/day is a reasonable approach to try before discontinuing or switching statins. Discuss with the prescribing physician. CoQ10 does not interact significantly with statins at these doses.

Sources

  1. Mortensen SA, et al. "The effect of coenzyme Q10 on morbidity and mortality in chronic heart failure (Q-SYMBIO)." JACC: Heart Failure, 2014.
  2. Banach M, et al. "Coenzyme Q10 and statin-induced myopathy: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials." Mayo Clinic Proceedings, 2015.
  3. Mantle D, Hargreaves I. "Coenzyme Q10 and Degenerative Disorders." Antioxidants, 2019.
Reality Check

The Resveratrol Disappointment: A Decade of Failed Promises

Mar 15, 2026 · 6 min read

Few supplements have generated as much scientific excitement — and subsequent scientific disappointment — as resveratrol. Found in red wine, grapes, and berries, resveratrol attracted enormous research investment after early studies suggested it could activate sirtuins (particularly SIRT1), extend lifespan in yeast and mice, and potentially replicate the effects of caloric restriction. Over a decade and hundreds of human trials later, it has delivered essentially none of what was promised.

The Promising Start

The story began with David Sinclair's 2003 Nature paper showing resveratrol activated Sir2p in yeast, extending lifespan by 70%. A 2006 Nature paper showed resveratrol extended lifespan in obese mice on high-fat diets. The biotech company Sirtris was founded (later acquired by GlaxoSmithKline for $720 million in 2008 to develop resveratrol analogs as drugs). Supplement sales soared globally.

Resveratrol: A Decade of Trial Data

Did the mouse result ever translate?

Mouse lifespan (2006)Sinclair, high-fat diet
+30%
Short RCT biomarkers≤6 mo, modest
Small
Long RCT outcomes≥1 yr, null
Null
Cancer preventionSIRT1 story, no signal
None
Lifespan extensionhumans, after 20 years
None
The 2006 Sinclair paper built a billion-dollar market. Not one human trial has replicated a longevity outcome since.

Where It Fell Apart

A 2009 PLoS Biology paper found resveratrol's SIRT1 activation in earlier studies was an artifact of the fluorescent protein assay used — resveratrol doesn't directly activate SIRT1 physiologically without that assay artifact. GSK's Sirtris program failed in drug development and was shut down in 2013. In human trials, resveratrol's bioavailability is poor — it is rapidly metabolized in the gut wall and liver. Multiple RCTs in type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer prevention found null or inconsistent results. A 2018 systematic review of 21 RCTs found resveratrol had no significant effect on blood glucose, insulin sensitivity, blood pressure, or body composition.

What We Learned

Resveratrol's story is a cautionary tale about the distance between animal model excitement and human efficacy. Yeast, worms, and mice are not small humans. The lesson — that mechanistic elegance in a petri dish or mouse cage is not clinical evidence — is one the supplement and biotech industries continue to relearn with each new molecule.

Sources

  1. Baur JA, Sinclair DA. "Therapeutic potential of resveratrol: the in vivo evidence." Nature Reviews Drug Discovery, 2006.
  2. Borra MT, et al. "Mechanism of Human SIRT1 Activation by Resveratrol." Journal of Biological Chemistry, 2005.
  3. Sahebkar A. "Effects of resveratrol supplementation on plasma lipids." Nutrition Reviews, 2013.
Reality Check

Activated Charcoal: Why Detox Claims Are Nonsense

Mar 12, 2026 · 5 min read

Activated charcoal does genuinely absorb toxins — emergency rooms use it for this purpose in acute poisoning cases within 1–2 hours of ingestion. It is this legitimate clinical application that wellness marketers extracted, amplified, and misapplied to sell black lemonade, charcoal toothpaste, charcoal face masks, and charcoal detox capsules as daily health products. The hospital context and the juice cleanse context have almost nothing in common.

How Activated Charcoal Actually Works

Activated charcoal is treated with oxygen to create a porous structure with ~1,500 m² surface area per gram. This surface adsorbs certain organic molecules, preventing their absorption in the GI tract. In acute poisoning — overdose of aspirin, acetaminophen, tricyclic antidepressants — this can meaningfully reduce systemic toxin levels if administered within 1–2 hours of ingestion.

Activated Charcoal: What It Binds

Clinical reality vs. wellness marketing

Acetaminophen (ER use)≤1 hr post-ingestion
Real
Many drug overdoseslegitimate ER indication
Real
Alcoholbound poorly
No
Heavy metalsessentially not
No
Generic 'toxins'none identified
—
YOUR medicationsbirth control, SSRIs, etc.
Yes (!)
Charcoal is life-saving in an ER. Taken at home with your prescriptions, it quietly binds them and reduces their effect.

The critical limitation: activated charcoal is entirely non-selective. It adsorbs whatever it encounters — including your oral medications, vitamins, and nutrients. As a daily supplement, activated charcoal would predictably reduce the efficacy of every medication taken around the same time. This is why emergency rooms monitor drug levels during charcoal treatment.

The "Detox" Application Makes No Sense

Most toxins encountered in daily life are absorbed in the upper GI tract within 30–60 minutes of consumption, long before charcoal in a supplement capsule reaches them. By the time toxins enter the bloodstream, liver, or tissues, charcoal in the gut cannot access them. Charcoal cannot cross the intestinal wall and does not enter the bloodstream. Charcoal toothpaste is abrasive and damages enamel with daily use; no clinical study supports its whitening claims.

When to Use It and When Not To

Activated charcoal is appropriate only under medical supervision for acute poisoning management. It is not appropriate as a daily supplement, and it actively interferes with medications. Anyone on prescription drugs should keep activated charcoal products well away from their supplement routine.

Sources

  1. Chyka PA, et al. "Position statement and practice guidelines on the use of multi-dose activated charcoal in the treatment of acute poisoning." Clinical Toxicology, 1999.
  2. American Academy of Clinical Toxicology. "Position Statement: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal." Journal of Toxicology: Clinical Toxicology, 2005.
  3. Brooks M. "The charcoal craze: a clinical toxicologist's view." Emergency Medicine Journal, 2019.
BREAKTHROUGH

Omega-3 and Depression: What 26 Meta-Analyses Found

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

For years, the omega-3 and depression literature was a mess of contradictory findings. Some meta-analyses found meaningful benefits; others found none. The problem was heterogeneity: different dosages, different omega-3 forms, different patient populations, and different outcome measures all pooled together. A landmark umbrella review — a meta-analysis of meta-analyses — has now synthesized 26 separate meta-analyses and produced the clearest picture yet.

EPA vs. DHA: The Critical Distinction

The most important finding is that EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) are not interchangeable for depression. EPA-dominant formulations, defined as containing at least 60% EPA relative to total omega-3 content, consistently outperform DHA-dominant or equal-ratio products in antidepressant trials. The umbrella review found EPA-dominant supplements produced a standardized mean difference of approximately -0.5 to -0.6 in depression scores — a clinically meaningful effect comparable to some second-line antidepressants.

DHA alone showed no significant antidepressant effect across the pooled literature. Mixed EPA+DHA products showed intermediate results, largely depending on the EPA fraction. This distinction matters enormously for anyone purchasing omega-3 supplements: a fish oil with 300mg EPA and 200mg DHA per capsule is meaningfully different from one with 200mg EPA and 300mg DHA.

Adjunctive Use Outperforms Monotherapy

Another consistent finding across the 26 meta-analyses: omega-3 works better as an add-on to antidepressants than as a standalone treatment. Studies testing EPA alongside SSRIs or SNRIs found larger effect sizes than studies testing EPA alone. This is consistent with mechanistic theories suggesting omega-3 modulates inflammation, serotonin signaling, and neuroplasticity — pathways that overlap with but are distinct from those targeted by conventional antidepressants.

The dosage range in positive trials clusters around 1–2g of EPA per day. Going above 3g EPA per day does not appear to produce additional benefit and increases the risk of blood-thinning effects. Duration matters too: most trials show effects emerging at 8–12 weeks, with maximal benefit around 16 weeks.

Who Benefits Most

Subgroup analyses consistently show the largest effect sizes in: patients with elevated inflammatory markers (high CRP or IL-6), people with concurrent cardiovascular risk factors, those with treatment-resistant depression, and people consuming very low omega-3 dietary intake (vegetarians, people eating minimal fish). In people eating two or more fatty fish servings per week with no inflammation markers, the incremental benefit of supplementation is smaller.

The omega-3 and depression story is one of the more mature supplement narratives in psychiatry. The question is no longer "does it work?" but "who benefits most and under what conditions?" — and for EPA-dominant formulas in people with depression and elevated inflammation, the answer is clear enough to recommend clinical consideration.

Sources

  1. Liao Y, et al. "Efficacy of omega-3 PUFAs in depression: A meta-analysis." Translational Psychiatry, 2019.
  2. Sublette ME, et al. "Meta-analysis of the effects of eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) in clinical trials in depression." Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, 2011.
  3. Mocking RJ, et al. "Meta-analysis and meta-regression of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid supplementation for major depressive disorder." Translational Psychiatry, 2016.
GUIDE

The Supplement Stack for Runners

Apr 11, 2026 · 9 min read

Endurance athletes face a genuinely different set of nutritional demands than the general population. High training volumes increase micronutrient losses through sweat, alter gut absorption, and create physiological stresses — particularly on iron stores, bone density, and the immune system — that can deplete nutrients faster than diet alone restores them. Here's what the evidence actually supports.

Iron: The Most Important Micronutrient for Runners

Iron deficiency — with or without anemia — is the single most common nutritional deficit in endurance runners, particularly women. Even non-anemic iron deficiency impairs VO2 max and running economy through reduced oxygen-carrying capacity. Female runners, vegetarian runners, and high-volume runners training over 60 miles per week should test ferritin annually. The target for endurance performance is ferritin above 50 ng/mL, not merely above the clinical "deficiency" cutoff of 12–20 ng/mL.

Supplementation with ferrous sulfate (325mg, 45–65mg elemental iron) every other day — rather than daily — has been shown in recent research to increase absorption by reducing hepcidin suppression. Vitamin C alongside iron supplements improves absorption; calcium and coffee taken simultaneously reduce it.

Vitamin D, Calcium, and Bone Stress

Stress fractures are the most feared overuse injury in distance running. Vitamin D deficiency — present in an estimated 50–70% of athletes in northern latitudes — doubles stress fracture risk. Target serum 25(OH)D above 40 ng/mL. Most runners need 2,000–4,000 IU D3 daily through winter months to maintain this range. Calcium supplementation via food rather than pills is preferred; calcium carbonate supplements in isolation show weaker evidence for bone outcomes than dietary calcium.

Beetroot Juice and Caffeine: Proven Performance Enhancers

Among ergogenic aids, two have the most robust evidence base for endurance athletes. Dietary nitrate from beetroot juice (approximately 400–600mg nitrate, or 500ml of concentrated beetroot juice) consumed 2–3 hours before effort improves running economy by 1–3% — a meaningful effect in competitive contexts. It works by increasing nitric oxide availability, improving muscle oxygen efficiency.

Caffeine at 3–6mg per kilogram of body weight taken 60 minutes before exercise consistently improves time-trial performance by 2–4% across a large body of RCT evidence. It is the most reliable legal ergogenic aid in sport. For a 70kg runner, this means approximately 200–420mg caffeine — equivalent to 2–4 espressos.

What to Skip

Branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) show no clear benefit over adequate dietary protein for endurance athletes. Glutamine supplements are popular but have not demonstrated immune or recovery benefits in well-nourished runners. HMB (beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate) has limited evidence in endurance contexts. For runners eating sufficient calories and protein, a basic iron and vitamin D check covers the most meaningful gaps.

Sources

  1. Peeling P, et al. "Evidence-based supplements for the enhancement of athletic performance." International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 2018.
  2. Jones AM. "Dietary nitrate supplementation and exercise performance." Sports Medicine, 2014.
  3. Burden RJ, et al. "Effect of intravenous iron on aerobic capacity and iron metabolism in elite athletes." Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 2015.
MYTH-BUSTING

Biotin for Hair Growth: Marketing vs Reality

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Biotin (vitamin B7) supplements are a $2 billion global market, sold almost entirely on the promise of thicker, faster-growing hair and stronger nails. Beauty influencers endorse it. Pharmacy shelves are dominated by it. There is just one problem: the clinical evidence supports hair and nail benefits from biotin only in people who are deficient in it — and true biotin deficiency is rare.

What Biotin Actually Does

Biotin is an essential cofactor for carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, amino acid metabolism, and gluconeogenesis. Hair follicles have high metabolic activity and require adequate biotin for keratin synthesis. When biotin is genuinely deficient — which can occur in people with rare genetic disorders, those eating large amounts of raw egg whites (which contain avidin, a biotin antagonist), people on long-term anticonvulsant therapy, or those with inflammatory bowel disease — hair loss, skin rash, and brittle nails are characteristic symptoms. Correcting deficiency resolves these symptoms.

The leap the supplement industry has made is assuming that if deficiency causes hair loss, then excess biotin will supercharge hair growth. This is not how vitamins work. Biotin is water-soluble, and any intake above the renal threshold is excreted in urine. Blood and urine biotin levels in the typical supplement consumer are already at saturation.

The Clinical Trial Evidence

There are no well-designed, placebo-controlled RCTs demonstrating biotin supplementation improves hair growth in biotin-sufficient individuals. The studies cited in marketing materials are almost universally case reports of deficient patients, small observational studies without control groups, or industry-funded research using composite supplements (biotin combined with other ingredients), making it impossible to attribute any benefit to biotin specifically.

A 2017 review in the journal Skin Appendage Disorders examined all published evidence and found that every study reporting benefit from biotin involved either confirmed deficiency or a pre-existing condition known to disrupt biotin metabolism.

The Diagnostic Interference Problem

High-dose biotin supplements (5,000–10,000 mcg, common in hair growth products) actively interfere with immunoassay-based laboratory tests, including thyroid function tests, troponin (heart attack marker), and hormone panels. The FDA has issued multiple warnings about biotin causing falsely normal troponin results in patients having heart attacks. Beyond being ineffective, high-dose biotin is a genuine medical hazard in people undergoing diagnostic testing.

Sources

  1. Patel DP, et al. "A review of the use of biotin for hair loss." Skin Appendage Disorders, 2017.
  2. FDA Safety Communication. "Biotin (vitamin B7): Safety communication — May interfere with lab tests." U.S. Food and Drug Administration, 2019.
  3. Zempleni J, et al. "Biotin and biotinidase deficiency." Expert Review of Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2008.
SAFETY ALERT

Weight Loss Supplements That Actually Kill People

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

The FDA's database of adverse event reports and enforcement actions related to weight loss supplements is far longer than most consumers realize. While the industry broadly operates in a regulatory gray zone, a subset of products contain compounds with documented fatality risks — some of which continue to circulate online long after regulatory actions.

DNP: The Most Dangerous Substance in Weight Loss

2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP) is an industrial chemical that uncouples mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, forcing the body to generate heat instead of ATP. It does cause rapid fat loss — and it kills. DNP has no therapeutic window. The difference between a "fat-burning" dose and a lethal dose is small and unpredictable across individuals. Symptoms of DNP toxicity include profuse sweating, hyperthermia, tachycardia, and death from hyperthermia or cardiac arrest. The FDA has issued multiple warnings; dozens of deaths have been documented in medical literature since 2010. DNP is still sold online, often marketed as a "research chemical" or mislabeled.

DMAA: Still in Products Despite Multiple Deaths

1,3-Dimethylamylamine (DMAA) is a synthetic stimulant that was added to pre-workout and weight loss products from the mid-2000s onward. It causes vasoconstriction and significantly elevates blood pressure. At least 5 confirmed deaths were associated with DMAA-containing products, including two U.S. military personnel. The FDA issued warning letters to 10 manufacturers in 2012 and has continued enforcement actions. Despite this, DMAA continues to appear in third-party tested products and online sales. USP, NSF, and Informed Sport certification programs reliably exclude it.

Sibutramine: A Prescription Drug in Your Supplement

Sibutramine was a prescription weight loss drug withdrawn from the US market in 2010 due to increased cardiovascular event risk. It continues to appear as an undisclosed ingredient in weight loss supplements, detected in FDA laboratory testing. A person taking sibutramine unknowingly — especially if also taking SSRIs, MAOIs, or other serotonergic medications — faces risk of serotonin syndrome, hypertensive crisis, and cardiac events. FDA's supplement contamination database lists hundreds of weight loss products testing positive for pharmaceutical adulterants.

How to Protect Yourself

Third-party certification (NSF Certified for Sport, Informed Sport, USP Verified) significantly reduces risk by testing for banned substances and adulterants. Avoiding products with proprietary blends, extreme thermogenic claims, or "pharmaceutical strength" marketing language eliminates the highest-risk tier. The FDA's MedWatch database and supplement warning list are publicly searchable.

Sources

  1. Grundlingh J, et al. "2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP): a weight loss agent with significant acute toxicity and risk of death." Journal of Medical Toxicology, 2011.
  2. Cohen PA. "Hazards of hindsight — monitoring the safety of nutritional supplements." New England Journal of Medicine, 2014.
  3. FDA. "Tainted Products Marketed as Dietary Supplements." U.S. Food and Drug Administration enforcement database.
OVERHYPED

Tongkat Ali and Testosterone: The Evidence Gap

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Tongkat Ali (Eurycoma longifolia), also marketed as longjack, has become one of the most aggressively marketed testosterone-boosting herbs of the 2020s. Social media advertising promises dramatic increases in free testosterone, libido, muscle mass, and energy. The actual clinical evidence tells a far more qualified story.

What the Trials Actually Show

Several small randomized controlled trials have found statistically significant increases in testosterone levels with tongkat ali supplementation. The key context: these benefits appear primarily in men with baseline testosterone in the below-normal or low-normal range — including men with late-onset hypogonadism, men under chronic stress (with elevated cortisol suppressing testosterone), and older men with age-related testosterone decline. A 2021 trial found significant testosterone increases in men starting with below-average levels, but minimal response in men with already-adequate testosterone.

The effect size in positive trials is modest: increases of 15–30% in free or total testosterone, compared to a baseline that is already low. For a man with total testosterone of 250 ng/dL (low-normal), a 25% increase brings him to approximately 310 ng/dL — still suboptimal. For a man with testosterone of 650 ng/dL (healthy range), a 25% increase would bring him to 812 ng/dL — but trials consistently show that people with adequate testosterone don't see this response.

The Quality and Standardization Problem

Tongkat ali's active constituents are eurycomanone and related quassinoids. The concentration of these compounds in commercial products varies enormously. Positive clinical trials typically use patented, standardized extracts (LJ100 or Physta) at specific dosages and concentrations. Most products on retail shelves use unstandardized root powder with unknown active content. Buying an unstandardized tongkat ali product and expecting results equivalent to clinical trials is like taking an unspecified plant extract hoping it behaves like a pharmaceutical.

Practical Assessment

Men with confirmed low-normal testosterone who have ruled out treatable causes (sleep deprivation, obesity, zinc deficiency, chronic stress) and who are not candidates for TRT might find modest benefit from a standardized extract. For men with normal testosterone seeking a meaningful enhancement, the evidence simply doesn't support the marketing claims. Test your baseline before spending on this supplement.

Sources

  1. Tambi MI, et al. "Standardised water-soluble extract of Eurycoma longifolia, Tongkat ali, as testosterone booster for managing men with late-onset hypogonadism." Andrologia, 2012.
  2. Leisegang K, et al. "Eurycoma longifolia (Jack) improves serum total testosterone in men." JBRA Assisted Reproduction, 2022.
  3. Rehman SU, et al. "The effects of Eurycoma longifolia Jack on testosterone levels and related outcomes." Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2016.
KIDS

Iron Supplements for Toddlers: When and How

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency in toddlers worldwide, affecting an estimated 40–50% of children under 5 in developing regions and 7–15% in high-income countries. Even in its non-anemic form, iron deficiency in toddlerhood has documented consequences for neurocognitive development — effects that may not be fully reversible even after iron status is corrected. Early identification and treatment matter.

Who Is at Risk

The period of highest vulnerability is 6–24 months. At birth, infants have iron stores sufficient for approximately 4–6 months, after which dietary iron becomes critical. Risk factors for iron deficiency in toddlers include: cow's milk as the dominant beverage (cow's milk is low in iron and inhibits iron absorption), low consumption of iron-rich foods, premature birth or low birth weight, and exclusive breastfeeding beyond 6 months without iron-rich complementary foods. Toddlers drinking more than 500ml (about 16oz) of cow's milk daily have significantly elevated deficiency risk regardless of other dietary factors.

Testing: What to Measure and When

The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends universal hemoglobin screening at 12 months with risk-factor-based assessment at each well-child visit. Hemoglobin alone misses non-anemic iron deficiency; ferritin is the most sensitive early marker but requires separate blood draw. A serum ferritin below 12 ng/mL confirms deficiency; below 20 ng/mL in a child with risk factors warrants a clinical discussion about supplementation.

How to Supplement Safely

Pediatric iron supplementation is typically dosed at 3–6mg of elemental iron per kilogram of body weight per day for deficiency treatment. Ferrous sulfate liquid drops are the standard formulation for toddlers. Side effects — constipation, dark stools, and stomach upset — are common but manageable. Giving iron between meals on an empty stomach maximizes absorption but may worsen GI side effects; with food reduces side effects but lowers absorption by approximately 40%. Vitamin C-containing juice given alongside iron supplements meaningfully improves absorption.

Treatment typically runs for 3 months after ferritin normalization. Follow-up ferritin testing 4 weeks after starting supplementation confirms adequate response. Prevention through iron-fortified cereals, pureed meats, and legumes from 6 months onward remains the preferred approach for at-risk children.

Sources

  1. Baker RD & Greer FR; AAP Committee on Nutrition. "Diagnosis and prevention of iron deficiency and iron-deficiency anemia in infants and young children." Pediatrics, 2010.
  2. Lozoff B. "Iron deficiency and child development." Food and Nutrition Bulletin, 2007.
  3. Domellöf M, et al. "Iron requirements of infants and toddlers." Journal of Pediatric Gastroenterology and Nutrition, 2014.
SAFETY ALERT

Ashwagandha and Thyroid: A Hidden Risk

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is one of the most popular adaptogens on the market, marketed for stress, cortisol, energy, and general wellbeing. It has genuine clinical support for some of these claims. But there is a safety consideration that almost no supplement marketing material discloses: ashwagandha stimulates thyroid hormone production and can precipitate hyperthyroidism or thyrotoxicosis in vulnerable individuals.

The Mechanism

Several studies — including animal models and human clinical trials — have documented that ashwagandha root extract increases serum T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) levels. The proposed mechanisms include stimulation of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) secretion and direct effects on thyroid tissue. A 2018 study in the Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine found statistically significant increases in both T3 and T4 with ashwagandha supplementation in a randomized controlled trial, with concurrent reductions in TSH — a pattern consistent with thyroid stimulation.

Who Is at Risk

People with pre-existing hyperthyroidism or Graves' disease face the most serious risk: ashwagandha can substantially worsen already-elevated thyroid hormone levels. Case reports document thyrotoxicosis precipitated by ashwagandha supplementation in people who had no prior thyroid diagnosis — suggesting subclinical hyperthyroidism that was accelerated by supplementation. People on levothyroxine (synthetic T4) for hypothyroidism face a different risk: ashwagandha-induced thyroid stimulation may interact unpredictably with medication dosing, potentially causing overmedication symptoms.

Practical Guidance

Anyone with a thyroid condition — diagnosed hyperthyroidism, hypothyroidism on medication, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, or a family history of thyroid disease — should discuss ashwagandha use with their physician before starting. Symptoms warranting thyroid evaluation in someone taking ashwagandha include unexpected weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, anxiety, or tremor. For healthy individuals with no thyroid history, short-term ashwagandha use at studied doses (300–600mg standardized root extract) appears safe, but the thyroid interaction warrants awareness.

Sources

  1. Sharma AK, et al. "Efficacy and safety of ashwagandha root extract in subclinical hypothyroid patients." Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine, 2018.
  2. van der Hooft CS, et al. "Thyroid disease caused by herbal remedies: case reports." Netherlands Journal of Medicine, 2005.
  3. Panda S & Kar A. "Withania somnifera and Bauhinia purpurea in the regulation of circulating thyroid hormone concentrations in female mice." Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 1999.
BREAKTHROUGH

Creatine for Older Adults: Muscle, Brain, and Bone

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

Creatine monohydrate has been studied for athletic performance for decades. But the emerging evidence for its role in healthy aging — across muscle, brain, and bone — has quietly become one of the more compelling stories in geriatric nutrition science. For adults over 60, the argument for creatine supplementation is now substantially stronger than most people realize.

Sarcopenia: Creatine's Primary Target in Aging

Adults lose approximately 3–8% of muscle mass per decade after age 30, accelerating to 15% per decade after 60 — a process called sarcopenia that drives frailty, fall risk, and loss of independence. Creatine supplementation combined with resistance training consistently outperforms resistance training alone in older adults for lean mass accretion and strength gains. A meta-analysis of 22 RCTs found creatine supplementation in older adults produced significantly greater gains in muscle mass (an additional ~1.3 kg) and leg press strength compared to placebo when combined with exercise. Critically, even without formal resistance training, older adults show modest lean mass and functional benefits from creatine, though the combination is far more powerful.

Brain Health: The Emerging Evidence

The brain is one of the body's highest consumers of phosphocreatine, using it to rapidly regenerate ATP during cognitive demands. Older adults have lower brain creatine content than younger adults. Several RCTs have found creatine supplementation improves performance on memory tasks — particularly working memory and executive function — in older populations. A 2022 meta-analysis found consistent positive effects of creatine on memory across the pooled older adult literature. Whether these effects translate to reduced dementia risk over long timescales is not yet established, but the mechanistic logic and short-term cognitive data are encouraging.

Bone Density: The Least-Known Benefit

Creatine supplementation alongside resistance training has been shown in multiple trials to improve bone mineral density in postmenopausal women and older men — populations at significant osteoporosis risk. The mechanism appears to involve creatine's role in supporting osteoblast (bone-forming cell) energy metabolism. Trials using 3–5g/day for 12 months show significant improvements in hip and femoral neck bone density versus resistance training alone.

The dosage is well-established: 3–5g of creatine monohydrate daily, no loading phase required. Creatine monohydrate is the only form with this evidence base; expensive alternative forms (creatine HCL, buffered creatine) offer no demonstrated advantage.

Sources

  1. Devries MC & Phillips SM. "Creatine supplementation during resistance training in older adults — a meta-analysis." Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 2014.
  2. Candow DG, et al. "Effect of creatine supplementation during resistance training on bone mineral density in older females." Journal of Nutritional Health and Aging, 2019.
  3. Avgerinos KI, et al. "Effects of creatine supplementation on cognitive function in healthy individuals." Experimental Gerontology, 2018.
OVERHYPED

The Ketone Supplement Scam

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Exogenous ketone supplements — ketone salts and ketone esters — have been marketed as a way to get all the benefits of a ketogenic diet without actually restricting carbohydrates. The pitch is compelling: raise blood ketone levels, burn fat, improve cognition, enhance athletic performance. The scientific evidence for most of these claims is substantially weaker than the marketing suggests.

What They Actually Do

Exogenous ketones (primarily beta-hydroxybutyrate, or BHB) do reliably raise blood ketone levels for 1–3 hours after consumption. This is not in dispute. The question is what elevated ketone levels for a few hours actually accomplish when carbohydrate intake and metabolic state are otherwise unchanged. In a person eating a normal carbohydrate-containing diet, circulating ketones represent a temporarily available fuel substrate — but insulin remains elevated enough to suppress fat mobilization, and glucose metabolism continues normally. You have raised a number without changing the underlying metabolic environment that drives ketogenic benefits.

Performance: A Mixed and Largely Negative Picture

Elite cycling studies funded by ketone ester developers initially suggested performance benefits. Subsequent independent replication attempts have largely failed to find performance gains — and several have found impairment, particularly at high intensities where glucose metabolism is rate-limiting. A 2020 meta-analysis of ketone supplementation and exercise performance found no significant benefit across pooled trials and noted that study quality inversely predicted positive outcomes (lower-quality studies showed benefits; better-controlled trials did not).

Cognitive Claims: Mostly Theoretical

The brain does use ketones as an alternative fuel to glucose, and this has generated enormous theoretical interest in ketones for neurological conditions including Alzheimer's disease. But translating this into "ketone drinks will sharpen your focus" requires several unproven steps. The few RCTs testing cognitive effects of exogenous ketones in healthy individuals have found mixed results, primarily in populations with mild cognitive impairment rather than healthy young adults.

The Price Problem

Ketone ester products cost $25–$40 per serving. For this price, you get a temporary ketone elevation with no demonstrated meaningful outcomes in healthy people. The ketogenic diet itself — which costs nothing beyond dietary changes — produces sustained ketosis and has documented therapeutic value in epilepsy and emerging research in other conditions. The supplement version is a very expensive way to briefly imitate a dietary state without the state itself.

Sources

  1. Evans M, et al. "Exogenous ketone supplementation and cognition." Nutrients, 2022.
  2. Prins PJ, et al. "Effects of a ketone ester drink on exercise performance." Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition, 2020.
  3. Dearlove DJ, et al. "Nutritional ketoacidosis during incremental exercise in healthy athletes." Frontiers in Physiology, 2019.
KIDS

Zinc for Children: Immune Support and Growth

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Zinc is a trace mineral essential for immune function, cell division, protein synthesis, and growth hormone signaling. In children, adequate zinc status is directly linked to normal physical growth, resistance to infections, and cognitive development. Deficiency — which globally affects an estimated 17–20% of the world's children and is more common than generally recognized even in wealthy countries — causes stunted growth and suppressed immunity.

The Growth and Immune Connection

Zinc is required for the activity of over 300 enzymes and is a structural component of more than 1,000 transcription factors. In childhood, its most visible effects are on linear growth (height) and immune competence. Studies in zinc-deficient populations show that supplementation produces catch-up growth and significantly reduces the incidence and duration of respiratory infections and diarrheal illness. A Cochrane review of zinc supplementation in children found significant reductions in the incidence of pneumonia, diarrhea, and overall duration of common cold symptoms in deficient populations.

Who Is at Risk in Developed Countries

Overt zinc deficiency is rare in well-nourished Western children, but subclinical deficiency occurs in picky eaters, vegetarians and vegans (phytates in plant foods inhibit zinc absorption), children with inflammatory bowel disease, and those with chronic diarrheal conditions that increase zinc losses. Meat and shellfish are the highest-bioavailability zinc sources; plant-based zinc from legumes and grains is poorly absorbed without fermentation or soaking to reduce phytate content.

Supplementation Guidance

Supplementing zinc without confirmed deficiency in replete children produces no benefit and risks copper depletion (zinc and copper compete for absorption). Zinc and copper are metabolic antagonists; high-dose zinc supplementation over extended periods without copper supplementation can cause copper deficiency anemia. In children with documented deficiency or high-risk dietary patterns, zinc supplementation at 5–10mg elemental zinc daily (age-dependent) is appropriate under medical guidance. Serum zinc levels are an imperfect but practical marker; simultaneous assessment of dietary intake and clinical symptoms is more informative.

Sources

  1. Lassi ZS, et al. "Zinc supplementation for the prevention of pneumonia in children aged 2 months to 59 months." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2016.
  2. Brown KH, et al. "International Zinc Nutrition Consultative Group: Assessment of the risk of zinc deficiency." Food and Nutrition Bulletin, 2004.
  3. Hambidge KM & Krebs NF. "Zinc deficiency: a special challenge." Journal of Nutrition, 2007.
GUIDE

Curcumin Absorption: Why 95% of Turmeric Supplements Fail

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Curcumin — the active polyphenol in turmeric — has a strong research profile for anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects in vitro and in animal models. The problem is that curcumin is notoriously difficult to absorb. Standard curcumin extract has near-zero oral bioavailability: most of an ingested dose passes through the GI tract unabsorbed and is excreted unchanged. This is why many human trials of standard curcumin have found disappointing results, while trials using enhanced formulations have shown clinical promise.

Why Standard Curcumin Fails

Three properties make curcumin biologically challenging: poor water solubility, rapid metabolism in the intestinal wall and liver, and fast elimination. When researchers measure blood curcumin levels after a standard 1g dose of typical "95% curcuminoids" extract, they detect only trace amounts for a brief window. This is not a dosage problem — higher doses of standard curcumin don't meaningfully change the absorption picture. The molecule needs to be physically reformulated to survive absorption.

Formulations That Actually Work

Several technologies significantly improve curcumin bioavailability and have clinical trial data to support their effectiveness. Piperine (black pepper extract at 20mg) increases curcumin bioavailability by approximately 2000% by inhibiting CYP3A4 metabolism in the liver and intestinal glucuronidation — the piperine-curcumin combination (Bioperine system) is the most accessible and least expensive enhanced option. Phytosome formulations (curcumin bound to phosphatidylcholine, as in Meriva) show 29-fold greater absorption than standard extract and have clinical trial data in osteoarthritis and inflammatory conditions. Nanoparticle and lipid nanoparticle dispersions achieve very high bioavailability but are more expensive and less widely available. SLCP (solid lipid curcumin particle) formulations also show substantial improvements.

Clinical Implications

Studies showing meaningful effects on joint pain, inflammatory markers, and DOMS (delayed-onset muscle soreness) have almost universally used one of these enhanced formulations — not standard turmeric powder or generic "95% curcuminoids." The takeaway is simple but frequently ignored by consumers: the form of curcumin matters far more than the label's stated curcumin percentage. A product listing "500mg curcumin" without specifying the delivery form is almost certainly providing negligible absorbed curcumin.

Sources

  1. Anand P, et al. "Bioavailability of curcumin: problems and promises." Molecular Pharmaceutics, 2007.
  2. Shoba G, et al. "Influence of piperine on the pharmacokinetics of curcumin in animals and human volunteers." Planta Medica, 1998.
  3. Belcaro G, et al. "Efficacy and safety of Meriva, a curcumin-phosphatidylcholine complex, during extended administration in osteoarthritis patients." Alternative Medicine Review, 2010.
MYTH-BUSTING

Garcinia Cambogia: The Weight Loss Fraud

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Garcinia cambogia became the most popular weight loss supplement in America virtually overnight after a high-profile television physician endorsed it in 2012 as a "revolutionary fat buster." At its peak it generated over $150 million in annual sales. A decade of clinical trials later, the evidence is clear: it does not cause meaningful weight loss in humans.

The Mechanism That Sounded Plausible

Hydroxycitric acid (HCA), the active compound in Garcinia cambogia's rind, inhibits an enzyme called ATP-citrate lyase, which plays a role in converting carbohydrates to fat. In animal models, HCA reduces food intake and body fat. This created a scientifically plausible-sounding pathway that drove enormous consumer interest. The problem is that the mechanism doesn't translate reliably to humans at supplemental doses.

What Clinical Trials Actually Found

The most rigorous trial — a 12-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study of 135 overweight subjects published in JAMA — found zero significant difference in weight loss between Garcinia cambogia and placebo (approximately 4.1 kg lost in both groups, attributable to the reduced-calorie diet both groups followed). Subsequent meta-analyses incorporating all available RCTs have found a pooled difference of approximately 0.8–1.3 kg more weight loss with Garcinia versus placebo — a difference so small as to be clinically meaningless and potentially explained by publication bias favoring positive results.

The Quality Problem

The FDA has also issued multiple warnings about Garcinia cambogia products adulterated with sibutramine — a withdrawn prescription drug — and other undisclosed pharmaceuticals. In competitive product testing, independent labs have found many products contain far less HCA than labeled or are adulterated. Even if HCA worked as advertised, there is no guarantee a given product contains what it claims.

The Garcinia cambogia story is a useful template for understanding supplement marketing: a plausible mechanism in animal models, a celebrity endorsement, hundreds of millions in sales, and rigorous clinical trials that fail to find the promised effect. The supplement industry moved on to the next mechanism-sounds-good ingredient; the clinical evidence never moved with it.

Sources

  1. Heymsfield SB, et al. "Garcinia cambogia (hydroxycitric acid) as a potential antiobesity agent: a randomized controlled trial." JAMA, 1998.
  2. Onakpoya I, et al. "The use of Garcinia extract (hydroxycitric acid) as a weight loss supplement: a systematic review and meta-analysis." Journal of Obesity, 2011.
  3. FDA Consumer Advisory. "Consumers beware: weight loss supplements containing Garcinia cambogia adulterated with sibutramine." U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
GUIDE

Electrolytes for Athletes: Science vs Marketing

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

Electrolyte supplements — drinks, tablets, powders, and drops — are a major market segment positioned to athletes as essential for performance and recovery. The actual exercise physiology literature is more nuanced: electrolyte replacement is critical in some situations and completely unnecessary in others. The marketing rarely makes this distinction.

When Electrolyte Replacement Is Genuinely Necessary

Exercise lasting less than one hour in temperate conditions with moderate sweat rates does not meaningfully deplete electrolytes to a degree that impairs performance. Water is sufficient. Electrolyte supplementation becomes genuinely important in: endurance exercise exceeding 60–90 minutes, exercise in hot and humid conditions with high sweat rates, multi-day events or training blocks with limited recovery time, and athletes prone to muscle cramping with documented sodium losses.

Sodium is the electrolyte lost in highest quantity through sweat (typically 500–1500mg per liter of sweat, varying substantially between individuals). Sodium depletion — not plain dehydration — is the primary driver of exercise-associated hyponatremia (dangerously low blood sodium), which paradoxically most often occurs in slower, longer-duration events where athletes over-hydrate with plain water. For events over 2–3 hours, sodium replacement of 500–700mg per hour of exercise is evidence-based.

Potassium and Magnesium: Important but Overmarketed

Sweat contains potassium (approximately 150–200mg/L) and magnesium (approximately 5–10mg/L) — much smaller amounts than sodium. In typical training scenarios, dietary intake easily covers losses. Potassium is widely distributed in whole foods (bananas, potatoes, avocado); deficiency from exercise alone is uncommon in athletes eating adequate calories. Magnesium deficiency is more relevant as a baseline nutritional concern in athletes (magnesium requirements are elevated by exercise) but is better addressed through diet or standalone magnesium supplementation rather than electrolyte drinks with small, variable magnesium content.

The Sports Drink Problem

Most commercial sports drinks contain 50–110mg of sodium per 240ml serving — far below the 500–700mg per hour needed in conditions where electrolyte replacement is warranted. Their primary substrate is sugar (glucose and sucrose), which is genuinely useful for endurance exercise but irrelevant for casual exercisers. The marketing of commercial sports drinks to recreational exercisers doing 30–45 minute gym sessions is nutritionally unjustified. For serious endurance athletes, purpose-formulated electrolyte products (like Precision Hydration or SiS GO Electrolyte) with higher, more targeted sodium content are better calibrated to actual needs.

Sources

  1. Shirreffs SM & Sawka MN. "Fluid and electrolyte needs for training, competition, and recovery." Journal of Sports Sciences, 2011.
  2. Hew-Butler T, et al. "Statement of the Third International Exercise-Associated Hyponatremia Consensus Development Conference." Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, 2015.
  3. Burke LM, et al. "Carbohydrates and fat for training and recovery." Journal of Sports Sciences, 2004.
OVERHYPED

CBD for Anxiety: The Gap Between Claims and Evidence

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

CBD (cannabidiol) products dominate wellness retail worldwide, with anxiety relief the most prominent claim. The market exceeds $5 billion in the US alone. Consumer demand is driven by a combination of personal testimonials, plausible neuroscience, and aggressive marketing. The clinical trial evidence for CBD in anxiety is thinner, less consistent, and more complicated than the industry presents.

What the Evidence Actually Shows

The strongest evidence for CBD in anxiety-related conditions comes from: (1) pharmaceutical-grade CBD (Epidiolex) in rare childhood epilepsy syndromes — where it is genuinely effective and FDA-approved; (2) high-dose intravenous CBD in social anxiety disorder — where a handful of well-designed trials show acute anxiolytic effects at doses of 300–600mg; and (3) animal models showing consistent anxiolytic effects via endocannabinoid modulation. The problem is the gap between these findings and what retail CBD products offer.

Most CBD supplements contain 10–50mg of CBD per dose. The positive human anxiety trials used 300–600mg. Whether lower doses produce proportional effects is not established — CBD's dose-response curve is described as inverted-U shaped, meaning too little and too much both show reduced effect relative to an intermediate dose, and this optimal dose appears to be much higher than typical supplement amounts.

The Bioavailability Problem

Oral CBD bioavailability is approximately 6–20%, highly variable between individuals and with food. This means a 25mg CBD gummy delivers somewhere between 1.5mg and 5mg to systemic circulation — a fraction of the doses used in clinical trials. Sublingual tinctures offer somewhat better absorption; vaporized CBD reaches systemic circulation most efficiently but carries its own risks. The effective dose window for anxiety in humans, if it exists for oral CBD, has not been established in adequately powered trials.

Regulatory and Quality Issues

CBD is in a regulatory gray zone in most markets. Independent laboratory testing of commercial CBD products routinely finds products containing 20–50% less CBD than labeled, along with trace THC levels that may cause positive drug test results. There is no FDA pre-market approval requirement for CBD content claims. For people spending $60–$120 per month on CBD for anxiety, the evidence that they are receiving a meaningful dose of an effective compound is genuinely weak.

Sources

  1. Blessing EM, et al. "Cannabidiol as a potential treatment for anxiety disorders." Neurotherapeutics, 2015.
  2. Kayser RR, et al. "Cannabidiol augmentation of exposure-based extinction." Neuropsychopharmacology, 2020.
  3. Bonn-Miller MO, et al. "Labeling accuracy of cannabidiol extracts sold online." JAMA, 2017.
BREAKTHROUGH

Vitamin K2 for Heart Health: Observational vs Trial Data

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

Vitamin K2 — distinct from K1 (phylloquinone) found in leafy greens — has attracted significant research interest for its role in cardiovascular health. The observational evidence is compelling; the randomized trial data is emerging but incomplete. Understanding the difference between these two evidence types is essential for a rational position on K2 supplementation.

The Mechanistic Story

K2 is a cofactor for carboxylation of matrix Gla protein (MGP), the most potent known inhibitor of vascular calcification. Uncarboxylated (inactive) MGP allows calcium to deposit in arterial walls, driving atherosclerotic plaque mineralization — a process strongly associated with cardiovascular events independent of cholesterol levels. K2 activates MGP and two other proteins (osteocalcin, Gas6) involved in redirecting calcium to bone rather than arteries. The biological logic is: K2 keeps calcium out of arteries and into bones simultaneously.

Observational Evidence: Strong but Confounded

The Rotterdam Study — a landmark Dutch prospective cohort of over 4,800 adults — found that highest dietary K2 intake was associated with 57% reduced cardiovascular mortality over a 10-year follow-up, even after adjusting for multiple confounders. The Prospect-EPIC cohort similarly found dietary K2 associated with significant reductions in coronary heart disease. These associations are among the stronger diet-outcome relationships in cardiovascular epidemiology. However, K2 is found primarily in fermented foods (natto, aged cheeses) and organ meats — dietary patterns themselves associated with other healthy behaviors, making confounding possible.

Randomized Trial Evidence: Promising but Incomplete

Several RCTs have tested K2 supplementation on surrogate cardiovascular outcomes. The MK-7 (menaquinone-7) form of K2, supplemented at 180–360mcg daily, consistently reduces circulating uncarboxylated MGP levels — confirming biological activity. A 2015 RCT of 244 postmenopausal women found 3 years of MK-7 at 180mcg daily significantly reduced arterial stiffness progression. A smaller Dutch trial found reductions in coronary artery calcification progression. These are promising mechanistic signals, but hard cardiovascular event outcomes — heart attacks, strokes, deaths — have not yet been demonstrated in adequately powered long-term trials.

The verdict: K2 has a mechanistically plausible and observationally supported case for cardiovascular benefit. The trial evidence is not yet definitive for hard outcomes. For people concerned about vascular calcification risk or with calcium supplement use (which may increase calcification risk), K2 at 100–200mcg MK-7 daily represents a low-risk, potentially high-upside addition pending further trial data.

Sources

  1. Geleijnse JM, et al. "Dietary intake of menaquinone is associated with a reduced risk of coronary heart disease: the Rotterdam Study." Journal of Nutrition, 2004.
  2. Knapen MH, et al. "Menaquinone-7 supplementation improves arterial stiffness in healthy postmenopausal women." Thrombosis and Haemostasis, 2015.
  3. Schurgers LJ, et al. "Regression of warfarin-induced medial elastocalcinosis by high intake of vitamin K." Blood, 2007.
SAFETY ALERT

The Dangers of Buying Supplements on Amazon

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Amazon is the largest single point of supplement purchase in the United States, with market research estimates suggesting 30–40% of all supplements sold in the country pass through its platform. It is also, by the assessment of independent researchers, one of the riskiest places to buy supplements — not because of Amazon's own products, but because of the third-party marketplace model that dominates supplement sales on the platform.

The Counterfeit and Authenticity Problem

Amazon's commingled inventory model (Fulfilled by Amazon, or FBA) means that products from different sellers — including authorized and unauthorized distributors — are physically mixed together in warehouses. A buyer ordering a name-brand supplement may receive inventory from a different seller's stock, including counterfeit units, expired inventory, or products stored outside recommended conditions. Multiple investigative reports and lawsuits have documented counterfeit supplements — often from overseas manufacturers with no quality controls — entering the FBA supply chain and being shipped to customers who believed they were buying authentic products.

Third-Party Seller Fraud

Independent research has repeatedly found supplements on Amazon containing wrong ingredients, incorrect doses, or active pharmaceutical drugs not listed on the label — from third-party sellers operating with minimal accountability. A 2020 report found that a significant proportion of sports supplements purchased from Amazon third-party sellers contained quantities of active ingredients substantially different from label claims, and some contained undisclosed stimulants.

How to Reduce Risk

For supplements where product integrity is critical, purchasing directly from the manufacturer's website eliminates third-party intermediary risks. Buying from authorized distributors with clear supply chain accountability is the next best option. On Amazon specifically: buying only "Sold by [Brand Name]" (not a third-party seller) and checking that Amazon is not commingling seller inventory provides better (though not perfect) protection. Looking for third-party certification marks — NSF, USP, Informed Sport — on the product label (not just the Amazon listing, which can be manipulated) provides the highest confidence in product integrity.

Sources

  1. Starr RR. "Too little, too late: ineffective regulation of dietary supplements in the United States." American Journal of Public Health, 2015.
  2. Cohen PA, et al. "Presence of banned drugs in dietary supplements following FDA recalls." JAMA, 2014.
  3. Branna J. "The Amazon effect on supplement retail." Nutritional Outlook, 2021.
KIDS

Multivitamins for Teens: Necessary or Wasteful?

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Multivitamin sales to teenagers and their parents represent a substantial market segment, driven by parental concern about nutritional adequacy. The nutritional science answer is considerably more nuanced than the marketing: for most teenagers eating a varied omnivorous diet, a daily multivitamin adds little of value and may create false assurance. But specific subgroups face genuine nutrient gaps where targeted supplementation makes sense.

Why Most Teens Don't Need a Multivitamin

Adolescence is a period of dramatically increased appetite and food intake, driven by growth spurts and hormonal change. A teen consuming adequate calories from a reasonably varied diet is simultaneously consuming large amounts of naturally occurring micronutrients. National dietary surveys consistently show that the majority of adolescents in developed countries meet or exceed RDAs for most vitamins and minerals from food alone. Generic multivitamin products provide standardized doses across all nutrients regardless of individual need; most of those doses are either already met by diet or are excreted without absorption.

Who Actually Needs to Supplement

Adolescent girls represent the highest-risk group for specific nutrient inadequacies. Iron deficiency is common after menarche due to menstrual blood losses; the average adolescent girl loses 30–45ml of blood per cycle. Ferritin testing is more informative than a generic multivitamin. Calcium and vitamin D are critical during peak bone mass accretion (ages 11–17); teens consuming less than 3 dairy or fortified plant-based servings daily, or living in low-sunlight climates, should specifically address these gaps. Folate is important for adolescent girls of reproductive age given the risk of unplanned pregnancy.

Teenage vegans and vegetarians face elevated risks of vitamin B12 deficiency (vegans) and potentially suboptimal iron and zinc (due to phytate inhibition). B12 supplementation is essential for vegan teens; it is the one supplement where deficiency risk is predictable and certain without supplementation. Teen athletes training intensively have elevated needs for iron, vitamin D, and overall caloric intake — targeted assessment is more appropriate than a generic multivitamin.

Sources

  1. Fulgoni VL, et al. "Foods, fortificants, and supplements: where do Americans get their nutrients?" Journal of Nutrition, 2011.
  2. Bailey RL, et al. "Why US adults use dietary supplements." JAMA Internal Medicine, 2013.
  3. Heaney RP & Weaver CM. "Calcium and vitamin D." Endocrinology and Metabolism Clinics of North America, 2003.
GUIDE

L-Theanine and Caffeine: The Evidence-Based Focus Stack

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

The combination of L-theanine and caffeine is the most studied cognitive supplement stack in existence, with over 20 randomized controlled trials and a consistent body of evidence supporting effects on attention, working memory, and subjective focus — while reducing the jitteriness and cardiovascular side effects associated with caffeine alone. It is one of the rare nootropic combinations where the mechanistic rationale, the pharmacokinetic data, and the clinical outcomes align clearly.

How Each Component Works

Caffeine is an adenosine receptor antagonist. By blocking adenosine (a sleep-promoting neurotransmitter that accumulates with wakefulness), caffeine reduces fatigue and increases arousal, alertness, and reaction time. At higher doses, it also increases anxiety, heart rate, and the likelihood of performance-degrading jitteriness. L-theanine is an amino acid found almost exclusively in green tea, where it naturally co-occurs with caffeine. It promotes alpha-wave brain activity — associated with a state of relaxed alertness — and modulates caffeine's excitatory effects on GABA and glutamate signaling without sedating the user.

What the RCTs Show

The most replicated finding across the literature: the combination of L-theanine (100–200mg) and caffeine (50–150mg) consistently outperforms either compound alone on tasks requiring sustained attention, rapid visual information processing, and working memory. The combination also consistently produces lower self-reported anxiety and fewer jitteriness ratings than caffeine alone, despite comparable alertness improvements. A 2008 study by Haskell et al. found the combination significantly improved accuracy on a demanding attentional task compared to caffeine alone, placebo, and L-theanine alone. These findings have been replicated multiple times.

The Ideal Ratio and Dosing

The most commonly studied and effective ratio is 2:1 L-theanine to caffeine by weight. Typical effective doses are 100mg caffeine with 200mg L-theanine, or 75mg caffeine with 150mg L-theanine. The 2:1 ratio appears to maximize the complementary effect. Effects onset within 30–45 minutes and peak at 60–90 minutes, broadly matching caffeine's pharmacokinetics. For people sensitive to caffeine, this ratio allows effective cognitive supplementation at lower caffeine doses with reduced side effects. Green tea naturally provides this combination — one cup contains approximately 30–50mg caffeine and 20–30mg theanine — though at lower absolute doses than most supplement formulations.

Sources

  1. Haskell CF, et al. "The effects of L-theanine, caffeine and their combination on cognition and mood." Biological Psychology, 2008.
  2. Owen GN, et al. "The combined effects of L-theanine and caffeine on cognitive performance and mood." Nutritional Neuroscience, 2008.
  3. Dodd FL, et al. "A double-blind, placebo-controlled study evaluating the effects of caffeine and L-theanine both alone and in combination on cerebral blood flow, cognition and mood." Psychopharmacology, 2015.
MYTH-BUSTING

Saw Palmetto for Prostate: Three Cochrane Reviews Say No

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) extract is one of the most widely used herbal supplements for urinary symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — the non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate common in men over 50. Global sales exceed $300 million annually. Three independent Cochrane systematic reviews, spanning two decades of cumulative trial evidence, have consistently found it no more effective than placebo. This is a story about how initial encouraging data, a plausible mechanism, and strong commercial interests can sustain a market that the science has consistently undermined.

The Initial Promise

Early small trials from the 1980s and 1990s found saw palmetto produced statistically significant improvements in urinary flow and symptom scores in men with BPH. The proposed mechanism — inhibition of 5-alpha-reductase, an enzyme that converts testosterone to DHT, the hormone that drives prostate growth — was biologically plausible (pharmaceutical 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors like finasteride are effective for BPH). These early positive signals drove adoption of saw palmetto as a mainstream prostate supplement.

What the Cochrane Reviews Found

The first Cochrane review (2002) found a modest positive signal but noted serious methodological limitations in available trials. The 2009 Cochrane review updated the analysis with better-quality data and found no significant benefit over placebo on primary outcomes. The most definitive evidence came from the STEP trial (Saw Palmetto for Treatment of Enlarged Prostates) — a large, rigorous, double-blind NCCAM-funded trial of 225 men — which found saw palmetto at standard, double, and triple doses produced no improvement in urinary symptoms compared to placebo after 72 weeks. The 2012 Cochrane review, incorporating this and other high-quality trials, confirmed: no benefit on peak urinary flow rate, no benefit on symptom scores, no benefit at any dose tested.

Why Men Still Buy It

BPH symptoms fluctuate naturally; many men notice subjective improvement on saw palmetto simply because symptom variation means they feel better sometimes regardless of treatment. The supplement industry continues to market the 1990s mechanism story without disclosing the subsequent Cochrane conclusions. For men with symptomatic BPH, evidence-based options include alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin), 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride), and lifestyle modifications — all substantially more effective than saw palmetto.

Sources

  1. Tacklind J, et al. "Serenoa repens for benign prostatic hyperplasia." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2012.
  2. Barry MJ, et al. "Effect of increasing doses of saw palmetto extract on lower urinary tract symptoms: a randomized trial." JAMA, 2011.
  3. Wilt T, et al. "Saw palmetto extracts for treatment of benign prostatic hyperplasia: a systematic review." JAMA, 1998.
GUIDE

Fasting and Supplements: What to Take and When to Stop

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Intermittent fasting — most commonly 16:8 (16 hours fasted, 8-hour eating window) — has become mainstream. Millions of people now regularly fast through the morning and are left with a practical question their fasting guides rarely answer: which supplements should be taken, which should be held until the eating window, and which actually break a fast in a meaningful way?

What "Breaking a Fast" Actually Means

The popular concept of breaking a fast is less biologically precise than its internet usage implies. A strict definition focuses on insulin response: anything that causes a significant insulin rise breaks a fasted metabolic state. A looser definition used by many practitioners is anything containing calories. A practical definition for most people is: any supplement that causes enough caloric intake or GI stimulation to defeat the main purpose of their fast (whether that purpose is metabolic, digestive rest, or ketosis).

Supplements That Are Fine During a Fast

The following have no meaningful effect on insulin or fasting metabolism: water, electrolytes without calories (sodium, potassium, magnesium in plain forms), creatine monohydrate (no calories, no insulin response), caffeine (plain, without additions), most fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) in capsule form — these contain minimal oil that does not meaningfully stimulate insulin. Magnesium glycinate, zinc, vitamin C, and B vitamins taken with plain water all have negligible caloric content and do not interfere with fasting goals.

Supplements That Break or Complicate a Fast

Protein supplements — whey, casein, collagen, BCAAs — all stimulate insulin secretion meaningfully and should be taken within the eating window. Fish oil capsules contain fat calories and technically break a strict fast, though the caloric amount (typically 10–15 calories) is unlikely to matter for most fasting purposes. MCT oil stimulates insulin and should be held for eating windows if ketosis is a fasting goal. Gummy vitamins contain sugar and calories. Probiotic capsules should ideally be taken with food to maximize survival through gastric acid, making eating-window administration functionally better regardless of fasting considerations.

Timing Strategy

A practical approach: take fat-soluble vitamins (D, K2, omega-3) with the first meal of the eating window (fat in the meal improves their absorption). Take magnesium at night before bed (best timing for sleep support regardless of fasting). Creatine can go any time. Protein supplements belong firmly within the eating window. Iron supplements are best taken 30 minutes before breaking fast if on an empty stomach for absorption purposes — a legitimate reason to take it just before the eating window opens.

Sources

  1. Longo VD & Panda S. "Fasting, circadian rhythms, and time-restricted feeding in healthy lifespan." Cell Metabolism, 2016.
  2. Anton SD, et al. "Flipping the metabolic switch: understanding and applying the health benefits of fasting." Obesity, 2018.
  3. Moro T, et al. "Effects of eight weeks of time-restricted feeding on basal metabolism, maximal strength, body composition, inflammation, and cardiovascular risk factors." Journal of Translational Medicine, 2016.
Breakthrough

Probiotics and Antibiotics: Timing Matters

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

The advice to take probiotics alongside antibiotics is so common it has become reflexive. The problem is that the timing and strain selection of co-administration can determine whether probiotics help, do nothing, or actively interfere with gut microbiome recovery after the antibiotic course ends.

Why Antibiotics Devastate the Gut Microbiome

Antibiotics are not selective weapons. A course of broad-spectrum antibiotics like amoxicillin-clavulanate or ciprofloxacin can reduce microbial diversity by 25–50% within 24 hours of the first dose. This ecological collapse is the underlying cause of antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD), which affects 5–30% of antibiotic users, and Clostridioides difficile infection, which causes around 450,000 infections in the US annually.

The Timing Problem

A landmark 2018 Cell paper by Suez and colleagues at the Weizmann Institute found that the probiotic group showed the slowest return to a normal microbiome after antibiotics — taking up to five months compared to three weeks in the untreated group. Probiotic organisms colonized the gut and displaced returning native bacteria, delaying ecological recovery. If the goal is microbiome recovery after antibiotics, taking probiotics immediately after may actually impede that recovery.

Where Probiotics Do Help: During Treatment

A 2019 Cochrane meta-analysis of 82 randomized trials and over 11,000 patients found that probiotics reduced the risk of AAD by approximately 42% compared to placebo. The most effective strains were Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and Saccharomyces boulardii. Optimal timing: take the probiotic 2 hours away from the antibiotic dose. S. boulardii, a yeast rather than a bacterium, is uniquely resistant to antibiotics and can survive concurrent administration.

Post-Antibiotic Recovery

For long-term microbiome recovery after a course ends, focusing on dietary diversity — fermented foods, prebiotic fiber, varied plant intake — may outperform probiotic supplementation. Prebiotics provide substrate that native bacteria use more efficiently than exogenous probiotic strains.

Sources

  1. Suez J, et al. “Post-Antibiotic Gut Mucosal Microbiome Reconstitution Is Impaired by Probiotics and Improved by Autologous FMT.” Cell, 2018.
  2. Goldenberg JZ, et al. “Probiotics for the prevention of Clostridium difficile-associated diarrhea in adults and children.” Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2017.
  3. Blaabjerg S, et al. “Probiotics for the Prevention of Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea in Outpatients.” Antibiotics, 2017.
Guide

The Best Supplements for Vegetarians and Vegans

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

Plant-based diets offer substantial health benefits but create predictable nutritional gaps. Several critical micronutrients are either absent from plants entirely, found in poorly absorbed forms, or present at levels requiring unrealistically large food amounts to meet daily requirements.

Vitamin B12: Non-Negotiable for Vegans

B12 is synthesized exclusively by microorganisms and found in meaningful quantities only in animal products. Vegans who do not supplement are virtually guaranteed to become deficient over time. B12 deficiency causes irreversible neurological damage if prolonged. Methylcobalamin or adenosylcobalamin are biologically active forms. Weekly high-dose supplementation (1,000–2,000 mcg weekly) works via passive diffusion and bypasses intrinsic factor issues reliably.

Vitamin D3 and Omega-3 DHA/EPA

Most D3 supplements derive from lanolin (non-vegan). Vegan D3 from algae is bioequivalent and widely available — target 1,000–2,000 IU daily. ALA conversion to DHA and EPA is highly inefficient in humans (under 0.5% to DHA). Algal oil supplements provide DHA and EPA directly. 200–300 mg DHA daily from algal oil is a reasonable minimum.

Iron, Zinc, Iodine, and Calcium

Non-heme iron (from plants) absorbs at 2–20% vs 15–35% for heme iron. Iron bisglycinate is better tolerated than ferrous sulfate. Phytic acid in legumes and grains reduces zinc absorption — vegetarians need approximately 50% more dietary zinc than omnivores per NIH. Vegans avoiding dairy and seafood are at serious iodine insufficiency risk; 150 mcg potassium iodide daily is reliable. Calcium citrate absorbs better than calcium carbonate at any meal timing and without requiring stomach acid.

Sources

  1. Melina V, Craig W, Levin S. “Position of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics: Vegetarian Diets.” Journal of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics, 2016.
  2. Saunders AV, Craig WJ, Baines SK. “Zinc and vegetarian diets.” Medical Journal of Australia, 2013.
  3. Swanson D, et al. “Omega-3 Fatty Acids EPA and DHA: Health Benefits Throughout Life.” Advances in Nutrition, 2012.
Reality Check

Tribulus Terrestris: Zero Evidence for Testosterone

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Tribulus terrestris has been one of the most consistently stocked supplements in gym retail — sold as a natural testosterone booster marketed aggressively to men seeking performance enhancement. The problem is straightforward: when put to rigorous clinical testing, it does not increase testosterone.

The Origins of the Claim

The testosterone claim traces back to Eastern European athlete anecdotes from the 1970s–1980s and animal studies suggesting saponin compounds might stimulate luteinizing hormone (LH) production. In rat models, these effects were observed. The critical mistake was assuming the same mechanism would operate in healthy human males. Rats have significantly different hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis regulation than humans, and compounds that manipulate LH in rodents frequently have no effect on the human axis. This translational failure is one of the most common sources of false supplement claims.

Tribulus & Testosterone

Results across 12 placebo-controlled human trials

Rodent studies (T↑)species-specific effect
Yes
Small open-labelbiased, no placebo
Mixed
RCTs: serum T change12/12 trials
0/12
Body composition gainsresistance-trained males
None
Libido (subjective)post-hoc rating scales
Possible
Twelve human RCTs. Zero show a testosterone increase vs. placebo. The subjective libido signal does not require a T change.

What the Clinical Trials Show

Multiple double-blind, placebo-controlled trials in young healthy men have consistently found tribulus at doses of 450–1,500 mg daily for 4–8 weeks produces no significant change in serum testosterone, LH, FSH, or any other androgen marker. There is no published RCT in healthy men showing a statistically significant testosterone increase from tribulus. The trials have been done and are consistently negative. Some weak preliminary evidence suggests libido improvement in men with hypoactive sexual desire disorder, independent of testosterone, but this is insufficient for clinical recommendation.

Why It Remains on Shelves

Supplements do not require proof of efficacy to be sold. Tribulus manufacturers cite rodent studies, traditional use claims, and mechanistic hypotheses — none of which constitute human clinical evidence. Consumers reasonably but mistakenly assume that widespread retail availability implies proven effectiveness.

Sources

  1. Neychev VK, Mitev VI. “The aphrodisiac herb Tribulus terrestris does not influence the androgen production in young men.” Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 2005.
  2. Rogerson S, et al. “The effect of five weeks of Tribulus terrestris supplementation on muscle strength and body composition during preseason training in elite rugby league players.” Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 2007.
  3. Antonio J, et al. “The effects of Tribulus terrestris on body composition and exercise performance in resistance-trained males.” International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 2000.
Safety Alert

Contaminated Protein Powders: Lead, Arsenic, and BPA

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Protein powders are among the most widely used supplements globally. Most consumers assume that a product sold legally at retail — especially at premium price points — has been verified as safe. That assumption is incorrect, and the contamination data is genuinely concerning.

The Clean Label Project Study

In 2018, the Clean Label Project published an analysis of 134 best-selling protein powder products across 52 brands. The findings were striking: 70% of tested products had detectable lead levels; 74% had detectable cadmium; 55% had detectable BPA (bisphenol A, an endocrine disruptor). Plant-based protein powders — particularly brown rice and pea protein — showed significantly higher heavy metal contamination than whey proteins. Several products exceeded California's Proposition 65 daily safety limits in a single serving. Given that many users consume two or more servings daily, cumulative heavy metal exposure from protein powder use alone could approach regulatory safety thresholds over time.

Why Protein Powders Are Especially Vulnerable

The manufacturing process concentrates not just protein but any contaminants in the source material. Brown rice protein bioaccumulates inorganic arsenic from irrigation water at higher levels than most other grains due to flooded growing conditions. BPA contamination can enter through BPA-lined processing equipment or plastic packaging — a quality control issue that varies by facility. The FDA does not routinely test supplements for heavy metals pre-market, creating a regulatory gap where manufacturers are technically responsible but face no pre-market testing requirement to verify it.

How to Reduce Your Risk

Choose products with NSF Certified for Sport or Informed Sport certification — both programs include heavy metal testing. Consult the Clean Label Project's searchable product database at cleanlabelproject.org. Prioritize whey or casein over plant-based protein if heavy metal risk is a primary concern. Limit protein powder use to what is necessary — food sources of protein don't carry the same concentration risk.

Sources

  1. Clean Label Project. “Protein Powder Study.” cleanlabelproject.org, 2018.
  2. Meharg AA, et al. “Inorganic arsenic levels in rice milk exceed EU and US drinking water standards.” Journal of Environmental Monitoring, 2008.
  3. Consumer Reports. “How Much Arsenic Is in Your Protein Powder?” consumerreports.org, 2018.
Reality Check

Turkey Tail Mushroom: Cancer Claims vs Reality

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Turkey tail (Trametes versicolor) has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries and has become one of the most commercially prominent medicinal mushroom supplements in Western markets. The marketing often implies — or outright states — that turkey tail can treat or prevent cancer. This claim goes well beyond what the science supports.

The Active Compounds and What They Actually Do

Turkey tail contains two polysaccharide complexes: polysaccharide-K (PSK, also known as Krestin) and polysaccharide-peptide (PSP). PSK is approved in Japan as an adjunct cancer therapy — used alongside conventional cancer treatment, not as a standalone treatment. PSK research from Japan showed that PSK supplementation alongside standard chemotherapy improved survival outcomes in colorectal and gastric cancer patients. This is real evidence — but for PSK specifically, in cancer treatment contexts, alongside chemotherapy, in Japanese populations.

Turkey Tail: Claim vs. Evidence

PSK/PSP research separated from marketing

PSK adjuvant w/ chemo (Japan)approved in Japan
Moderate
NK-cell immune markershuman, small trials
Limited
Cancer preventionno prospective data
Weak
Standalone cancer treatmentnot tested, not approved
None
General 'immune support'healthy-adult trials
Weak
PSK/PSP is a real adjuvant in Japanese gastric-cancer protocols. OTC mushroom capsules are not the same product.

The Retail Product Gap
