What this does not support: turkey tail supplements preventing cancer in healthy people, replacing conventional therapy, or commercial products that have not standardized PSK content being equivalent to pharmaceutical PSK extracts used in clinical research. Most retail turkey tail supplements in the US do not verify PSK content or replicate the dosing (3 g/day pure PSK) used in trials. A 2012 USDA-funded study found turkey tail enhanced natural killer cell function in breast cancer recovery patients — a legitimate modest finding for immune support, not cancer treatment.

Safety

Turkey tail is generally safe at typical supplement doses. Patients undergoing chemotherapy should consult their oncologist before adding any immune-modulating supplement, as interactions with immunosuppressive regimens are theoretically possible.

Sources

  1. Kidd PM. “The use of mushroom glucans and proteoglycans in cancer treatment.” Alternative Medicine Review, 2000.
  2. Standish LJ, et al. “Trametes versicolor Mushroom Immune Therapy in Breast Cancer.” Journal of the Society for Integrative Oncology, 2008.
  3. Torkelson CJ, et al. “Phase 1 Clinical Trial of Trametes versicolor in Women with Breast Cancer.” ISRN Oncology, 2012.
Kids

Omega-3 DHA During Pregnancy: Critical for Brain Development

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) accounts for approximately 97% of the omega-3 fatty acids in the brain and 93% of those in the retina. The fetal brain accumulates DHA at an accelerating rate during the third trimester and the first two years of postnatal life — the critical window for neural migration, myelination, and synaptogenesis.

What Maternal DHA Status Determines

The fetus depends entirely on maternal DHA transfer across the placenta. The placenta actively concentrates DHA in fetal circulation above maternal levels — but this only works if maternal DHA is adequate. Maternal DHA status in the third trimester predicts neonatal visual acuity, early cognitive development scores, and language acquisition. A 2013 Cochrane review of 11 RCTs found DHA and EPA supplementation during pregnancy improved infant visual development and reduced preterm delivery risk, with positive signals for long-term cognitive outcomes.

Current Intake Gaps and the Mercury Dilemma

The WHO recommends 200–300 mg/day of DHA during pregnancy and lactation. Most Western pregnant women consume 50–100 mg/day. Prenatal vitamins frequently include omega-3s heavily weighted toward ALA (plant-based), not DHA. Fatty fish low in mercury — salmon, sardines, anchovies, herring — can safely be eaten 2–3 times per week per FDA/EPA guidance. For women who avoid fish, algal oil DHA bypasses the mercury issue entirely: algae produce DHA directly with no heavy metal accumulation from the marine food chain.

Breastfeeding

Breast milk DHA content directly reflects maternal intake. Breastfeeding women who eat little fish produce milk with DHA levels that may be inadequate for optimal neural development. The WHO 200–300 mg/day recommendation applies throughout breastfeeding.

Sources

  1. Middleton P, et al. “Omega-3 fatty acid addition during pregnancy.” Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2018.
  2. Koletzko B, et al. “The roles of long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids in pregnancy, lactation and infancy.” Annals of Nutrition and Metabolism, 2007.
  3. FDA/EPA. “Advice about Eating Fish: For Women Who Are or Might Become Pregnant, Breastfeeding Mothers, and Young Children.” FDA, 2024.
Breakthrough

NAC for Mental Health: OCD, Addiction, and Depression

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is best known as the antidote for acetaminophen overdose. Over the last two decades, a growing body of psychiatric research has found NAC producing clinically meaningful effects in OCD, addiction, and depression — via mechanisms that have made it one of the more serious candidates for psychiatric supplementation.

The Glutamate-Glutathione Connection

NAC works on two relevant psychiatric pathways. As a cysteine precursor, it replenishes glutathione — the brain's primary antioxidant. More importantly, NAC modulates the cystine-glutamate antiporter, which regulates extracellular glutamate. Dysregulated glutamate signaling is centrally implicated in addiction, OCD, and treatment-resistant depression.

NAC for OCD and Addiction

A 2012 double-blind RCT in the Journal of Clinical Psychiatry tested NAC (2,400 mg/day) as an adjunct to SRIs in OCD patients with incomplete response. At 16 weeks, the NAC group showed statistically and clinically significant reductions in Y-BOCS scores — the standard OCD severity measure. For addiction, multiple RCTs have found NAC reducing craving and use in cannabis and cocaine dependence. A 2017 Cochrane review concluded the evidence was promising enough for further large-scale trials, with cannabis data most robust. The mechanism is glutamate homeostasis: chronic drug use dysregulates the cystine-glutamate exchanger, and NAC appears to restore it.

Depression and Dosing

Several RCTs have found NAC producing antidepressant effects, particularly in bipolar depression and cases with elevated inflammatory markers. Evidence is insufficient for first-line recommendation but supports adjunct use in treatment-resistant cases. Most psychiatric NAC research uses 1,800–2,400 mg/day in divided doses. Gastrointestinal side effects are the most common complaint. Anyone taking NAC alongside psychiatric medications should discuss it with their prescribing physician.

Sources

  1. Afshar H, et al. “N-acetylcysteine add-on treatment in refractory obsessive-compulsive disorder: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial.” Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, 2012.
  2. Gray KM, et al. “N-acetylcysteine for cannabis use disorder: A randomized, placebo-controlled pilot study.” American Journal of Psychiatry, 2012.
  3. Berk M, et al. “N-acetylcysteine for depressive symptoms in bipolar disorder — a double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial.” Biological Psychiatry, 2008.
Reality Check

The Supplement Industry Greenwashing Problem

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Browse any major supplement website and you'll encounter a visual language of environmental virtue: earth tones, leaf imagery, words like "clean," "pure," "natural," "sustainably sourced," and "eco-friendly." These terms are not regulated. They have no legal definition in the context of dietary supplements. They function as marketing signals, not verified facts.

What "Natural" Means Legally: Nothing

The FDA has no official definition for "natural" as applied to dietary supplements. Any manufacturer can print "all natural" on a product containing titanium dioxide (a synthetic whitening agent) and magnesium stearate (a synthetic lubricant) with zero regulatory consequence. "Sustainably sourced" similarly has no regulatory definition or third-party verification requirement. A product claiming sustainably sourced ashwagandha might mean a genuine regenerative farming partnership — or it might mean the company purchased from a broker who claims their supplier practices are responsible, with no audit chain to verify it.

Label vs. Bottle

What's actually in your 'premium' supplement

Label = bottle contentNYSAG DNA barcoding 2015
20%
'Natural' is regulatedFDA position: no
None
'Clinically proven' = productusually ingredient only
15%
3rd-party tested (any)NSF / Informed / USP
35%
USP-verifiedgold standard
8%
NYSAG tested 4 major retailers: 4 of 5 products had none of the labeled herb. The industry is not independently policed.

The Organic Exception

USDA Organic certification does apply to supplements when the organic claim is made — requiring at least 95% organically produced ingredients produced without prohibited synthetic pesticides, GMOs, or irradiation. This is one of the few environmentally significant certifications with real weight. "Clean label" is an industry marketing term, not a regulatory category — a product can be "clean label" and still be ineffective, contaminated with heavy metals, or sourced using environmentally destructive practices.

What Meaningful Certification Looks Like

Certifications worth trusting: USDA Organic, Rainforest Alliance, Fair Trade USA, and B Corporation certification for whole-company environmental and social performance. Everything else labeled "natural" or "sustainable" is unverified marketing language.

Sources

  1. FDA. “Use of the Term Natural on Food Labeling.” fda.gov, 2023.
  2. Roe B, et al. “Consumer perceptions of natural and organic food label claims.” Journal of Food Products Marketing, 2014.
  3. USDA National Organic Program. “Organic Regulations.” ams.usda.gov, 2024.
Guide

Boswellia vs NSAIDs for Joint Pain

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

NSAIDs — ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac — are effective and fast for joint pain. They are also associated with significant gastrointestinal and cardiovascular risks with chronic use. Boswellia serrata (Indian frankincense) has a distinct mechanism and an emerging clinical evidence base that makes it a legitimate option for patients needing long-term joint pain management who want to minimize NSAID exposure.

Different Mechanisms

NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) enzymes, blocking prostaglandin synthesis. COX-1 inhibition also reduces the protective prostaglandins maintaining the gastric mucosal lining — causing GI effects ranging from indigestion to peptic ulcer. Long-term NSAID use is associated with a 3–5x increase in serious GI complications and independently increases cardiovascular event risk. Boswellic acids (particularly AKBA) work through a different pathway: inhibiting 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX), the enzyme responsible for leukotriene synthesis. Leukotrienes drive cartilage degradation in osteoarthritis and synovial inflammation. This 5-LOX inhibition does not impair gastric mucosal protection.

What the Clinical Trials Show

A 2011 systematic review in Phytomedicine assessed five randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials of Boswellia extract in osteoarthritis patients. All five trials showed statistically significant improvements in pain and physical function vs placebo. A direct comparison trial found Boswellia and valdecoxib (a COX-2 inhibitor) produced similar pain reduction, but Boswellia maintained improvements longer after the treatment period ended. A 2019 RCT found AKBA-enriched extract at 100–250 mg/day produced clinically meaningful pain relief at 90 days with no significant adverse effects.

Practical Guidance

Boswellia takes 4–8 weeks to show benefit — not a rescue treatment but a chronic management option. Look for products standardized to at least 30% boswellic acids with measurable AKBA content. Take with a fat-containing meal for best absorption. Products using ApresFlex or 5-Loxin forms have direct RCT support.

Sources

  1. Siddiqui MZ. “Boswellia serrata, a potential antiinflammatory agent: an overview.” Indian Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 2011.
  2. Kimmatkar N, et al. “Efficacy and tolerability of Boswellia serrata extract in treatment of osteoarthritis of knee.” Phytomedicine, 2003.
  3. Sengupta K, et al. “A double blind, randomized, placebo controlled study of the efficacy and safety of 5-Loxin for treatment of osteoarthritis of the knee.” Arthritis Research & Therapy, 2008.
Safety Alert

SARMs: The Illegal Supplements in Your Gym

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Selective androgen receptor modulators (SARMs) are a class of compounds originally developed to treat muscle wasting and osteoporosis with a more favorable safety profile than anabolic steroids. None have been approved by the FDA for any medical use in humans. All are actively sold online and in gyms — often labeled as "research chemicals" — leaving millions of consumers unknowingly using unapproved drugs with documented serious risks.

The Appeal and the Reality

SARMs were designed to selectively activate androgen receptors in muscle and bone while minimizing activity in the prostate and liver. In theory, this delivers anabolic benefits without the serious side effect profile of testosterone and synthetic androgens. In practice, the selectivity is incomplete, recreational doses frequently exceed what was studied, products are not pharmaceutical-grade, and long-term safety data in healthy people is essentially nonexistent — because no SARM completed FDA approval, so the required safety research was never done.

Documented Adverse Effects

FDA adverse event reports document: liver toxicity (including acute liver failure), hypogonadism and testosterone suppression sometimes requiring hormonal intervention, stroke in young athletes, and cardiac events. A 2020 analysis of FDA's FAERS database found liver injury as the most common serious adverse event. The FDA issued consumer warnings in 2017, 2018, and 2020, noting products had been found containing SARMs not on the label or at concentrations far exceeding stated levels. Selling SARMs as human dietary supplements is illegal under DSHEA. The SARMs Control Act, if passed, would classify them as Schedule III controlled substances.

If You Have Used SARMs

If you experience jaundice, dark urine, fatigue, elevated heart rate, testicular atrophy, or significant mood changes, seek medical evaluation. Inform your physician — many are unfamiliar with SARMs. Request liver enzymes (AST, ALT), a lipid panel, and a hormone panel (testosterone, LH, FSH) as an initial assessment.

Sources

  1. FDA. “FDA warns against using SARMs in body-building products.” fda.gov, 2017 and 2020 updates.
  2. Ganesan K, et al. “Selective androgen receptor modulators as anabolic therapies.” Drugs, 2019.
  3. Flores JE, et al. “Drug-Induced Liver Injury Associated with Selective Androgen Receptor Modulators Use.” ACG Case Reports Journal, 2020.
Reality Check

Shilajit: Ancient Remedy or Modern Marketing?

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Shilajit is a blackish-brown resinous material that seeps from rock cracks in high-altitude mountain ranges, formed over centuries by the decomposition of plant matter under pressure. In Ayurvedic medicine it is classified as a rejuvenating substance. In the current Western supplement market it is promoted as a testosterone booster, energy enhancer, and anti-aging remedy at prices reaching $50–$100 per month. The human evidence base is thin.

What It Contains and the Clinical Gap

Purified shilajit contains fulvic acid (40–60% of content), dibenzo-alpha-pyrones, and over 80 mineral compounds in ionic form. Human clinical trials are limited, small, and inconsistent. A 2015 Andrologia RCT found improvements in sperm count and testosterone in infertile men — real but not generalizable to healthy men. A 2019 Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition study found modest strength improvements in healthy men, with small effect sizes. A 2012 study found shilajit did not significantly affect testosterone in healthy men at 200 mg/day — contradicting the fertility study's results. The database is too limited to draw firm conclusions for any specific outcome in healthy adults.

Shilajit: Contamination Audit

What's in the bottle beyond the marketing

Lead > WHO limitsampled batches
~45%
Arsenic > safe limitsampled batches
~30%
Mercury detectedsampled batches
~15%
Fulvic acid as labeledHPLC verification
Rare
Batch-to-batch variancesame brand, diff lots
Poor
Shilajit is a geological exudate — heavy-metal contamination is a known baseline risk, not a manufacturing flaw.

Contamination Risk

Raw or poorly purified shilajit can contain significant heavy metals (arsenic, lead, mercury, cadmium) reflecting the geological environment from which it was sourced. Purification quality varies substantially by manufacturer, and shilajit has no formal standardization framework unlike most plant-based supplements. This makes independent third-party testing particularly important before use.

Bottom Line

The claims made for shilajit — testosterone boosting, energy enhancement, anti-aging — are not supported by adequate human clinical evidence. The contamination risk adds another reason for caution with products of uncertain quality.

Sources

  1. Biswas TK, et al. “Clinical evaluation of spermatogenic activity of processed shilajit in oligospermia.” Andrologia, 2010.
  2. Keller JL, et al. “The effects of Shilajit supplementation on fatigue-induced decreases in muscular strength and serum hydroxyproline levels.” Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition, 2019.
  3. Meena H, et al. “Shilajit: A panacea for high-altitude problems.” International Journal of Ayurveda Research, 2010.
Kids

Probiotics for Babies: What Pediatric Research Shows

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

The infant probiotic market has grown substantially, driven by the premise that supplementing a developing microbiome improves health outcomes. The infant literature has specific, well-studied applications worth distinguishing from general supplement marketing noise.

The Infant Microbiome

At birth, the gut is essentially sterile. The gut microbiome is colonized from the birth canal, maternal skin, breast milk, and environment. Caesarean-section infants miss vaginal colonization and show microbiome differences persisting for years in some studies. Breastfeeding is the most powerful natural modulator of early microbiome development, delivering human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs) that selectively feed beneficial Bifidobacterium species.

Infant Colic: The Strongest Evidence

The most consistent pediatric probiotic evidence is for Lactobacillus reuteri DSM 17938 for infant colic. A 2014 Pediatrics meta-analysis of five randomized trials found L. reuteri DSM 17938 reduced crying time in breastfed infants with colic by approximately 50 minutes per day versus placebo. This effect was specific to breastfed infants. For necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) in premature infants, a Cochrane meta-analysis of 56 trials found probiotic supplementation reduced NEC incidence and all-cause mortality — now incorporated into some NICU protocols.

What the Evidence Does Not Support

Routine probiotic supplementation for all infants to "boost immunity" or "support development" lacks robust evidence. Parents should be skeptical of infant probiotic products with generic claims that do not specify strain or cite infant clinical research. Safety note: extremely premature infants (<32 weeks) and immunocompromised infants carry a theoretical risk of probiotic sepsis — use only under neonatal medical supervision.

Sources

  1. Sung V, et al. “Lactobacillus reuteri to Treat Infant Colic: A Meta-analysis.” Pediatrics, 2018.
  2. Bi LW, et al. “Which is the best probiotic treatment strategy to prevent the necrotizing enterocolitis in premature infants?” Medicine, 2019.
  3. Thomas DW, Greer FR. “Probiotics and prebiotics in pediatrics.” Pediatrics, 2010.
Breakthrough

How Exercise Outperforms Every Supplement

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

This is not a motivational essay. It is a direct comparison of effect sizes. When you place the biological outcomes of regular exercise against the biological outcomes of any supplement — including the best-supported ones — exercise wins every comparison that matters, typically by a wide margin.

Cardiovascular Health

A 2018 meta-analysis in Circulation of 174 studies found that meeting physical activity guidelines (150 minutes moderate-intensity exercise per week) was associated with a 35% reduction in cardiovascular mortality and a 35% reduction in all-cause mortality. The best cardiovascular supplement — omega-3s at 4 g/day in the REDUCE-IT trial — reduced major cardiovascular events by 25% in a high-risk population using a pharmaceutical-grade formulation at a dose far exceeding typical supplement use. Exercise produces a larger absolute risk reduction across a broader population at zero financial cost.

Cognitive Function, Inflammation, and Metabolic Health

A 2011 RCT in PNAS found that 1 year of aerobic exercise increased hippocampal volume by 2% in older adults — reversing typical age-related loss. No supplement has demonstrated structural brain volume increases in a controlled human trial. Regular moderate-intensity exercise reduces CRP, IL-6, and TNF-alpha through multiple mechanisms; the best anti-inflammatory supplement (fish oil) has consistently smaller effect sizes. The Diabetes Prevention Program found structured exercise reduced diabetes incidence by 58% over 2.8 years — more than metformin (31%) and any supplement ever tested.

Depression and the Correct Framing

A 2023 BMJ meta-analysis of 218 RCTs found exercise superior to many antidepressants in effect size for mild-to-moderate depression. Ashwagandha, the best-evidenced supplement for anxiety, shows effect sizes meaningfully smaller than those produced by aerobic exercise. The correct framing: supplements are adjuncts to a physically active life, not replacements for one.

Sources

  1. Erickson KI, et al. “Exercise training increases size of hippocampus and improves memory.” PNAS, 2011.
  2. Knowler WC, et al. “Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin.” New England Journal of Medicine, 2002.
  3. Noetel M, et al. “Effect of exercise for depression: systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials.” BMJ, 2024.
Reality Check

The Nootropics Myth: Smart Drugs That Aren’t Smart

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

The current nootropics market — worth over $5 billion annually — sells everything from lion's mane to racetam compounds to focus stacks of a dozen herbs, under the premise that they make healthy brains work better. The evidence for most of it is somewhere between thin and nonexistent for healthy adults.

The Healthy Brain Problem

The foundational challenge for nootropic research: the trials showing cognitive improvement from supplements are predominantly conducted in populations with cognitive deficits — elderly adults with mild cognitive impairment, dementia patients, people under extreme sleep restriction. The effect on these populations does not generalize to healthy young or middle-aged adults with normally functioning brains. A 2017 systematic review assessed 19 nootropic supplements and found that while several showed benefits in cognitively impaired populations, evidence of cognitive enhancement in healthy adults was consistently absent or unreliable.

Cognitive Benefit in Healthy Adults

Nootropics ranked by actual replicated effect

Caffeineattention, reaction time
Strong
Modafinil (Rx)sleep-deprived subjects
Strong
Creatine 5 gworking memory if depleted
Moderate
L-theanine + caffattention under stress
Modest
Alpha-GPCmild, sport-focused
Weak
Racetams / NoopeptOTC, no FDA approval
Weak
Two freely-available compounds (caffeine, creatine) dominate the nootropic evidence base. The rest is mostly story.

The Most Overhyped Nootropics

Bacopa monnieri has genuine evidence for working memory in older adults after 8–12 weeks, but reliably slows processing speed in the first few weeks. The marketing version presents it as an acute cognitive enhancer; the real version is a slow-acting compound with mild long-term memory benefits in older populations. Ginkgo biloba: the GEMS trial — a large well-funded RCT — found no reduction in cognitive decline, dementia incidence, or memory test performance after 6 years of supplementation. Phosphatidylserine: FDA describes its qualified health claim as "highly uncertain"; evidence in healthy young adults is nearly absent.

What Actually Works in Healthy People

The honest short list: caffeine (the most potent cognitive supplement known), L-theanine combined with caffeine, and creatine for working memory under fatigue or sleep deprivation. Sleep, exercise, and stress management produce cognitive benefits an order of magnitude larger than any supplement.

Sources

  1. Suliman NA, et al. “Establishing Natural Nootropics: Recent Molecular Enhancement Influenced by Natural Nootropic.” Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2016.
  2. DeKosky ST, et al. “Ginkgo biloba for Prevention of Dementia: A Randomized Controlled Trial.” JAMA, 2008.
  3. Aguiar S, Borowski T. “Neuropharmacological review of the nootropic herb Bacopa monnieri.” Rejuvenation Research, 2013.
Safety Alert

Supplement Interactions with Blood Pressure Medications

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Hypertension affects nearly half of all American adults. The supplements commonly used by people with hypertension — potassium, magnesium, CoQ10, hawthorn, licorice root — can interact with antihypertensive medications in ways ranging from beneficial and additive to dangerous and potentially life-threatening. Most patients do not disclose supplement use to their physicians, and most physicians do not ask.

Potassium: The Double-Edged Mineral

ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) and ARBs (losartan, valsartan) raise blood potassium by reducing aldosterone-mediated potassium excretion. Adding potassium supplements to either drug class can cause hyperkalemia — dangerously elevated blood potassium causing cardiac arrhythmias that can be rapidly fatal. This is a documented cause of adverse events. Potassium supplementation in patients taking ACE inhibitors or ARBs requires regular lab monitoring and physician oversight.

Licorice Root: The Underappreciated Risk

Licorice root contains glycyrrhizin, which effectively causes mineralocorticoid excess that raises blood pressure and causes potassium wasting. Regular consumption can significantly counteract antihypertensive medications at doses as low as 100 mg/day of glycyrrhizin. Licorice is a common ingredient in "adrenal support" and "cortisol balance" products where it may not be prominently labeled.

Magnesium, Hawthorn, and CoQ10

Magnesium supplementation has mild antihypertensive effects and is generally safe with blood pressure medications; the primary caution is with potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride). Hawthorn extract can potentiate antihypertensives and digoxin — potentially causing excessive BP reduction. CoQ10 produces modest antihypertensive effects (typically 10–17 mmHg reduction in systolic BP at 100–200 mg/day) and can reduce the anticoagulant effect of warfarin.

Sources

  1. Rossi S (ed.). “Licorice root.” Australian Medicines Handbook, 2019.
  2. Houston M. “The role of magnesium in hypertension and cardiovascular disease.” Journal of Clinical Hypertension, 2011.
  3. Bhatt DL, et al. “Drug interactions with nutraceuticals.” Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 2016.
Guide

Mushroom Supplements: Lion's Mane, Reishi, and What Works

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

The medicinal mushroom category has exploded, with lion's mane, reishi, chaga, cordyceps, and turkey tail now found in everything from coffee blends to gummies to expensive multi-mushroom powders. Each species has a distinct bioactive compound profile and distinct clinical evidence base. Marketing treats them as interchangeable wellness agents; the science does not.

Lion's Mane: The Strongest Cognitive Evidence

Lion's mane contains hericenones and erinacines that stimulate nerve growth factor (NGF) synthesis. A landmark 2009 double-blind RCT (Mori et al.) in Phytotherapy Research randomized 50 Japanese adults with mild cognitive impairment to 3 g/day lion's mane or placebo for 16 weeks. The lion's mane group showed significantly higher cognitive assessment scores, with improvements reversing when supplementation stopped. A 2020 parallel RCT in healthy older adults showed improvements in information processing and working memory. Key quality point: products made from mycelium grown on grain substrate are predominantly grain starch with minimal active compounds. Look for fruiting body extraction and beta-glucan content above 25%.

Reishi: Sleep and Immune Modulation

The most consistent clinical findings for reishi are for sleep quality: a 2012 RCT found reishi spore powder significantly reduced time to sleep onset and improved sleep quality scores. The mechanism involves triterpenes modulating GABAergic signaling. Reishi also has immune-modulating evidence, particularly polysaccharide content affecting natural killer cell activity. For cancer support (as an adjunct, not treatment), some positive findings exist for fatigue and quality of life.

Cordyceps and Chaga

Cordyceps is marketed aggressively for athletic performance. A 2016 RCT found modest VO2max improvements (1–2%) in healthy young adults — small and not consistently replicated. Chaga (Inonotus obliquus) has extensive in vitro and animal data but essentially no human clinical trials as of 2026. Marketing claims are significantly ahead of evidence. Chaga also contains oxalates at relatively high levels — a concern for those prone to kidney stones.

Sources

  1. Mori K, et al. “Improving effects of the mushroom Yamabushitake (Hericium erinaceus) on mild cognitive impairment: a double-blind placebo-controlled clinical trial.” Phytotherapy Research, 2009.
  2. Tang W, et al. “A randomized, double-blind and placebo-controlled study of a Ganoderma lucidum polysaccharide extract in neurasthenia.” Journal of Medicinal Food, 2005.
  3. Hirsch KR, et al. “Cordyceps militaris Improves Tolerance to High-Intensity Exercise After Acute and Chronic Supplementation.” Journal of Dietary Supplements, 2017.
Reality Check

Fadogia Agrestis: The Influencer Supplement with Zero Human Data

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

In 2022, a single podcast appearance discussing the theoretical testosterone-boosting mechanism of fadogia agrestis — a shrub native to Nigeria — drove an astronomical spike in Google searches, product launches, and sales. Within months, fadogia appeared in dozens of commercially sold testosterone supplements. This is how supplement trends work in the social media era: one influential mention, no human data, full commercial deployment.

The Rat Study That Launched a Product Category

Fadogia agrestis has been studied almost exclusively in rodent models. The initial interest came from a 2005 paper in the Asian Journal of Andrology (Yakubu et al.) finding that aqueous stem extract increased testosterone levels in male rats dose-dependently. This is the totality of the mechanistic evidence base that fueled commercial launch in human supplement products. As of 2026, there are zero published randomized controlled trials of fadogia agrestis in humans — no pharmacokinetic studies, no dose-finding studies, no safety studies, no efficacy studies in any human population. The move from rat data to commercial human supplementation with no intervening clinical research bypasses the basic research pipeline that exists to protect consumers.

Fadogia Agrestis: The Research Base

What actually exists in the literature

Rodent studies2 papers, Nigerian lab
2
Human trials (any)PubMed, all years
0
Safety data in humansdose, duration, toxicity
None
Podcast mentions (2021–24)Huberman, JRE, etc.
100s
US retail SKUs todayAmazon + specialty
400+
A compound with zero human trials went from obscurity to category-leader in 24 months — driven by two podcast appearances.

Toxicity Signals That Were Ignored

The same rodent literature that generated excitement also contains toxicity findings that received far less commercial attention. The Yakubu 2005 paper itself noted histological changes in testicular tissue at higher doses. A subsequent 2008 study by the same group found high-dose extract caused significant liver toxicity and kidney damage in male rats at doses extrapolated to be achievable with commercial supplement products. The podcast discussion that triggered the fadogia trend acknowledged the lack of human data; the supplement labels that followed did not.

Sources

  1. Yakubu MT, Afolabi MO, Oladiji AT. “Aphrodisiac potentials of the aqueous extract of Fadogia agrestis stem in male albino rats.” Asian Journal of Andrology, 2005.
  2. Yakubu MT, Akanji MA, Oladiji AT. “Male sexual dysfunction and methods used in assessing medicinal plants with aphrodisiac potentials.” Pharmacognosy Reviews, 2007.
  3. Irvine FR. Woody Plants of Ghana. Oxford University Press, 1961.
Kids

Melatonin Gummies for Kids: The Overdosing Problem

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Pediatric melatonin use in the United States has increased dramatically over the past decade. A 2023 analysis found approximately 20% of children aged 5–13 regularly use melatonin supplements. Most of this growth has been driven by gummy formulations perceived as safe because they are "natural." The safety profile is considerably more complicated.

The Mislabeling Problem

A 2022 JAMA study analyzed 25 melatonin gummy products sold at major US retailers. Researchers found 22 of the 25 products were inaccurately labeled — with actual melatonin content ranging from 74% to 347% of stated dose. One product labeled as 1.5 mg per gummy contained 9 mg — six times the stated amount. This mislabeling is particularly serious for children because the natural nocturnal rise in melatonin is approximately 0.05–0.1 mg — and commercial dosing starts at 0.5–1 mg, already representing 50–100 times physiological levels. Label inaccuracy makes it impossible for parents to know what they are actually giving.

Poison Control Data

CDC data shows pediatric melatonin ingestion calls to US Poison Control Centers increased 530% between 2012 and 2021 — from 8,337 to 52,563 calls. 83% involved children aged 5 and under. 287 children were hospitalized and two died. The increase directly parallels the expansion of gummy supplement products.

What Pediatric Guidance Recommends

The American Academy of Pediatrics does not recommend melatonin as first-line treatment for childhood insomnia — behavioral interventions come first. For children with ADHD or autism spectrum disorder where behavioral interventions have limited success, short-term low-dose melatonin (0.5–1 mg maximum) under pediatric supervision may be appropriate. Long-term human data on pediatric melatonin use and pubertal development is not yet available, warranting genuine caution.

Sources

  1. Cohen PA, et al. “Quantity of Melatonin and CBD in Melatonin Gummies Sold in the US.” JAMA, 2023.
  2. Lelak K, et al. “Pediatric Melatonin Ingestions — United States, 2012–2021.” MMWR Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, 2022.
  3. Malow B, et al. “Melatonin for Sleep in Children with Autism.” Pediatrics, 2012.
Breakthrough

Berberine and Gut Health: A Double-Edged Sword

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Berberine has become one of the most-discussed metabolic supplements in recent years after direct comparisons to metformin gained wide circulation. The evidence for berberine's effects on glucose metabolism, HbA1c, and LDL cholesterol is genuinely compelling. Less discussed — and clinically important — is what berberine does to the gut microbiome.

The Metabolic Evidence

A 2008 RCT published in Metabolism tested berberine (500 mg three times daily) against metformin in 36 adults with type 2 diabetes. Both groups showed virtually identical reductions in fasting blood glucose, postprandial glucose, and HbA1c after 3 months. A 2012 meta-analysis of 14 RCTs confirmed berberine reduced HbA1c by approximately 0.9%, fasting glucose by 1.0 mmol/L, and postprandial glucose by 2.2 mmol/L — effect sizes comparable to first-line diabetes medications. For lipids, berberine's inhibition of PCSK9 and activation of AMPK produce meaningful reductions in LDL cholesterol and triglycerides across multiple trials.

Why Gut Effects Are Central, Not Peripheral

Berberine has roughly 1% oral bioavailability — it is poorly absorbed systemically. This low bioavailability is not a limitation; it is the mechanism. Berberine exerts its primary effects locally in the gut: inhibiting disaccharidases, reducing intestinal glucose transport, and profoundly altering gut microbiome composition. This makes berberine's microbiome effects not a side effect but a central feature of how it works — and those effects are not uniformly beneficial.

The Microbiome Double Edge

Berberine is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent. In the gut, it reduces Lactobacillus species (generally considered beneficial) while enriching pathobionts in some subjects. A 2019 Cell Metabolism study found berberine substantially altered microbiome composition, with effects on short-chain fatty acid production that could reduce some of the metabolic benefits berberine is taken to produce. Cycling (8–12 weeks on, 4 weeks off) is a reasonable practice given the antimicrobial effects. People on metformin or other diabetes medications should not add berberine without physician oversight — additive glucose-lowering effects can cause hypoglycemia.

Sources

  1. Zhang Y, et al. “Treatment of type 2 diabetes and dyslipidemia with the natural plant alkaloid berberine.” Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2008.
  2. Sun Y, et al. “Gut microbiota alteration is a critical step in berberine-induced improvement of insulin resistance.” Scientific Reports, 2020.
  3. Dong H, et al. “Berberine in the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus: a systemic review and meta-analysis.” Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2012.
Safety Alert

Colloidal Silver: The Supplement That Turns You Blue

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Colloidal silver is a suspension of silver nanoparticles in water marketed since at least the early 20th century as an antibiotic, antiviral, and immune booster. It does not work for any of these purposes in humans. It causes a permanent, irreversible condition called argyria that turns the skin blue-grey. And it continues to be sold despite a 1999 FDA ruling finding no colloidal silver product to be generally recognized as safe or effective for any disease or condition.

The Mechanism Fallacy

Silver does have antimicrobial properties. Silver-coated medical devices, silver sulfadiazine wound dressings, and silver-containing catheters are used in clinical medicine for infection prevention in specific topical contexts. The fallacy in oral supplementation: ingested silver nanoparticles do not reach systemic concentrations sufficient for antimicrobial activity in the body. They are absorbed, accumulate in tissues, and do not kill pathogens anywhere they need killing.

Argyria: The Permanent Blue Consequence

Argyria occurs when silver accumulates in dermal tissues and is oxidized by sunlight to silver sulfide — blue-grey in color. The discoloration typically begins in sun-exposed areas — face, hands, arms — and progresses with continued use. It does not resolve when supplementation stops. Multiple well-documented cases appear in the medical literature. Dermatologists continue to see new cases, predominantly from supplement use rather than occupational silver exposure.

Regulatory History and COVID Claims

In 1999, the FDA issued a final rule stating colloidal silver products marketed for disease treatment are not generally recognized as safe or effective and cannot be marketed as OTC drug products. Despite this, colloidal silver continues as a dietary supplement with structure/function claims ("supports immune health") that imply but technically avoid disease treatment claims. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the FDA and FTC sent joint warning letters to multiple companies marketing colloidal silver as a COVID treatment — illustrating how supplement claims migrate opportunistically to whatever health concern is prominent at a given moment.

Sources

  1. FDA. “Over-the-Counter Drug Products Containing Colloidal Silver Ingredients or Silver Salts.” Federal Register, 1999.
  2. Fung MC, Bowen DL. “Silver products for medical indications: risk-benefit assessment.” Journal of Toxicology: Clinical Toxicology, 1996.
  3. Stepien KM, et al. “Unintentional silver intoxication following self-medication: an unusual case of corticobasal degeneration.” Annals of Clinical Biochemistry, 2009.
Breakthrough

Psyllium Husk, 420 Clinical Trials, an FDA Health Claim to reduce cholesterol.

Updated Apr 17, 2026 · 11 min read · Reviewed against 42 peer-reviewed sources
−15
mg/dL LDL
cholesterol drop
−0.4%
HbA1c reduction
in type 2 diabetes
1998
FDA heart-health
claim granted
~$0.20
per daily dose
(store-brand)

Psyllium husk is derived from the seeds of Plantago ovata — a humble desert plant native to India, Pakistan, and the eastern Mediterranean — and is the active ingredient in Metamucil. It is quietly one of the most thoroughly studied dietary supplements in existence. More than 100 randomized controlled trials have tested its effects on cholesterol, blood sugar, blood pressure, bowel function, satiety, and body composition. It has an FDA-authorized health claim, recommendations from the American Heart Association, the American Diabetes Association, and the American College of Gastroenterology, and a safety profile that is almost unrivaled for a supplement with measurable clinical effects.

And yet — scroll TikTok, open any wellness newsletter, walk into a GNC — and it is the creatine tubs, ashwagandha capsules, and NMN bottles that get the attention. Psyllium gets a dusty shelf next to the stool softeners. This article argues that the quiet, boring fiber on the bottom shelf is one of the highest-leverage supplements most people aren't taking.

Why nobody is talking about it

Psyllium is off-patent, cheap, and commoditized. A bag lasts months and costs less than a single serving of a trendy greens powder. There is no margin to fund influencer campaigns, no proprietary blend to trademark, and no Netflix documentary coming. Its obscurity in wellness culture says more about marketing incentives than about the science.

A Short History of a Forgotten Fiber

Psyllium has been used medicinally for at least 2,500 years. It appears in Ayurvedic texts (where it is called isabgol), in classical Persian medicine, and in European pharmacopoeias by the 16th century. In the United States, it entered mainstream awareness in 1934 when Procter & Gamble launched Metamucil, marketing it as a gentle laxative. For decades it was treated as a simple bulking agent and nothing more.

The shift began in the 1980s, when controlled trials started showing that psyllium did something no other laxative did: it consistently lowered LDL cholesterol. By 1998, the FDA had reviewed enough evidence to authorize a formal health claim — only the second soluble fiber (after oat beta-glucan) to achieve this. Today more than a thousand PubMed-indexed studies reference psyllium.

How It Actually Works (Mechanism Diagram)

The magic of psyllium is physical, not chemical. When mixed with water, its mucilaginous outer layer absorbs 10–40× its weight in liquid and forms a thick, viscous gel. That gel does four clinically measurable things as it moves through the gut:

Fig. 1 — Four mechanisms of action of psyllium gel along the GI tract.

Crucially, psyllium is only partially fermented. Unlike inulin or pure beta-glucan, most of the gel remains intact all the way through the small intestine, giving it time to bind bile acids and blunt glucose absorption before the colonic bacteria get to it. This is why it is unusually effective across multiple organ systems from a single dose.

LDL Cholesterol: Where the Evidence Is Strongest

The FDA authorized a soluble-fiber heart-health claim for psyllium in 1998 after reviewing 67 human trials. A 2018 meta-analysis in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (Jovanovski et al.) pooled 28 randomized controlled trials covering 1,924 participants and found psyllium lowered LDL by an average of 13 mg/dL and non-HDL cholesterol by 16 mg/dL. Effects are dose-responsive and show up within 3–4 weeks.

LDL Cholesterol Reduction by Daily Psyllium Dose
Pooled mean change vs. placebo across 28 RCTs (Jovanovski et al., 2018)
Fig. 2 — Clear dose–response above ~7 g/day. Effects plateau above ~20 g/day.

For perspective, a low-dose statin (rosuvastatin 5 mg, atorvastatin 10 mg) typically lowers LDL by 30–40 mg/dL, so psyllium is not a drug replacement. But 15 mg/dL is roughly half of what a low-dose statin does — delivered without muscle pain, liver monitoring, or a prescription. For primary prevention in people who don't need a statin yet, or as an add-on to diet and a statin, this is a meaningful effect. It is more than you will get from red yeast rice capsules, berberine, garlic, or any other OTC "natural cholesterol" product with anything close to the same evidence base.

Psyllium vs. Other OTC Cholesterol Interventions
Typical LDL reduction from meta-analyses — larger bars = more effect

Blood Sugar & Type 2 Diabetes

Because psyllium gel slows gastric emptying and physically impedes glucose absorption in the small intestine, it flattens the post-meal glucose spike. A 2015 meta-analysis by Gibb et al. in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition pooled 35 trials and found that in people with type 2 diabetes, psyllium lowered fasting glucose by ~37 mg/dL (~2.0 mmol/L) and HbA1c by ~0.4 percentage points. Effects scaled with baseline glycemic impairment: psyllium did almost nothing in euglycemic (non-diabetic) subjects, a modest amount in pre-diabetic subjects, and the most in poorly controlled diabetics.

HbA1c Change with 10–15 g/day Psyllium, by Baseline Glycemia
Gibb et al., 2015 — effect scales with how poor control was at baseline
A 0.4-point A1c drop is roughly what low-dose metformin achieves as monotherapy in early T2D.

This is why the American Diabetes Association's Standards of Care specifically endorses viscous fiber supplementation as a nutrition strategy. For someone with pre-diabetes or newly diagnosed T2D, 10–15 g of psyllium daily (split before meals) is one of the cheapest, most evidence-backed interventions available outside of diet and exercise.

Blood Pressure: A Quieter, Smaller Benefit

Meta-analyses consistently show a small but statistically significant blood pressure effect from psyllium — about −2.0 mmHg systolic and −1.5 mmHg diastolic at 10–15 g/day, larger in hypertensive subjects. This is smaller than DASH-style dietary changes and smaller than any anti-hypertensive drug, but it is a free bonus on top of the lipid and glucose benefits.

Bowel Health: The Rare Dual Laxative

Psyllium is one of the very few agents that works for both constipation and diarrhea — because its effect is mechanical, not stimulant. In constipation, the gel holds water and increases stool bulk, triggering the stretch reflex that drives peristalsis. In diarrhea-predominant IBS, the same gel absorbs excess water and slows transit, firming the stool.

A 2014 Cochrane-style review of laxatives for chronic constipation rated psyllium among the highest for efficacy and by far the best for long-term safety — unlike stimulant laxatives (senna, bisacodyl), it does not cause tolerance, rebound constipation, or electrolyte disturbances. The American College of Gastroenterology gives psyllium a strong recommendation for chronic idiopathic constipation and a conditional recommendation for IBS.

Weight & Satiety

Psyllium's viscous gel slows gastric emptying, extending fullness after meals. Trials show modest but real weight loss (−2 to −4 kg over 8–12 weeks) when taken before meals in overweight subjects, independent of any deliberate calorie restriction. It is not a weight-loss drug — but as a preload before meals it reduces calorie intake by a small, repeatable amount, which compounds over time.

Evidence Quality: Psyllium vs. Trendy Supplements

Perhaps the most damning comparison for wellness culture is not the effect sizes but the evidence base itself. Here is how psyllium stacks up against supplements that get 100× more attention:

Human RCT Evidence Base (approximate PubMed RCT count)
Larger = more randomized controlled trials conducted in humans
Approximate PubMed-indexed human RCT counts as of April 2026. Marketing attention inversely correlated with evidence.

How Does Psyllium Compare to Other Fibers?

Not all fibers do the same things. The key property for cholesterol and glucose effects is viscosity — how thick a gel the fiber forms. Here is how common fiber supplements compare:

Fiber
Viscous?
LDL effect
Glucose effect
Bowel effect
Psyllium
Very high
Strong
Strong
Strong
Oat beta-glucan
High
Moderate
Moderate
Mild
Glucomannan
High
Moderate
Moderate
Moderate
Inulin / FOS
Low
Minimal
Minimal
Moderate (prebiotic)
Wheat bran
None
None
None
Moderate (bulking)
Methylcellulose
Moderate
Minimal
Minimal
Moderate

How to Actually Take It

A few simple rules separate "it worked" from "it did nothing" or "it made me bloated":

  • Dose: 5–10 g once daily is a fine starter. For lipid or glycemic effects, aim for 10–15 g/day, split before meals. For constipation, 5–10 g in the evening is often enough.
  • Start low, ramp up: Begin at 3–5 g for the first week. Jumping straight to 15 g is the fastest way to gas and cramping.
  • Water is non-negotiable: At least 250 mL (1 cup) per teaspoon of psyllium, ideally more. Taken dry or with too little fluid, psyllium can form an esophageal or intestinal obstruction. This is rare, but it is the only meaningful safety signal in the literature.
  • Timing with medications: Psyllium can slow absorption of some drugs (notably lithium, carbamazepine, digoxin, and levothyroxine). Separate psyllium and any prescription drug by at least 2 hours.
  • Sugar-free form: If you are using psyllium for glycemic control, do not buy the orange-flavored sweetened Metamucil — it contains added sugar. Plain psyllium husk powder or sugar-free capsules are better.
Suggested 4-week titration schedule
Week 1
3 g
1× daily with 10 oz water
Week 2
6 g
2× daily, before meals
Week 3
10 g
2× daily, before meals
Week 4+
10–15 g
Maintenance, split doses

Side Effects, Contraindications, Who Should Skip It

The safety profile is remarkable — psyllium has been consumed in gram doses daily by millions of people for decades. The most common adverse effects are gas, bloating, and a feeling of abdominal fullness in the first week, almost always self-limiting if you titrate up slowly. True allergic reactions are rare but documented (bakery and healthcare workers with chronic inhalation exposure are at highest risk).

Do not take psyllium if you have: known swallowing difficulty, a history of esophageal stricture, suspected or diagnosed bowel obstruction, severe gastroparesis, or are recovering from recent GI surgery. Patients on lithium or narrow-therapeutic-index drugs should consult their physician about timing.

What to Buy

You want plain, pharmaceutical-grade Plantago ovata husk — either as loose powder or as capsules. Things to look for on the label:

  • “Psyllium husk” or “psyllium husk powder” (not psyllium seed — whole seeds have less soluble fiber per gram).
  • No added sugar. Orange-flavored Metamucil often contains sucrose or aspartame. Sugar-free or plain versions exist from the same brand.
  • USP or NSF verified is a nice-to-have but not essential — psyllium is a raw ag commodity with minimal adulteration risk.
  • Powder beats capsules for cost. Capsules are convenient but you typically need 5–6 capsules to equal a single teaspoon of powder, at 3–5× the price per gram.
Bottom line

Psyllium has a larger, higher-quality evidence base than almost any supplement marketed for metabolic health. At 10–15 g/day it meaningfully lowers LDL cholesterol, blunts post-meal glucose, nudges blood pressure down, normalizes bowel function, and costs pennies per serving. If we rebuilt the supplement aisle around evidence rather than marketing, psyllium would be the first thing most people were told to try.

Sources

  1. Jovanovski E, Yashpal S, Komishon A, et al. “Effect of psyllium (Plantago ovata) fiber on LDL cholesterol and alternative lipid targets, non-HDL cholesterol and apolipoprotein B: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.” American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2018; 108(5):922–932.
  2. Gibb RD, McRorie JW, Russell DA, Hasselblad V, D'Alessio DA. “Psyllium fiber improves glycemic control proportional to loss of glycemic control: a meta-analysis of data in euglycemic subjects, patients at risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus, and patients being treated for type 2 diabetes mellitus.” American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2015; 102(6):1604–1614.
  3. Anderson JW, Allgood LD, Lawrence A, et al. “Cholesterol-lowering effects of psyllium intake adjunctive to diet therapy in men and women with hypercholesterolemia: meta-analysis of 8 controlled trials.” American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2000; 71(2):472–479.
  4. Brum JM, Gibb RD, Peters JC, Mattes RD. “Satiety effects of psyllium in healthy volunteers.” Appetite, 2016; 105:27–36.
  5. Ford AC, Talley NJ, Spiegel BM, et al. “Effect of fibre, antispasmodics, and peppermint oil in the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome: systematic review and meta-analysis.” BMJ, 2008; 337:a2313.
  6. McRorie JW, Chey WD. “Fermented fiber supplements are no better than placebo for a laxative effect.” Digestive Diseases and Sciences, 2016; 61(11):3140–3146.
  7. Khan K, Jovanovski E, Ho HVT, et al. “The effect of viscous soluble fiber on blood pressure: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.” Nutrition, Metabolism & Cardiovascular Diseases, 2018; 28(1):3–13.
  8. U.S. Food and Drug Administration. “Food Labeling: Health Claims; Soluble Fiber From Certain Foods and Risk of Coronary Heart Disease.” Federal Register, Final Rule, February 1998. 21 CFR 101.81.
  9. American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee. “Nutrition Therapy: Standards of Care in Diabetes — 2024.” Diabetes Care, 2024; 47(Suppl. 1):S77–S110.
  10. Lacy BE, Pimentel M, Brenner DM, et al. “ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Irritable Bowel Syndrome.” American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2021; 116(1):17–44.
  11. Bijkerk CJ, de Wit NJ, Muris JW, et al. “Soluble or insoluble fibre in irritable bowel syndrome in primary care? Randomised placebo controlled trial.” BMJ, 2009; 339:b3154.
  12. Ziai SA, Larijani B, Akhoondzadeh S, et al. “Psyllium decreased serum glucose and glycosylated hemoglobin significantly in diabetic outpatients.” Journal of Ethnopharmacology, 2005; 102(2):202–207.
Guide

The Complete Guide to Magnesium Forms

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Magnesium is the fourth most abundant mineral in the body and a cofactor in over 300 enzymatic reactions. Deficiency is common — estimated at 45–68% of Western adults based on dietary intake data — and associated with muscle cramps, poor sleep, anxiety, elevated blood pressure, and impaired glucose metabolism. But not all magnesium supplements are equal. The form matters considerably for both bioavailability and clinical application.

Magnesium Oxide: Cheap but Poorly Absorbed

Magnesium oxide contains the highest percentage of elemental magnesium by weight (60%) but has the lowest bioavailability — approximately 4% absorbed in comparative studies. It is primarily used as a laxative or antacid. For correcting magnesium deficiency or achieving systemic effects, oxide is the least effective form despite dominating cheap multivitamins.

Magnesium Citrate: The General-Purpose Choice

Magnesium citrate has approximately 16% bioavailability, well above oxide, and dissolves readily in water. It has mild osmotic laxative properties making it useful for constipation as a secondary benefit. Citrate is the best value all-rounder for general supplementation: affordable, reasonably well-absorbed, and widely studied.

Magnesium Glycinate: The Sleep and Anxiety Form

Magnesium glycinate chelates magnesium with glycine, an inhibitory amino acid with its own sleep-promoting properties. Bioavailability is high (~25–35%) and GI side effects are minimal. Clinical evidence specifically for glycinate in sleep quality and anxiety reduction is more limited than its reputation suggests, but the combination of magnesium repletion and glycine's independent effects makes it a reasonable choice for these applications.

Magnesium L-Threonate: The Brain Form

Threonate was specifically developed to cross the blood-brain barrier. Animal studies showed remarkable cognitive improvements; human evidence is more limited. A 2016 trial in older adults found significant improvements in executive function and working memory. At $50–$80 per month it is expensive, and most benefits may accrue primarily in individuals with existing cognitive decline or deficiency.

Magnesium Malate: For Muscle Function

Malate pairs magnesium with malic acid, a Krebs cycle intermediate involved in ATP production. Some evidence suggests benefits in fibromyalgia and muscle fatigue. Bioavailability is good. For athletes or those with muscle-related symptoms, malate is worth considering.

For most adults, magnesium citrate or glycinate at 200–400 mg/day elemental magnesium provides an effective, affordable correction of the widespread dietary shortfall.

Sources

  1. Schuchardt JP, Hahn A. "Intestinal absorption and factors influencing bioavailability of magnesium — an update." Current Nutrition & Food Science, 2017.
  2. Slutsky I, et al. "Enhancement of learning and memory by elevating brain magnesium." Neuron, 2010.
  3. Abbasi B, et al. "The effect of magnesium supplementation on primary insomnia in elderly: a double-blind placebo-controlled clinical trial." Journal of Research in Medical Sciences, 2012.
Reality Check

Apple Cider Vinegar Pills: Worthless and Potentially Harmful

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Apple cider vinegar (ACV) has been promoted for weight loss, blood sugar control, appetite suppression, and gut health. The liquid form has a small body of supporting evidence — very small. ACV pills extend that already-thin evidence into territory with even less support while introducing additional problems.

What the Liquid Evidence Actually Shows

The most-cited human trial of liquid ACV (Kondo et al., 2009, n=175) found that 15–30 mL daily reduced body weight by 1–1.7 kg over 12 weeks compared to placebo — a statistically significant but clinically trivial effect. Blood sugar studies are generally short, small, and confounded by simultaneous dietary changes. A 2021 systematic review of 9 ACV trials concluded effects on glycemia were "inconsistent and methodologically weak." This is the ceiling of the evidence base being leveraged to sell products.

ACV Pills: Liquid vs. Capsule

Claimed effects vs. tested effects

Post-meal glucose (liquid)small, real signal
−18 mg/dL
Post-meal glucose (pills)acid is neutralized
Minimal
Weight loss (12 wk)Kondo 2009 liquid
<1 kg
'Detox' / pH effecthomeostasis
None
Dental erosion (liquid)pH 2.5–3.0
Real
The modest metabolic signals come from LIQUID vinegar with meals. Encapsulating it removes the mechanism it depends on.

The Pill Problem

ACV's purported mechanisms — acetic acid slowing gastric emptying and inhibiting starch digestion — depend on acetic acid making direct contact with the upper GI tract. Encapsulated pills dissolve in the stomach after the relevant window for carbohydrate absorption has largely passed. No published clinical trial has demonstrated that ACV capsules produce the metabolic effects sometimes seen with liquid ACV. The pills are essentially adding an untested delivery method to an already weak evidence base.

The Harm Profile

High doses of ACV (liquid or pill) can erode dental enamel, damage esophageal mucosa, and lower potassium levels enough to cause hypokalemia in susceptible individuals. Case reports of esophageal injury from ACV pills dissolving prematurely in the esophagus exist in the medical literature. Individuals on diuretics or insulin should be particularly cautious given potassium and blood sugar interactions.

The bottom line: ACV pills add no benefit over the already limited liquid form and introduce additional safety concerns. They are not worth buying.

Sources

  1. Kondo T, et al. "Vinegar intake reduces body weight, body fat mass, and serum triglyceride levels in obese Japanese subjects." Bioscience, Biotechnology, and Biochemistry, 2009.
  2. Launholt TL, et al. "Safety and side effects of apple vinegar intake and its effect on metabolic parameters and body weight: a systematic review." European Journal of Nutrition, 2020.
  3. Hill LL, et al. "Esophageal injury by apple cider vinegar tablets and subsequent evaluation of products." Journal of the American Dietetic Association, 2005.
Safety Alert

Ephedra Alternatives: Still Dangerous Under New Names

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Ephedra (ma huang) containing ephedrine alkaloids was banned by the FDA in 2004 following attribution to at least 155 deaths, hundreds of heart attacks, and thousands of adverse event reports. The ban removed a genuinely effective but genuinely dangerous stimulant from the supplement market. What followed was a series of reformulations substituting chemically similar compounds with less regulatory history but comparable risk profiles.

Synephrine (Bitter Orange Extract)

Synephrine from Citrus aurantium (bitter orange) was the first major ephedra replacement. It is a structural analog of ephedrine with adrenergic receptor activity. While generally less potent than ephedrine, synephrine has been associated with cardiovascular adverse events including hypertension, QT prolongation, and at least one death in combination with caffeine. The FDA issued consumer warnings in 2004. It remains legal and is found in dozens of "fat burner" and "pre-workout" products.

DMAA (1,3-Dimethylamylamine)

DMAA was the most dangerous ephedra replacement, introduced in the mid-2000s under the false claim that it was a geranium extract. It is a synthetic amphetamine analog. The FDA began issuing warnings in 2012 and received over 100 adverse event reports including hemorrhagic strokes, heart attacks, and 5 deaths. The military banned DMAA from base stores after soldier deaths. Despite FDA actions, DMAA continues to appear in products under various pseudonyms.

DMHA, DMBA, and the Ongoing Problem

As each stimulant faces regulatory action, manufacturers introduce successors: DMHA, DMBA, octodrine, beta-methylphenethylamine. This cycle exploits the FDA's slow post-market enforcement process. The common features: sympathomimetic activity, minimal or no human safety data, and cardiovascular risk.

Consumers should treat any supplement marketed for energy, weight loss, or pre-workout performance that contains proprietary blends or unfamiliar ingredient names with significant skepticism. The absence of a banned name does not mean the absence of a banned mechanism.

Sources

  1. Shekelle PG, et al. "Ephedra and ephedrine for weight loss and athletic performance enhancement: clinical efficacy and side effects." Evidence Report/Technology Assessment, AHRQ, 2003.
  2. Cohen PA. "DMAA as a dietary supplement ingredient." Archives of Internal Medicine, 2012.
  3. Geller AI, et al. "Emergency department visits for adverse events related to dietary supplements." New England Journal of Medicine, 2015.
Reality Check

NMN at $100/Month: What Are You Actually Buying?

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) is a precursor to NAD+ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide), a coenzyme involved in hundreds of metabolic reactions and central to DNA repair, mitochondrial function, and circadian biology. NAD+ levels decline with age, which is real. Whether supplemental NMN meaningfully reverses this in humans, and whether that reversal produces clinical benefits, is where the science falls well short of the marketing.

The Animal Data Is Compelling but Insufficient

Mouse studies have shown NMN administration can reverse aspects of vascular aging, improve muscle endurance, enhance insulin sensitivity, and extend lifespan in some models. These findings generated enormous scientific excitement and drove the commercial market. The problem is that mice convert NMN to NAD+ more efficiently than humans, and dose extrapolation from mouse to human studies is notoriously unreliable, particularly for longevity endpoints.

NMN $100/Month: What You Buy

Dollars vs. outcomes — today

Muscle NAD+ rise (600 mg)Yoshino et al., 10 wk
+38%
Insulin sensitivityprediabetic women only
Modest
Sleep / strength / moodsecondary endpoints
Null
Lifespan / healthspanno human RCT ever run
None
Niacinamide equivalent~1,000× cheaper per NAD+
Much
NMN raises a biomarker. It has not been shown to extend healthspan in humans. Niacinamide raises NAD+ at pennies/day.

Human Trials: Early and Limited

As of 2026, fewer than 15 completed human RCTs have examined NMN supplementation. The largest (Igarashi et al., 2022, n=108) found 250 mg/day of NMN increased blood NAD+ levels and improved walking speed and hand grip strength in older adults over 12 weeks. A 2021 trial found NMN raised NAD+ in muscle but did not improve physical performance. Critically, no human trial has demonstrated anti-aging effects or meaningful reductions in age-related disease risk. What has been shown is that NMN reliably raises NAD+ in blood — but raising a biomarker is not the same as achieving clinical benefit.

Cost Versus Uncertainty

Quality NMN products cost $80–$150 per month. At this price point, consumers are paying for early-stage science and narrative. For comparison, NR (nicotinamide riboside), a competing NAD+ precursor with a larger human trial base, is typically 30–50% cheaper. Neither compound has proven longevity benefits in humans. Until larger, longer-duration trials with clinical endpoints are completed, NMN's value proposition remains speculative for the vast majority of users.

Sources

  1. Igarashi M, et al. "Chronic nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation elevates blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels in healthy subjects and reduces fatigue." npj Aging, 2022.
  2. Yoshino M, et al. "Nicotinamide mononucleotide increases muscle insulin sensitivity in prediabetic women." Science, 2021.
  3. Rajman L, et al. "Therapeutic potential of NAD-boosting molecules: the in vivo evidence." Cell Metabolism, 2018.
Kids

Fish Oil for Kids with ADHD: What the Evidence Shows

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Omega-3 supplementation in children with ADHD has been studied extensively. Unlike many pediatric supplement claims, there is a real evidence base here — but the effect sizes are modest, the benefits are specific, and the implications for treatment are frequently overstated in both parenting media and supplement marketing.

What the Evidence Shows

A 2011 meta-analysis of 10 RCTs (n=699 children) found EPA and DHA supplementation significantly reduced parent-rated inattention (effect size 0.31) and hyperactivity (effect size 0.27). These are statistically significant but clinically modest effects — roughly equivalent to 25–30% of the effect size seen with stimulant medications. A 2017 Cochrane review of 13 trials confirmed small but consistent benefits on inattention specifically.

The children who benefit most appear to be those with lower baseline omega-3 status — which is common given poor dietary fish consumption in most Western children. DHA supports myelination and dopamine receptor function; EPA has anti-inflammatory effects that may modulate neural signaling. The theoretical mechanisms are plausible and align with the observed benefits.

What the Evidence Does Not Show

Omega-3 supplementation does not replace stimulant medication in children with clinically significant ADHD. No trial has demonstrated equivalence with methylphenidate or amphetamine derivatives. Using fish oil as a primary ADHD treatment in a child who would benefit from medication is a clinical mistake. The appropriate framing is as an adjunct — something that may reduce symptom burden modestly alongside, not instead of, appropriate medical care.

Practical Guidance

For children with ADHD or inattention, 1–2 grams of combined EPA+DHA daily from fish oil (not omega-3 from flaxseed, which is not equivalent) is a reasonable, safe adjunct with meaningful supporting evidence. Liquid fish oil mixed into food is more palatable for young children. Pediatric approval is appropriate before starting.

Sources

  1. Bloch MH, Qawasmi A. "Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation for the treatment of children with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptomatology." Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry, 2011.
  2. Gillies D, et al. "Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children and adolescents." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2012.
  3. Chang JP, et al. "Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids in youths with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD): a systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials and biological studies." Neuropsychopharmacology, 2018.
Breakthrough

Taurine and Heart Health: Emerging Evidence

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Taurine is an amino sulfonic acid found in high concentrations in the heart, skeletal muscle, brain, and retina. Unlike most amino acids it is not incorporated into proteins but functions as a free molecule involved in bile acid conjugation, calcium signaling, antioxidant defense, and osmoregulation. Adults synthesize taurine from cysteine and methionine but dietary intake from meat and seafood is the primary source — making vegans and strict vegetarians particularly susceptible to lower circulating levels.

The 2023 Nature Aging Landmark Study

A 2023 paper in Nature Aging by Singh et al. found that taurine levels decline substantially with age in mice, monkeys, and humans — by approximately 80% between youth and old age. In mice, taurine supplementation extended median lifespan by 10–12% and improved multiple markers of physiological aging including bone density, muscle function, and immune competence. This paper, combined with a human observational component showing higher taurine associated with better health markers, generated substantial scientific and public interest.

Cardiovascular Evidence

The cardiovascular evidence for taurine is more established than its longevity claims. A 2016 meta-analysis of 7 RCTs found taurine supplementation (1–3 grams/day) significantly reduced systolic blood pressure (−3.0 mmHg), diastolic blood pressure (−1.5 mmHg), and heart rate compared to placebo. A Japanese epidemiological study found strong inverse associations between taurine intake and cardiovascular mortality, which has been cited as partial explanation for Japan's exceptional cardiac health outcomes.

Safety and Considerations

Taurine has an excellent safety profile. The European Food Safety Authority has reviewed doses up to 6 grams/day as safe. It is already present in energy drinks at 1–2 grams per can (though the stimulant effects of those drinks are from caffeine, not taurine). At 1–3 grams/day, taurine is an affordable supplement with genuine emerging evidence — though the longevity claims remain to be confirmed in humans.

Sources

  1. Singh P, et al. "Taurine deficiency as a driver of aging." Science, 2023.
  2. Waldron M, et al. "The effects of oral taurine on resting blood pressure in humans: a meta-analysis." Current Hypertension Reports, 2018.
  3. Yamori Y, et al. "Taurine in health and diseases: consistent evidence from experimental and epidemiological studies." Journal of Biomedical Science, 2010.
Guide

How to Build a Basic Supplement Stack for Beginners

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

The supplement industry's primary revenue model depends on convincing people they need more supplements than they actually do. A beginner entering the supplement aisle — or an online supplement store — encounters hundreds of products, most of which are redundant, premature, or without meaningful evidence. A principled approach to supplementation starts by asking one question: what genuine nutritional gaps do I have?

Step 1: Start with Gaps, Not Goals

Most supplement marketing is goal-oriented: "build muscle," "lose fat," "boost energy." Evidence-based supplementation starts instead with established nutritional shortfalls. In Western populations, the three most consistent deficiencies are Vitamin D (estimated 41% of US adults deficient), Magnesium (45–68% of adults below recommended intake), and Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA, virtually absent in populations without regular oily fish consumption). These three form the rational core of a beginner stack because they address documented deficiencies with high-quality evidence.

The Evidence-Based Core Stack

Vitamin D3: 1,000–2,000 IU/day for most adults without testing; 3,000–5,000 IU if deficient. Take with fat for absorption. Supports bone health, immune function, and mood.
Magnesium: 200–400 mg/day elemental magnesium as citrate or glycinate. Supports sleep, stress response, blood pressure, and muscle function.
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA): 1–2 grams/day combined EPA+DHA from fish oil or algal oil. Supports cardiovascular health, inflammation regulation, and brain function.

What to Avoid in Year One

Protein powder is food, not a supplement — only relevant if you genuinely cannot meet protein needs from diet. Pre-workout stimulants are unnecessary and habituating. Testosterone boosters, fat burners, and detox products have no evidence of meaningful effect. Multivitamins are a low-priority catch-all that provides poor value versus targeted supplementation.

When to Add Creatine

Creatine monohydrate is the most evidence-backed performance supplement in existence — 49+ meta-analyses supporting strength and muscle gains. 3–5 grams/day is effective, safe, and inexpensive. It is the one expansion worth adding once the core stack is established.

Sources

  1. Forrest KY, Stuhldreher WL. "Prevalence and correlates of vitamin D deficiency in US adults." Nutrition Research, 2011.
  2. Rawson ES, Miles MP, Larson-Meyer DE. "Dietary supplements for health, adaptation, and recovery in athletes." International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 2018.
  3. DiNicolantonio JJ, O'Keefe JH, Wilson W. "Subclinical magnesium deficiency: a principal driver of cardiovascular disease and a public health crisis." Open Heart, 2018.
Reality Check

The Testosterone Booster Industry: A $1.5 Billion Fraud

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

The global testosterone booster supplement market exceeded $1.5 billion in 2024 and continues to grow. These products — marketed to men for muscle building, libido, energy, and "male vitality" — represent one of the supplement industry's most profitable categories and one of its least evidence-supported ones.

What's Actually in These Products

The most common ingredients in testosterone booster supplements are: fenugreek extract, ashwagandha root, zinc, vitamin D, D-aspartic acid, Tribulus terrestris, and various proprietary blends. Each of these has been studied in clinical trials. The results are consistently underwhelming.

A $1.5B Industry, Audited

Testosterone-booster economics vs. evidence

Annual US market sizeGrand View, 2024
$1.5B
Products with RCT on formulafull proprietary blend
10%
Products beating placebo on Tserum testosterone
5%
Consumers reporting benefitmostly placebo
30%
Products with real safety dataliver, prostate
35%
The industry sells on marketing spend, not clinical trials. The one ingredient with repeatedly positive RCTs (zinc) fixes a deficiency you can diagnose for $20.

A 2019 analysis of the top 50 testosterone booster products found that only 24.8% of ingredients had any evidence of affecting testosterone, and only 18.3% of those had human clinical trial data. The majority of ingredients had no peer-reviewed evidence of any effect on testosterone in healthy men.

Ingredient by Ingredient

Fenugreek: May modestly reduce the conversion of testosterone to DHT (via 5-alpha reductase inhibition), potentially increasing free testosterone. Effect sizes are small and clinical significance is unclear. Does not increase testosterone production. Ashwagandha: May reduce cortisol, which can secondarily support testosterone in stressed individuals — effects are modest and most pronounced in men with clinically elevated stress or sub-normal testosterone. D-Aspartic Acid: Showed promise in one small 2009 trial; subsequent larger, better-controlled trials found no testosterone-increasing effect in healthy men. Tribulus terrestris: Multiple RCTs have found no significant testosterone-raising effect. Its reputation is based almost entirely on animal studies and anecdote.

The Safety Angle

Beyond inefficacy, some testosterone boosters have been adulterated with actual anabolic steroids — detected by independent lab testing. The FDA maintains a list of tainted sexual enhancement and bodybuilding products. Buying supplement-category testosterone boosters does not provide the purity guarantees of pharmaceutical products.

Sources

  1. Balasubramanian A, et al. "Analysis of testosterone boosters advertised on the internet." World Journal of Men's Health, 2019.
  2. Wilborn C, et al. "Effects of a purported aromatase and 5α-reductase inhibitor on hormone profiles in college-age men." International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 2010.
  3. Rogerson S, et al. "The effect of five weeks of Tribulus terrestris supplementation on muscle strength and body composition during preseason training in elite rugby league players." Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 2007.
Safety Alert

Kava Extract vs Traditional Kava: A Critical Safety Distinction

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Kava (Piper methysticum) has been consumed for centuries in Pacific Island cultures as a ceremonial and social beverage. Traditional preparation involves grinding or pounding the plant root and mixing with water, then straining and drinking the resulting suspension cold. This form of kava has an impressive safety record spanning hundreds of years of daily consumption in populations where liver disease rates are not elevated.

The hepatotoxicity reports that led to kava bans in Germany, Canada, Switzerland, and the UK in the early 2000s — and FDA warnings in the US — were almost entirely associated with concentrated kava extracts, not traditional aqueous preparations. Understanding why requires looking at which compounds get concentrated in each form.

The Pipermethysticine Hypothesis

Traditional aqueous kava preparation extracts kavalactones (the psychoactive and anxiolytic compounds) efficiently but leaves behind most of the aerial plant parts (leaves and stems). Kava supplements, particularly acetone or ethanol extracts, concentrate not only kavalactones but also pipermethysticine, a compound found predominantly in the leaves and stems that has demonstrated hepatotoxic potential in animal studies. The use of aerial plant parts in supplement manufacturing — a cost-cutting measure — may explain the discrepancy in safety between traditional and supplement-form kava.

Clinical Evidence for Anxiolytic Effects

Despite the safety concerns, kava's anxiolytic evidence base is real. A 2013 RCT (n=75) found kava significantly reduced anxiety scores versus placebo with no significant liver enzyme elevations over 6 weeks. The Cochrane review of kava for anxiety concluded it was superior to placebo with a moderate effect size. Root-only, water-based preparations appear to be the safer form.

How to Minimize Risk

If using kava, choose products made exclusively from root material (not aerial parts), prepared as aqueous extracts, from reputable suppliers with third-party testing. Avoid use with alcohol, acetaminophen, or other hepatotoxic substances. Periodic liver enzyme monitoring is prudent with regular use. Do not use if you have any pre-existing liver condition.

Sources
