  1. Sarris J, et al. "Kava: a comprehensive review of efficacy, safety, and psychopharmacology." Australian & New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry, 2011.
  2. Teschke R, Sarris J, Lebot V. "Contaminant hepatotoxins as culprits for kava hepatotoxicity — fact or fiction?" Phytotherapy Research, 2013.
  3. Pittler MH, Ernst E. "Kava extract versus placebo for treating anxiety." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2003.
Reality Check

Urolithin A: The Next NAD+?

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Urolithin A is a metabolite produced by gut bacteria from polyphenols found in pomegranates, berries, and walnuts. It has attracted significant scientific interest for its ability to activate mitophagy — the cellular process of clearing dysfunctional mitochondria — which declines with age and may contribute to muscle aging, reduced energy production, and metabolic dysfunction.

The Science Is Genuinely Interesting

The mitophagy angle is a legitimate biological pathway. A 2019 clinical trial in older adults (n=66) found urolithin A supplementation increased muscle mitochondrial biogenesis markers and exercise capacity compared to placebo — results published in Nature Metabolism and well-received by the research community. A 2022 randomized trial found urolithin A (1,000 mg/day) improved skeletal muscle strength and endurance in older adults after 4 months. These are real findings from well-designed trials.

Urolithin A: What We Actually Know

Mitopure/Amazentis trial data, in plain numbers

Mitophagy gene expressionmuscle biopsy signal
Moderate
Muscle endurance (old mice)animal, impressive
Moderate
Muscle strength (humans)modest 12% in elderly
Small
Lifespan (any species)not demonstrated
None
Pomegranate juice alternativecheaper natural source
Yes
A 16-ounce glass of pomegranate juice contains enough precursor for most people's gut microbiome to produce urolithin A.

The Hype Gets Ahead of the Evidence

The problem is the gap between what has been shown and what is claimed. Marketing for urolithin A products describes it as an "anti-aging supplement," "longevity molecule," and "mitochondria recharger" — framing that vastly overstates what two short-duration trials in older adults can support. Urolithin A has not been shown to extend human lifespan, prevent any age-related disease, or produce benefits in younger, healthy adults. The trials to date have been conducted primarily in people 65+ with reduced exercise capacity.

The Cost Problem

Leading urolithin A products (notably Mitopure) cost $60–$100 per month for doses used in clinical trials. Crucially, only 30–40% of people are "Urolithin A producers" — individuals with the specific gut microbiome composition to convert polyphenols into urolithin A naturally. The remainder would not benefit from dietary sources and might benefit from supplementation, but there are no cheap tests to determine which group you fall into.

Urolithin A is promising science in a specific population. It is not a universal longevity supplement at current evidence levels.

Sources

  1. Liu S, et al. "Urolithin A improves muscle strength, exercise performance, and biomarkers of mitochondrial health in a randomized trial in middle-aged adults." Cell Reports Medicine, 2022.
  2. Andreux PA, et al. "The mitophagy activator urolithin A is safe and induces a molecular signature of improved mitochondrial and cellular health in humans." Nature Metabolism, 2019.
  3. Singh A, et al. "Direct supplementation with Urolithin A overcomes limitations of dietary exposure and gut microbiome variability in healthy adults to achieve consistent levels across the population." European Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2022.
Kids

Vitamin Gummies for Kids: Sugar, Dosing, and Better Alternatives

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Gummy vitamins for children are the most popular pediatric supplement format globally, generating over $6 billion in annual sales. Their appeal is obvious: children who refuse pills or chewables will enthusiastically eat what is effectively candy. But the format comes with significant trade-offs that parents are rarely told about when purchasing them.

The Sugar Problem

A typical children's gummy vitamin contains 2–5 grams of sugar per serving — comparable to a small piece of candy. For a daily vitamin, this amounts to 730–1,825 grams of supplemental sugar per year — roughly equivalent to 3–7 pounds of added sugar added to a child's diet annually for no nutritional benefit beyond the vitamins (which could be delivered without sugar). Given that the American Heart Association recommends children under 6 consume no more than 25 grams of added sugar daily, and children 6–12 no more than 25 grams, a daily gummy vitamin representing 5–20% of this budget deserves consideration.

Dosing Inconsistency

ConsumerLab testing has repeatedly found gummy vitamins to be among the least accurately dosed supplement formats. The gummy manufacturing process — mixing vitamins into a gelatin matrix — results in uneven distribution, and the sticky texture causes vitamins to degrade faster than in tablets or capsules. Multiple products have been found to contain 20–50% less of the labeled ingredient than claimed after several months of storage.

Better Alternatives

Chewable tablets offer better dosing accuracy and no added sugar in most formulations. Dissolvable powders are effective for younger children who cannot chew tablets. For children who only accept gummies, sugar-free gummy formulations using xylitol or erythritol exist — though xylitol should be kept strictly away from pets. The specific vitamins worth giving children depend on diet and geography; Vitamin D is universally under-supplemented in northern latitudes, and iron is a common deficiency in toddlers. Consult a pediatrician for targeted guidance.

Sources

  1. ConsumerLab.com. "Multivitamins and Gummy Vitamin Review." Annual supplement testing report, 2024.
  2. American Heart Association. "Added Sugars." AHA Dietary Guidelines for Children, 2023.
  3. Ufer M, et al. "Assessing the actual content of vitamin supplements: results of a quality surveillance study." European Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 2020.
Breakthrough

Glycine for Sleep: Cheap, Safe, and Surprisingly Effective

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Glycine is the simplest amino acid, present in abundance in collagen and gelatin, and is one of the cheapest bulk amino acids available — typically under $15 for a 500-gram supply. Despite its low cost and favorable evidence profile for sleep improvement, it receives comparatively little attention in wellness media relative to far more expensive and less-studied alternatives like lion's mane or ashwagandha.

The Clinical Evidence

A series of well-designed RCTs from Japanese researchers (primarily Inagawa and Bannai et al.) established glycine's sleep effects with reasonable rigor. The key findings: 3 grams of glycine taken before bed significantly reduced time to sleep onset, improved subjective sleep quality, reduced fatigue the following morning, and improved performance on cognitive tests after sleep restriction — all compared to placebo. Sleep architecture studies showed glycine increased REM sleep duration and reduced core body temperature, which is consistent with its proposed mechanism.

Mechanism

Glycine acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the spinal cord and brainstem (via glycine receptors) and as a co-agonist at NMDA glutamate receptors. The sleep-promoting effects appear to involve glycine-mediated thermoregulation: glycine causes peripheral vasodilation, accelerating heat loss from the extremities and lowering core body temperature, which is a key physiological signal for sleep onset. This mechanism is distinct from sedatives, melatonin, or antihistamines and does not impair cognitive function or create dependence.

Practical Use

3 grams of glycine powder dissolved in water 30–60 minutes before bed is the protocol used in the clinical trials. Glycine has a mildly sweet taste, making it more palatable than most amino acid powders. It can be mixed into herbal tea or water. The safety profile is excellent — no adverse effects at clinical doses, no interactions with common medications, and no risk of dependence or tolerance. For anyone with sleep difficulties who has not tried glycine, it is arguably the most undervalued first-line option available.

Sources

  1. Bannai M, Kawai N. "New therapeutic strategy for amino acid medicine: glycine improves the quality of sleep." Journal of Pharmacological Sciences, 2012.
  2. Inagawa K, et al. "Subjective effects of glycine ingestion before the sleep period on sleep quality." Sleep and Biological Rhythms, 2006.
  3. Kawai N, et al. "The sleep-promoting and hypothermic effects of glycine are mediated by NMDA receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus." Neuropsychopharmacology, 2015.
Guide

Supplement Timing: Does It Actually Matter?

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Supplement timing is one of the more aggressively marketed aspects of sports nutrition. The concept of "anabolic windows," optimal dosing times, and strategic cycling has generated enormous product category proliferation — pre-workout, intra-workout, post-workout, morning, and evening formulations. The actual evidence for most of this precision is considerably thinner than the marketing implies.

Where Timing Genuinely Matters

Caffeine: Timing matters significantly. Taking caffeine within 90 minutes of the intended performance window (exercise or cognitive work) is necessary for peak effect. Consuming caffeine too early in the day can also disrupt sleep if half-life is not accounted for (half-life is 5–6 hours in most adults).

Creatine: Minor evidence favoring post-workout timing for muscle gains, but the difference versus other timing is small. Daily consistency matters far more than specific timing.

Vitamin D and fat-soluble vitamins (A, E, K): Should be taken with a meal containing fat for optimal absorption. Timing relative to time of day is largely irrelevant.

Magnesium and glycine for sleep: Evening timing is meaningful when the goal is sleep improvement, as both have timing-dependent mechanisms.

Iron: Best absorbed on an empty stomach. Taking with vitamin C enhances absorption; coffee and calcium reduce it within the same meal.

Where Timing Is Largely Irrelevant

For most fat-soluble vitamins (beyond fat co-ingestion), probiotics, fish oil, B vitamins, and zinc, the research shows daily consistency matters but precise timing within the day does not produce meaningfully different outcomes. The "post-workout window" for protein consumption was overstated in earlier research — total daily protein intake, not timing, is the primary driver of muscle protein synthesis.

Practical Rule

Take supplements at a time you will consistently remember. For most people, anchoring supplements to a meal is the most effective strategy — both for absorption (for fat-soluble compounds) and for compliance. The marginal benefit of optimized timing is dwarfed by the benefit of consistent daily use.

Sources

  1. Schoenfeld BJ, Aragon AA. "Is there a postworkout anabolic window of opportunity for nutrient consumption?" Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy, 2013.
  2. Antonio J, Ciccone V. "The effects of pre versus post workout supplementation of creatine monohydrate on body composition and strength." Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition, 2013.
  3. Cajochen C, et al. "Evening exposure to a light-emitting diodes (LED)-backlit computer screen affects circadian physiology and cognitive performance." Journal of Applied Physiology, 2011.
Reality Check

Homeopathic Supplements: Why They Cannot Work

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Homeopathy is a 200-year-old system developed by Samuel Hahnemann based on two principles: "like cures like" (a substance that causes symptoms in healthy people cures similar symptoms in the sick) and the "law of infinitesimals" (extreme dilution makes a substance more potent rather than less). Both principles contradict fundamental chemistry, physics, and pharmacology. Understanding why homeopathy cannot work is not a matter of ideology but of basic science.

The Dilution Problem

Homeopathic remedies are typically sold at potencies like 30C or 200C. A 30C dilution means the original substance has been diluted 1:100 thirty times in succession. The resulting dilution factor is 10−60 — a number so small that a 30C remedy made from a substance would contain, statistically, not a single molecule of the original ingredient in the entire observable universe's worth of water. Avogadro's number (6 × 10²³) tells us there are only ~3 × 10²² molecules in a mole of water. A 12C dilution already exceeds Avogadro's number; at 30C, the mathematical impossibility of any active molecule remaining is absolute.

Homeopathy: The Math of 30C

A 30C dilution is 1 part in 10^60

Probability of 1 active moleculein a typical oral dose
≈ 0
Rigorous RCTs > placeboCochrane summaries
0
Plausible mechanismaccepted physics/chem
None
FDA-approved indicationshomeopathic 'OTCs'
0
Placebo response attributablecontext & ritual
Real
At 30C, you would need a sphere of water 150 light-years across to contain one molecule of the original substance.

"Water Memory" Is Not Real

The theoretical response to the dilution problem — that water "remembers" the substances dissolved in it — was proposed by immunologist Jacques Benveniste in 1988 in a Nature paper that was later retracted after independent replication failed. Subsequent investigations by independent labs, including the Randi Foundation and multiple university chemistry departments, found no replicable evidence for water memory. The concept has no physical mechanism and contradicts hydrogen bond chemistry.

The Clinical Evidence

The clinical evidence is consistent with what physics predicts: homeopathic remedies perform no better than placebo. A 2015 systematic review by Australia's National Health and Medical Research Council analyzed 1,800 homeopathy papers and found no high-quality evidence of effect for any health condition. The Cochrane Collaboration has produced similar conclusions for individual homeopathic applications. Apparent benefits in uncontrolled studies reflect placebo effects, regression to the mean, and natural disease resolution.

Sources

  1. Australian Government NHMRC. "Evidence on the effectiveness of homeopathy for treating health conditions." National Health and Medical Research Council, 2015.
  2. Ernst E. "A systematic review of systematic reviews of homeopathy." British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 2002.
  3. Maddox J, Randi J, Stewart WW. "'High dilution' experiments a delusion." Nature, 1988.
Safety Alert

Phenibut: The Legal Supplement Causing Hospitalizations

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

Phenibut (β-phenyl-γ-aminobutyric acid) was developed in the Soviet Union in the 1960s as a pharmaceutical drug for anxiety, PTSD, and sleep disorders. It is widely prescribed in Russia, Ukraine, and Latvia as a medication under controlled conditions. In the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, and Australia, phenibut is not approved as a drug but also not formally scheduled — a regulatory gap that allows it to be legally sold as a dietary supplement.

How Phenibut Works

Phenibut is a GABA-B receptor agonist (similar to baclofen) with additional activity at voltage-gated calcium channels and GABA-A receptors. At typical doses (250–1,000 mg), it produces anxiolytic, sedative, and euphoric effects. At higher doses, its effects more closely resemble alcohol or benzodiazepines. Unlike most dietary supplement ingredients, phenibut crosses the blood-brain barrier efficiently due to its phenyl group, producing reliable and potent psychoactive effects.

The Dependence Problem

Phenibut's most serious clinical issue is rapid physical dependence. Case reports and clinical series document tolerance developing within 2–4 days of daily use. Withdrawal syndrome — anxiety, insomnia, agitation, tremor, psychosis in severe cases — can occur after as little as one week of daily use. Unlike caffeine withdrawal, phenibut withdrawal can be medically serious, sometimes requiring hospitalization and benzodiazepine treatment. The American Association of Poison Control Centers has reported a dramatic increase in phenibut exposure calls since 2012, when it became commonly available online.

ER and Hospitalization Reports

A 2020 case series published in Clinical Toxicology documented 16 cases of phenibut toxicity requiring ER treatment, including respiratory depression and altered consciousness. The European Monitoring Centre for Drugs and Drug Addiction (EMCDDA) has listed phenibut as a substance of increasing concern. Australia scheduled phenibut as a controlled substance in 2015; the UK's Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs recommended scheduling in 2021.

There is no legitimate reason for phenibut to be available without prescription. Anyone using it should be aware that daily use, even for a week, creates physiological dependence requiring a medically supervised taper to discontinue safely.

Sources

  1. Samokhvalov AV, et al. "Phenibut dependence." BMJ Case Reports, 2013.
  2. Jouney EA. "Phenibut (β-phenyl-γ-aminobutyric acid): an easily obtainable 'dietary supplement' with propensities for physical dependence and addiction." Current Psychiatry Reports, 2019.
  3. Graves JM, et al. "Phenibut exposures reported to poison control centers — United States, 2009–2019." MMWR Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, 2020.
Reality Check

Exogenous Ketones for Weight Loss: Science Says No

Apr 11, 2026 · 6 min read

Exogenous ketone products — ketone esters and ketone salts that raise blood ketone levels directly without dietary restriction — have been aggressively marketed for weight loss, fat burning, mental clarity, and athletic performance. They are expensive ($5–$10 per serving), taste unpleasant, and the clinical evidence for weight loss does not support their use for this purpose.

The Basic Mechanism Problem

Ketogenic diets produce weight loss through multiple mechanisms: caloric restriction (high-fat diets are satiating and reduce caloric intake), water loss from glycogen depletion, and potentially mild metabolic advantages. Exogenous ketones replicate only the elevated blood ketone state — without the caloric restriction or glycogen depletion. More critically, ketones are a calorie source (approximately 4 kcal/gram). Consuming exogenous ketones provides additional calories and signals to the body that energy is available, which can actually suppress endogenous fat breakdown (lipolysis).

Exogenous Ketones & Weight Loss

8-week placebo-controlled data

Ketone salts weight lossvs. placebo
−0.5 kg
Ketone esters weight losssmaller trials
−0.7 kg
Appetite suppression (2 h)short-term, real
Modest
vs. actual ketogenic dietketo diet dominates
−5 kg
Cost: $150/mo productvs. dietary carb cut
Bad
Ketosis from diet and ketosis from a drink are not pharmacologically equivalent. The drink is the one that gets sold.

What the Research Shows

A 2017 study in Frontiers in Physiology found that ketone ester drinks significantly suppressed free fatty acid availability (a marker of fat breakdown) for 3–4 hours after consumption. A 2020 meta-analysis of exogenous ketone trials found no significant effect on body weight or fat mass in any controlled study to date. Claims about appetite suppression from ketones have small, inconsistent evidence — some ketone esters may modestly suppress ghrelin, but this has not translated into caloric deficit or weight loss in clinical trials.

Performance Claims Are Nuanced

The picture is more nuanced for athletic performance. Some studies suggest ketone esters (not salts) may improve endurance performance in trained athletes by providing an additional oxidative substrate. This is a legitimate area of research, though results are inconsistent and the practical benefits for most athletes are unclear. These performance applications are entirely separate from weight loss claims.

Exogenous ketones are not a fat-loss supplement. They are an additional calorie source that may interfere with fat mobilization. Caloric restriction and exercise remain the only reliably effective weight loss interventions.

Sources

  1. Stubbs BJ, et al. "A ketone ester drink lowers human ghrelin and appetite." Obesity, 2018.
  2. Evans M, et al. "No benefit of ingestion of a ketone monoester supplement on 10-km running performance." Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 2019.
  3. Harvey CJ, et al. "The use of nutritional supplements to induce ketosis and reduce symptoms associated with keto-induction: a narrative review." PeerJ, 2018.
Kids

Vitamin A for Children: Essential but Easy to Overdose

Apr 11, 2026 · 5 min read

Vitamin A presents one of the starkest examples of context-dependence in pediatric nutrition. In low-income countries with limited dietary variety and endemic vitamin A deficiency, supplementation programs have reduced child mortality by 24% and halved deaths from measles-related complications — one of the most cost-effective public health interventions in history. In well-nourished children in Western countries, the same supplementation can cause harm.

Why Western Children Are Often Over-Supplemented

Vitamin A (as retinol) is a fat-soluble vitamin stored in the liver. Unlike water-soluble vitamins, excess cannot be excreted efficiently. Children in Western countries typically receive vitamin A from fortified cereals, dairy products, eggs, and liver — and many already consume amounts approaching or exceeding the Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA). Adding supplemental vitamin A on top of this dietary baseline can push intakes toward the tolerable upper limit (UL).

For children 1–3 years, the RDA is 300 mcg RAE/day; the UL is 600 mcg RAE/day. A single serving of liver can contain 2,000–8,000 mcg RAE. Many children's multivitamins contain 100–200% of the RDA as preformed retinol (not beta-carotene, which is self-limiting). Children who eat fortified foods, dairy, and eggs daily while taking a retinol-containing multivitamin may chronically exceed safe levels.

Signs of Toxicity

Chronic vitamin A toxicity in children presents as irritability, bone pain, increased intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri), and liver damage. Acute toxicity — from a single large dose — causes nausea, vomiting, and neurological symptoms. Vitamin A toxicity is a genuine pediatric medical emergency requiring intervention.

The Beta-Carotene Distinction

Beta-carotene (provitamin A from plants) does not cause toxicity — the body converts it to retinol only as needed and the conversion efficiency drops when vitamin A status is sufficient. Supplements providing vitamin A as beta-carotene are safer for children who may already have adequate intake. Pediatricians should review all vitamin A sources when evaluating a child's supplement regimen.

Sources

  1. Imdad A, et al. "Vitamin A supplementation for preventing morbidity and mortality in children from 6 months to 5 years of age." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2017.
  2. National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements. "Vitamin A: Fact Sheet for Health Professionals." NIH, 2023.
  3. Penniston KL, Tanumihardjo SA. "The acute and chronic toxic effects of vitamin A." American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2006.
Breakthrough

The Gut-Immune Connection: Probiotics and Respiratory Health

Apr 11, 2026 · 7 min read

The gut contains approximately 70% of the body's immune tissue, and gut microbiome composition has measurable effects on systemic immune function — including respiratory immunity. The gut-lung axis, a bidirectional communication network between intestinal microbiota and pulmonary immune responses, has emerged as one of the most clinically relevant areas of probiotic research.

What the RCTs Show

A 2015 Cochrane review of 13 RCTs (n=3,720) found probiotic supplementation reduced the incidence of upper respiratory tract infections (URTIs) by approximately 47% compared to placebo and reduced the duration of URTIs by approximately 1.9 days. Benefit was observed primarily with Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Lactobacillus acidophilus, and multi-strain preparations including Bifidobacterium species. The effect sizes are among the largest seen for any supplement intervention for immune outcomes.

A 2020 meta-analysis of 42 trials specifically examining probiotics and URTI outcomes found consistent reduction in incidence and duration, with greater benefits observed in older adults, children in daycare settings, and athletes with high training loads (who are more susceptible to respiratory infections).

Mechanisms

The proposed mechanisms are multiple and partially characterized. Probiotic colonization stimulates gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), which modulates the production of secretory IgA — the primary antibody defense at mucosal surfaces including the respiratory tract. Certain strains also enhance natural killer cell activity and modulate cytokine profiles toward anti-inflammatory patterns, potentially reducing both infection susceptibility and symptom severity.

Strain Specificity Matters

Not all probiotics are equivalent for respiratory outcomes. The evidence is strongest for Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and multi-strain products containing both Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species. Single-strain products with generic Lactobacillus acidophilus show weaker and less consistent effects. Dose should be at least 10 billion CFU/day with evidence of viability at point of consumption (enteric coating or refrigeration as appropriate).

Sources

  1. Hao Q, et al. "Probiotics for preventing acute upper respiratory tract infections." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2015.
  2. Kang EJ, et al. "The effect of probiotics on prevention of common cold: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trial studies." Korean Journal of Family Medicine, 2013.
  3. Zhu R, et al. "Meta-analysis of the efficacy of probiotics in the management of upper respiratory tract infections and allergy symptoms." Frontiers in Pharmacology, 2020.
Guide

Women's Supplement Guide: Evidence-Based Picks by Life Stage

Apr 11, 2026 · 8 min read

Women's nutritional needs are not simply smaller versions of men's needs. Hormonal cycles, reproductive biology, and distinct physiological transitions create supplement priorities that shift meaningfully across the lifespan. Generic "women's multivitamins" — which typically differ from standard multivitamins only in their marketing and iron content — address these needs poorly. Evidence-based supplementation for women requires thinking by life stage.

Reproductive Years (18–40)

Folate/Folic acid: The most critical supplement for women of reproductive age. 400–800 mcg/day reduces neural tube defect risk by 50–70% when taken before conception and in early pregnancy. The FDA requires folic acid fortification of grains for this reason, but supplementation remains important for those planning pregnancy or using unreliable contraception. Methylfolate (5-MTHF) is preferable for the ~10% of women with MTHFR polymorphisms that impair folic acid conversion.
Iron: Menstrual blood loss makes iron deficiency the most common nutrient deficiency in premenopausal women globally. Testing ferritin before supplementing is ideal — excessive iron is also harmful. 18 mg/day (the RDA for premenopausal women) from food or supplements corrects mild deficiency; higher therapeutic doses require medical supervision.
Vitamin D: As for all adults, universal supplementation of 1,000–2,000 IU/day is appropriate given widespread deficiency.

Pregnancy

Prenatal vitamins are the foundation. Key additions: DHA (200–300 mg/day) for fetal brain development, choline (450 mg/day, often under-represented in prenatal formulas), and iodine (150 mcg/day for thyroid function — critical for fetal neurological development). Vitamin A as retinol should be limited to 700 mcg RAE/day — excess is teratogenic.

Perimenopause and Menopause (40–60)

Declining estrogen accelerates bone resorption and increases cardiovascular risk. Calcium: 1,200 mg/day (total from food and supplements) with Vitamin D for bone preservation. Calcium carbonate requires stomach acid; calcium citrate is better absorbed if taken without food or in achlorhydric individuals. Magnesium: Supports sleep (disturbed by hormonal changes), bone health, and cardiovascular function. Black cohosh: Modest evidence for reducing vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes); Cochrane review suggests small benefit. Not a hormone replacement — not appropriate for women with hormone-sensitive cancers.

Post-Menopause (60+)

Bone health remains central: calcium and vitamin D continue to be the most evidence-supported supplements. Vitamin B12 absorption decreases with age (due to declining gastric acid); supplementation or intramuscular injection may be warranted. Omega-3 fatty acids for cardiovascular protection become increasingly relevant as estrogen's cardioprotective effects are lost.

Sources

  1. Blencowe H, et al. "Folic acid to reduce neonatal mortality from neural tube disorders." International Journal of Epidemiology, 2010.
  2. Recker RR, et al. "Calcium absorption and achlorhydria." New England Journal of Medicine, 1985.
  3. Leach MJ, Moore V. "Black cohosh (Cimicifuga spp.) for menopausal symptoms." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2012.
Guide

The Complete Sleep Supplement Guide: Beyond Melatonin

Apr 12, 2026 · 9 min read

Melatonin dominates the sleep supplement market, but it is one of many compounds with evidence for improving sleep quality. More importantly, melatonin primarily addresses circadian timing — it helps shift when you fall asleep, not necessarily how deeply. For people whose problem is not sleep onset timing but rather sleep quality, anxiety at bedtime, or middle-of-the-night waking, other supplements may work better.

Magnesium Glycinate

Magnesium deficiency is widespread — estimated to affect 50–60% of adults in the US — and magnesium plays a direct role in regulating the GABA receptors that promote relaxation. Magnesium glycinate (bound to glycine, itself a sleep-promoting amino acid) is the best-absorbed form for neurological effects. A 2022 meta-analysis of 19 RCTs found magnesium supplementation significantly improved subjective sleep quality, sleep efficiency, and sleep onset latency, particularly in individuals with baseline deficiency. Dose: 200–400 mg elemental magnesium (as glycinate), taken 1 hour before bed.

L-Theanine

L-theanine, an amino acid found in green tea, promotes alpha-wave brain activity associated with relaxed alertness and reduces anxiety without sedation. In sleep research, it reduces time to sleep onset and improves sleep quality ratings without the grogginess associated with sedative drugs. Its primary mechanism — antagonizing glutamate receptors while enhancing GABA — makes it synergistic with melatonin. Dose: 100–200 mg, 30–60 minutes before bed. Low side effect risk at these doses.

Glycine

Glycine is a non-essential amino acid that lowers core body temperature and modulates NMDA receptors in ways that promote sleep onset and deeper sleep. Japanese trials found 3 g of glycine at bedtime improved sleep quality, reduced daytime fatigue, and improved cognitive performance the following day in adults with self-reported poor sleep. It is inexpensive, well tolerated, and underutilized. Best taken as a powder dissolved in water.

Phosphatidylserine

Phosphatidylserine (PS) is most relevant for people whose sleep problems stem from elevated evening cortisol — the night-owl pattern where cortisol fails to decline appropriately in the evening. PS blunts cortisol and ACTH responses by approximately 20–30% at doses of 400–600 mg/day. Less useful for primary insomnia, but worth considering for stress-related sleep disruption.

Putting It Together

A practical stack for most people: melatonin (0.5–1 mg, low dose) combined with magnesium glycinate (300 mg) and L-theanine (200 mg), 45 minutes before target sleep time. Add glycine (3 g) if sleep quality is the primary complaint. Reserve phosphatidylserine for stress-driven insomnia. Start with one compound at a time to identify what works before combining.

Sources

  1. Arab A, et al. "The role of magnesium in sleep health: a systematic review of available literature." Biological Trace Element Research, 2022.
  2. Bannai M, Kawai N. "New therapeutic strategy for amino acid medicine: glycine improves the quality of sleep." Journal of Pharmacological Sciences, 2012.
  3. Lyon MR, et al. "The effects of L-theanine on objective sleep quality in boys with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder." Alternative Medicine Review, 2011.
Breakthrough

Folate vs Folic Acid: Why the Form Matters

Apr 12, 2026 · 7 min read

Folate and folic acid are often used interchangeably on supplement labels, but they are not the same compound and they are not equally useful for everyone. This distinction has real clinical consequences — especially for pregnancy, cardiovascular risk reduction, and mental health — and the supplement industry has been slow to reflect it clearly.

The Biochemistry

Folic acid is the synthetic, oxidized form of folate found in most supplements and fortified foods. To be biologically active, it must be converted through several enzymatic steps into 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF) — the form that circulates in the blood and crosses the blood-brain barrier. The key enzyme in this conversion, MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase), is significantly impaired in individuals carrying common MTHFR polymorphisms. The C677T variant reduces enzyme activity by approximately 35% in heterozygotes and 70% in homozygotes.

Prevalence of MTHFR Variants

MTHFR variants are among the most common genetic polymorphisms in humans. The C677T variant is carried by approximately 40–60% of the US population in heterozygous form, and 10–15% are homozygous. A second variant, A1298C, affects a smaller proportion. Together, these variants affect somewhere between 10–20% of people's ability to efficiently process folic acid — a significant public health consideration given how widely folic acid supplementation is recommended.

The Clinical Implications

For most people, adequate dietary folate and low-dose folic acid supplementation are sufficient. For MTHFR homozygotes, however, standard folic acid may not effectively raise active folate levels — and evidence suggests unmetabolized folic acid may accumulate in blood and potentially interfere with natural folate utilization. Methylfolate (5-MTHF) bypasses the MTHFR enzyme entirely and raises plasma folate more reliably in carriers. Several studies have shown methylfolate is superior to folic acid for reducing elevated homocysteine — a cardiovascular risk marker — in MTHFR variant carriers.

Mental Health Connection

Folate is essential for synthesis of serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine via the one-carbon methylation cycle. In patients with MTHFR variants, impaired folate metabolism has been associated with higher rates of depression and poorer response to antidepressants. L-methylfolate (prescription Deplin at 7.5–15 mg, or OTC 5-MTHF at lower doses) has been shown in RCTs to augment SSRI response in treatment-resistant depression. This is among the most clinically underutilized applications of nutrient supplementation in psychiatry.

Practical Guidance

If you are pregnant or planning pregnancy, both forms are protective against neural tube defects — but 5-MTHF is preferable if you know you carry MTHFR variants or simply want to ensure maximum bioavailability. For general supplementation, methylfolate costs slightly more than folic acid but is appropriate for anyone uncertain of their MTHFR status. Genetic testing (23andMe, AncestryDNA, or targeted MTHFR panels) can identify your status for under $50.

Sources

  1. Greenberg JA, Bell SJ, Auld G. "Folic acid supplementation and pregnancy: more than just neural tube defect prevention." Reviews in Obstetrics and Gynecology, 2011.
  2. Papakostas GI, et al. "L-methylfolate as adjunctive therapy for SSRI-resistant major depression." American Journal of Psychiatry, 2012.
  3. Prinz-Langenohl R, et al. "5-methyltetrahydrofolate increases plasma folate more effectively than folic acid in women with the homozygous or wild-type MTHFR 677C→T genotype." British Journal of Pharmacology, 2009.
Reality Check

Detox Foot Pads and Cleanses: Pure Pseudoscience

Apr 12, 2026 · 6 min read

The detox supplement industry generates over $50 billion annually, built on a premise that basic human physiology does not support. Your liver, kidneys, skin, and lungs are a highly effective detoxification system that has evolved over millions of years. No commercially available supplement, foot pad, or cleanse meaningfully enhances this system in healthy individuals. The evidence for this statement is not ambiguous.

Foot Pads: The Color Trick

Detox foot pads — adhesive patches applied to the soles of the feet overnight — are sold with the claim that they draw toxins out through the skin, turning brown or black as evidence of extracted waste. Toxicological analyses of used foot pads consistently show the color change is caused by the reaction of wood vinegar (bamboo vinegar) and tourmaline with moisture from perspiration. The darkening occurs in response to humidity, not toxin extraction. Chemical analyses of used versus unused pads show no detectable increase in heavy metals, environmental toxins, or metabolic waste products.

Detox Foot Pads: What Turns Them Brown

The 'toxin' color is a chemistry demo

Foot 'toxins' leaving bodynot a biological route
None
Pad color w/ pure watersame as on foot
Yes
Sweat + pad extract reactionwood-vinegar browning
Yes
Heavy metals in used padslab-tested vs. unused
None
Placebo / ritual effectsubjective reports
Real
Independent lab analyses (including by 20/20 and academic testers) found used pads contained no significant toxins.

The "Toxin" Problem

A recurring feature of detox marketing is the failure to name specific toxins. When asked what toxins their product removes, supplement companies invariably respond with vague terms: “environmental toxins,” “accumulated waste,” “heavy metals,” “free radicals.” Legitimate chelation therapy for heavy metal poisoning (a real medical treatment) uses specific pharmaceutical chelators for specific metals confirmed by blood testing — not dietary supplements or foot patches. The supplement industry's use of the word “toxin” is a marketing term, not a medical one.

Juice Cleanses

Multi-day juice cleanses claim to rest the digestive system and purge accumulated toxins. The liver processes and excretes metabolic waste products continuously and does not require “rest” from food — in fact, certain nutrients found in whole foods (cruciferous vegetables, sulfur compounds) upregulate liver detoxification enzymes. The weight lost during juice cleanses is primarily water and glycogen — not fat — and is almost entirely regained within days of resuming normal eating. No randomized controlled trial has demonstrated a toxin-removing effect of any commercial cleanse.

When Real Detoxification Is Needed

True toxic exposures — lead poisoning, mercury toxicity, acetaminophen overdose — are medical emergencies treated with specific, evidence-based interventions in hospital settings. If you suspect heavy metal exposure, request serum and urine testing from a physician. If confirmed, treatment will involve pharmaceutical chelation under medical supervision, not any supplement. Consumer “heavy metal detox” products have no clinical evidence for removing significant quantities of any specific toxin from the body.

Sources

  1. Ernst E. "Detox: not a treatment but a con." British Journal of General Practice, 2012.
  2. Klein AV, Kiat H. "Detox diets for toxin elimination and weight management: a critical review of the evidence." Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics, 2015.
  3. Novella S. "Detox scams." Science-Based Medicine, 2013.
Safety Alert

Kratom: The Opioid Supplement Hiding in Plain Sight

Apr 12, 2026 · 7 min read

Kratom (Mitragyna speciosa) is a Southeast Asian plant whose leaves are sold in the United States as a botanical supplement — in capsules, powders, and shots at gas stations, convenience stores, and online retailers. It is marketed for energy, mood enhancement, and opioid withdrawal management. What the marketing rarely discloses clearly: kratom's active alkaloids, mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine, are potent opioid receptor agonists.

The Pharmacology

7-hydroxymitragynine (7-HMG) binds mu-opioid receptors with higher potency than morphine at equivalent doses. It produces dose-dependent opioid effects: analgesia at low doses, euphoria and sedation at higher doses, respiratory depression at toxic doses. The DEA considered Schedule I classification of kratom's major alkaloids in 2016 before withdrawing the proposal following public opposition. As of 2026, kratom remains federally legal in the US despite classification as a drug of concern.

Dependence and Withdrawal

Physical dependence on kratom develops with regular use, typically within several weeks. Withdrawal symptoms — documented in hundreds of case reports and multiple observational studies — include muscle aches, insomnia, anxiety, irritability, sweating, nausea, and cravings. The syndrome is clinically similar to, though often milder than, opioid withdrawal. The FDA has received thousands of adverse event reports related to kratom, including reports of deaths — though many involve polysubstance use, making kratom's independent contribution difficult to isolate.

Who Uses Kratom

Kratom has a substantial user community, including people who use it to self-manage chronic pain, to taper from prescription opioids, and recreationally. Some users report kratom to be genuinely helpful for pain management or opioid substitution. This represents a real harm-reduction question: for individuals already opioid-dependent, kratom may represent a less harmful alternative. However, this is a medical decision that should involve a physician, not a supplement purchase decision.

The Regulatory Gap

Because kratom is sold as a supplement rather than a drug, it bypasses the FDA's drug approval process. Products vary dramatically in potency, may be adulterated, and lack dosage standardization. The American Kratom Association has lobbied successfully to prevent scheduling in many states and federally. Several states have enacted the Kratom Consumer Protection Act requiring labeling and purity standards — a step toward harm reduction, though not a resolution of the underlying safety questions.

Sources

  1. Kruegel AC, Grundmann O. "The medicinal chemistry and neuropharmacology of kratom." Neuropharmacology, 2018.
  2. Veltri C, Grundmann O. "Current perspectives on the impact of kratom use." Substance Abuse and Rehabilitation, 2019.
  3. FDA. "FDA and Kratom." US Food and Drug Administration, updated 2024.
Reality Check

Colostrum: Ancient Superfood or Influencer Hype?

Apr 12, 2026 · 6 min read

Bovine colostrum — the first milk produced by cows after birth — has become one of the fastest-growing supplement categories in 2025–2026, fueled by wellness influencer endorsements and claims ranging from immune enhancement to gut repair to athletic performance. The science on colostrum is genuine but narrow. The gap between what has been demonstrated in clinical trials and what is claimed in marketing is substantial.

What Colostrum Actually Contains

Colostrum is rich in immunoglobulins (antibodies), lactoferrin, growth factors (IGF-1, TGF-β), proline-rich polypeptides, and various cytokines. These components serve a clear biological role in newborn mammals — establishing gut immunity and promoting tissue development in infants who lack mature immune systems. The question for supplementation in healthy adults is whether these proteins survive digestive degradation intact and whether they produce meaningful biological effects.

Colostrum, Take Two (TikTok Era)

Influencer claims vs. what exists in trials

Gut permeability (athletes)Marchbank
Moderate
URI reduction (athletes)3 small RCTs
Modest
General immunityno healthy-adult RCT
Weak
Skin / 'dewy glow'zero trials
None
Human long-term safety>3 months, dosed
Unstudied
2024 saw a 400% spike in colostrum sales driven by a handful of TikTok creators. Clinical trial volume: unchanged.

Where the Evidence Is Legitimate

The best evidence for colostrum supplementation in adults is in athletes undergoing intense training. A 2012 meta-analysis found colostrum (20 g/day for 8 weeks) modestly reduced upper respiratory tract infection incidence compared to whey protein in elite athletes — a population with known immune suppression during high training loads. A separate line of evidence supports bovine colostrum for reducing exercise-induced gut permeability ("leaky gut") in endurance athletes. These are real, if modest, findings in a specific population.

The Healthy Adult Problem

For non-athlete, healthy adults — the demographic that represents most colostrum buyers — the evidence is minimal. There are no large RCTs demonstrating immune enhancement, anti-aging effects, skin improvement, or the broad wellness benefits marketed by colostrum brands. IGF-1 in colostrum is largely digested in the gut and does not reach systemic circulation at meaningful concentrations. The dose of immunoglobulins from a typical supplement serving is a fraction of what the gut encounters from food sources daily.

Cost vs Evidence

Quality bovine colostrum supplements cost $40–$80 per month. At current evidence levels, this expenditure is poorly justified for most healthy adults. If you are an athlete in heavy training with recurrent upper respiratory infections, a short-term trial may be reasonable. For everyone else, the immune and gut health outcomes colostrum claims to produce are better supported by established interventions: adequate sleep, a fiber-rich diet, and regular exercise.

Sources

  1. Davison G. "Bovine colostrum and immune function after exercise." Medicine and Sport Science, 2012.
  2. Marchbank T, et al. "The nutriceutical bovine colostrum truncates the increase in gut permeability caused by heavy exercise in athletes." American Journal of Physiology: Gastrointestinal and Liver Physiology, 2011.
  3. Shing CM, et al. "The influence of bovine colostrum supplementation on exercise performance in highly trained cyclists." British Journal of Sports Medicine, 2006.
Kids

Vitamin C for Kids: How Much Is Too Much?

Apr 12, 2026 · 5 min read

Vitamin C is widely regarded as the safest vitamin to give children — it is water-soluble, excess is excreted, and deficiency (scurvy) is a genuine concern in picky eaters. These facts are all true. What is less often communicated is that the safe upper intake levels for children are substantially lower than for adults, and that high-dose supplementation — common in households where parents supplement themselves with 1,000+ mg daily and give children scaled-down versions — can cause real problems.

Age-Appropriate Upper Limits

The NIH establishes Tolerable Upper Intake Levels (ULs) for vitamin C as follows: children aged 1–3: 400 mg/day; ages 4–8: 650 mg/day; ages 9–13: 1,200 mg/day; ages 14–18: 1,800 mg/day. The RDA for most children is only 15–75 mg depending on age — easily met by diet. A single orange provides approximately 70 mg. These upper limits exist because exceeding them is associated with GI disturbances (diarrhea, nausea, abdominal cramps), and at persistently high doses, an increased risk of oxalate kidney stones due to vitamin C metabolism to oxalate.

Vitamin C and Colds: What the Evidence Shows

The most common reason parents give children extra vitamin C is cold prevention. The Cochrane review on vitamin C for the common cold (updated 2013, over 29 trials, 11,000+ participants) found that regular vitamin C supplementation did not reduce the incidence of colds in the general population — though it did modestly reduce duration by roughly half a day. Therapeutic dosing at cold onset also showed no significant benefit. The immune-boosting narrative drives most pediatric vitamin C supplementation but is not well-supported by clinical evidence at doses above dietary intake.

Iron Absorption Interaction

One genuinely useful application of vitamin C in children is enhancing non-heme iron absorption from plant foods. Consuming 25–75 mg of vitamin C alongside iron-rich plant foods (lentils, beans, fortified cereals) increases iron absorption by up to 67%. This is clinically relevant for vegetarian children or those with iron-deficiency anemia. The interaction is achievable with food sources — a small glass of orange juice with a bowl of fortified cereal — without supplementation.

Practical Guidance for Parents

Children eating a varied diet including fruits and vegetables almost certainly meet vitamin C requirements without supplementation. Supplementation is warranted for very picky eaters with restricted diets. If supplementing, use age-appropriate doses: 25–50 mg for toddlers, 50–100 mg for school-age children. Avoid adult-dose vitamin C products. Chewable tablets are preferable to gummies for dosing accuracy.

Sources

  1. Hemilä H, Chalker E. "Vitamin C for preventing and treating the common cold." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2013.
  2. NIH Office of Dietary Supplements. "Vitamin C — Fact Sheet for Health Professionals." Updated 2024.
  3. Hallberg L, Rossander L. "Effect of different drinks on the absorption of non-haeme iron from composite meals." Human Nutrition: Applied Nutrition, 1982.
Breakthrough

Boron: The Forgotten Trace Mineral

Apr 12, 2026 · 6 min read

Boron does not have an established Recommended Dietary Allowance. It has no known essential biochemical function that has been formally characterized in the way that, say, zinc's role in enzyme catalysis has been. And yet, the accumulating evidence from human studies suggests boron plays meaningful roles in bone health, sex hormone metabolism, and cognition — and that deficiency in Western diets may be common.

Testosterone and Estrogen Effects

A landmark 1987 study by the USDA found that supplementing 3 mg of boron per day in postmenopausal women on a low-boron diet increased serum estradiol and testosterone to levels comparable to estrogen replacement therapy. A 2011 pilot study in healthy males found that 6 weeks of boron supplementation (10 mg/day) significantly increased free testosterone (by 28%), reduced estradiol (by 39%), and reduced dihydrotestosterone. A 2015 clinical study confirmed significant free testosterone increases and sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) reductions — SHBG binds testosterone, so lower SHBG means more bioavailable testosterone.

Bone Health

Boron appears to influence bone metabolism by reducing urinary calcium and magnesium excretion and activating vitamin D. Studies in boron-deficient animals show decreased bone strength. Population data from NHANES suggest adequate boron intake correlates with bone mineral density. Boron's interactions with vitamin D and magnesium make it particularly interesting as a co-supplement for bone health protocols, though randomized trial evidence in humans is more limited than for calcium and vitamin D.

Cognitive Effects

Studies from the USDA's Human Nutrition Research Center found that low dietary boron (0.25 mg/day) was associated with significantly impaired performance on tasks involving attention, hand-eye coordination, and short-term memory compared to adequate boron intake (3.25 mg/day). Boron appears to influence brain electrical activity, with adequate intake associated with lower theta wave activity (associated with lower arousal) and higher alpha and beta activity.

Dietary Sources and Supplementation

The best dietary sources of boron are fruits (particularly avocado, prunes, raisins, and apples), legumes, and nuts. Average Western dietary intake is estimated at 0.5–3 mg/day — on the low end relative to doses showing effects in trials (3–10 mg/day). Boron supplements are inexpensive. The Tolerable Upper Intake Level is set at 20 mg/day for adults. No significant toxicity has been observed at supplemental doses of 3–10 mg/day.

Sources

  1. Nielsen FH, et al. "Effect of dietary boron on mineral, estrogen, and testosterone metabolism in postmenopausal women." FASEB Journal, 1987.
  2. Naghii MR, et al. "Comparative effects of daily and weekly boron supplementation on plasma steroid hormones and proinflammatory cytokines." Journal of Trace Elements in Medicine and Biology, 2011.
  3. Penland JG. "The importance of boron nutrition for brain and psychological function." Biological Trace Element Research, 1998.
Guide

The Supplement Stack for Brain Health After 50

Apr 12, 2026 · 9 min read

Cognitive decline with aging is not uniform or inevitable, but it is common. Working memory, processing speed, and verbal recall all show measurable decline across population averages beginning in the fifth decade. Several supplements have meaningful clinical evidence for slowing or modifying aspects of this decline — with important caveats about effect size and which populations benefit most.

Omega-3 DHA: The Foundation

DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) is the dominant structural fat in brain gray matter and synaptic membranes. Brain DHA content declines with age, and plasma DHA levels correlate with cognitive performance in numerous observational studies. The MIDAS trial (485 healthy adults with age-related cognitive complaints, 900 mg DHA/day for 24 weeks) found significant improvements in episodic memory and learning. Higher baseline DHA levels are consistently associated with lower dementia risk in large prospective cohorts. Dose: 900 mg–1,000 mg DHA daily, from algal oil (to avoid mercury concerns of fish oil).

B Vitamins: Homocysteine Management

Elevated homocysteine is a well-established independent risk factor for cognitive decline and dementia. B6, B9 (folate), and B12 are the three vitamins that regulate homocysteine levels. The VITACOG trial (168 older adults with mild cognitive impairment, 2-year B vitamin supplementation) found treatment halved the rate of brain atrophy in participants with elevated homocysteine, with improved cognitive outcomes. B12 deficiency specifically is common in people over 60 due to declining gastric acid production — blood testing before supplementation is ideal, but 500–1,000 mcg B12 daily is low-risk.

Phosphatidylserine

Phosphatidylserine (PS) is a phospholipid that constitutes a major structural component of neuronal cell membranes. PS declines in the brain with age. Clinical trials — including studies that received an FDA qualified health claim — show PS (100–400 mg/day) improves memory, concentration, and word recall in people with age-associated memory impairment. The effect size is modest (10–15% improvement over placebo on standardized memory tests) but consistent across multiple trials.

Lion’s Mane

Lion’s mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) stimulates production of Nerve Growth Factor (NGF) — a protein that supports neuron survival and function. A 2009 Japanese RCT in adults with mild cognitive impairment found significant cognitive score improvements after 16 weeks at 3 g/day, with decline observed after stopping supplementation. A 2023 Australian trial found improvements in immediate and delayed word recall in healthy adults. The compound is promising, though most trials are small. Dose: 500–1,000 mg/day of a fruiting body extract standardized for beta-glucans.

What Doesn’t Make the Cut

Ginkgo biloba, despite decades of use and marketing for memory, failed in the large GEM trial (3,000+ older adults, 6 years) to reduce dementia incidence. Prevagen (apoaequorin, a protein from jellyfish) has no plausible mechanism for crossing the blood-brain barrier and has failed in independent testing to outperform placebo. These are the most popular brain supplements in the US — neither should be prioritized over the above.

Sources

  1. Yurko-Mauro K, et al. "Beneficial effects of docosahexaenoic acid on cognition in age-related cognitive decline." Alzheimer's & Dementia, 2010.
  2. Smith AD, et al. "Homocysteine-lowering by B vitamins slows the rate of accelerated brain atrophy in mild cognitive impairment." PLOS ONE, 2010.
  3. Mori K, et al. "Improving effects of the mushroom Yamabushitake (Hericium erinaceus) on mild cognitive impairment: a double-blind placebo-controlled clinical trial." Phytotherapy Research, 2009.
Safety Alert

Green Tea Extract and Liver Failure: What You Need to Know

Apr 12, 2026 · 7 min read

Green tea is one of the most extensively studied plant-derived compounds in human nutrition research, with a strong safety record when consumed as a beverage. Green tea extract (GTE) — a concentrated supplement form providing 400–1,000 mg of catechins, primarily EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), per dose — is a different matter. At these doses, GTE has caused acute liver injury, liver failure requiring transplantation, and death in documented case reports. The FDA issued a detailed review in 2017 concluding that GTE used in weight-loss supplements is “possibly unsafe.”

The Dose Problem

A cup of green tea provides approximately 50–100 mg of catechins. Green tea extract supplements typically provide 400–1,000 mg per serving — equivalent to 4–10 strong cups consumed simultaneously. EGCG at high doses appears to generate reactive oxygen species in hepatocytes (liver cells) in a dose-dependent manner. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) concluded in 2018 that GTE doses above 800 mg EGCG/day raise safety concerns and recommended against use at these levels. Animal dose-response studies consistently show hepatotoxicity at concentrations achievable with supplementation.

The Case Reports

The World Journal of Hepatology documented multiple cases of acute hepatitis attributable to GTE weight-loss supplements — including cases in previously healthy young women who required liver transplantation. The consistent pattern: use of a commercially available GTE product (often marketed for fat loss), onset of jaundice within weeks, biopsy-confirmed hepatocellular damage, resolution after discontinuation. Hepatotoxicity from GTE is classified as idiosyncratic — most users show no liver damage — but the subset that does react can develop severe injury rapidly.

Who Is at Greatest Risk

Risk factors for GTE-induced liver injury appear to include fasting consumption (taking GTE on an empty stomach significantly increases peak EGCG plasma concentrations), high doses (>800 mg EGCG/day), female sex, and potentially certain genetic variants in drug metabolism enzymes. Stacking GTE with other supplements (particularly those with their own hepatotoxic potential, such as niacin at high doses or certain herbal extracts) substantially increases risk.

Safer Alternatives

If you are seeking the cardiovascular or metabolic benefits attributed to green tea polyphenols, beverage consumption is substantially safer than extract supplementation — the matrix of tea slows absorption and keeps plasma EGCG concentrations well below potentially toxic thresholds. If using GTE for weight management, the evidence for benefit is modest at best and does not justify hepatotoxic risk. Avoid fasting use. Never exceed 400 mg EGCG/day from supplements.

Sources

  1. EFSA Panel on Food Additives and Nutrient Sources. "Safety of green tea catechins." EFSA Journal, 2018.
  2. Navarro VJ, et al. "Liver injury from herbals and dietary supplements in the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network." Hepatology, 2014.
  3. Mazzanti G, et al. "Hepatotoxicity from green tea: a review of the literature and two unpublished cases." European Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 2009.
Reality Check

Turkesterone: The TikTok Muscle Builder That Doesn’t Work

Apr 12, 2026 · 5 min read

Turkesterone is an ecdysteroid — a steroid-like compound found in plants and insects — that became a breakout supplement trend on TikTok and fitness forums starting around 2021. Influencers claimed it produced steroid-like muscle gains without steroid side effects. The hype was extraordinary. The evidence was, at the time, essentially nonexistent in humans. In 2022, the first rigorous human trial was published. It found turkesterone did not work.

The Animal Data That Started It

The excitement around turkesterone traces to rodent and insect studies showing ecdysteroids can activate anabolic signaling pathways — specifically, stimulation of the ERb (estrogen receptor beta) pathway and protein synthesis. A 2019 German study found ecdysterone supplementation in rats increased muscle mass. The authors noted that ecdysterone should be considered for addition to the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA) prohibited list given its anabolic potential. This was the study cited in virtually every pro-turkesterone argument. It was conducted in rodents.

Turkesterone: The Evidence Map

TikTok muscle builder vs. actual research

Rat / insect studiesecdysteroid mechanism
Weak
Human RCTsPubMed, any year
0
Oral bioavailability (human)poor absorption
Low
Anabolic vs. actual steroidsorders of magnitude
~0
TikTok #turkesterone views2023–24 peak
500M+
Turkesterone's reputation is built on two things: a Soviet-era insect study, and a Liver King endorsement. Neither is evidence of muscle gain.

The Human Trial

The landmark Willoughby et al. 2022 trial enrolled 42 trained men in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled 8-week resistance training protocol with turkesterone supplementation (500 mg/day). Outcomes measured included muscle mass (DXA scan), strength (1RM bench and leg press), and anabolic hormones. Results: no statistically significant differences between turkesterone and placebo on any primary outcome measure. Muscle gains were essentially identical in both groups. The paper concluded there was “no evidence for an ergogenic effect of turkesterone supplementation in resistance-trained men.”

The Bioavailability Problem

Even setting aside the human trial failure, turkesterone faces a fundamental pharmacological problem: its oral bioavailability is extremely poor. Ecdysteroids are poorly absorbed in the human GI tract and rapidly metabolized. The plasma concentrations achieved with typical supplement doses are a fraction of the concentrations used in cell culture experiments showing anabolic activity. Supplement companies sell “complexed” or “cyclodextrin” turkesterone at premium prices claiming enhanced absorption — but no human pharmacokinetic data has established that these formulations produce meaningfully higher bioavailability.

The Bottom Line

Turkesterone supplements typically cost $40–$80 per month. The mechanism is speculative in humans, oral bioavailability is poor, and the only rigorous human trial found no effect. For muscle building, the evidence-based options remain: creatine monohydrate, adequate protein (1.6–2.2 g/kg/day), progressive resistance training, and adequate sleep. Turkesterone is a case study in how rodent data and influencer amplification can create a market with no clinical foundation.

Sources

  1. Willoughby DS, et al. "Effects of turkesterone supplementation on body composition and exercise performance in resistance-trained males." Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition, 2022.
  2. Isenmann E, et al. "Ecdysteroids as non-conventional anabolic agent: performance enhancement by ecdysterone supplementation in humans." Archives of Toxicology, 2019.
  3. Lafont R, Dinan L. "Practical uses for ecdysteroids in mammals including humans: an update." Journal of Insect Science, 2003.
Kids

Probiotics for Kids After Antibiotics

Apr 12, 2026 · 6 min read

Antibiotics are among the most valuable medical interventions in pediatric medicine, but they come with a well-recognized cost: broad-spectrum disruption of the gut microbiome. Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (AAD) affects 10–40% of children taking antibiotics, depending on the antibiotic class and the child’s age. The question of whether probiotic supplementation during and after antibiotic courses can reduce this disruption has been studied extensively in children, with more consistent evidence than in most supplement categories.

The Evidence: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG

Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) is the best-studied probiotic strain in pediatric AAD prevention. A 2015 Cochrane review of 23 trials (3,938 children) found that probiotics — primarily LGG — reduced the risk of AAD by 52% (RR 0.48). LGG also significantly reduced risk of Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea. The number needed to treat was approximately 7 — meaning roughly 1 in 7 children given LGG avoided AAD who would otherwise have developed it. This is a clinically meaningful effect size for a low-risk intervention.

The Evidence: Saccharomyces boulardii

Saccharomyces boulardii is a yeast probiotic that is naturally antibiotic-resistant (it is a yeast, not a bacterium, so antibiotics don’t kill it). This makes it uniquely practical during antibiotic courses — unlike bacterial probiotics, timing relative to the antibiotic dose is less critical. Multiple trials show S. boulardii (250–500 mg/day) reduces AAD incidence by approximately 50% in children, comparable to LGG. Some guidelines recommend S. boulardii over LGG specifically because its antibiotic resistance eliminates the question of whether the probiotic is being killed alongside the pathogenic bacteria.

Timing and Dosing

For maximum effectiveness, probiotics should be started at the same time as the antibiotic course — not after. Waiting until symptoms appear reduces effectiveness. Continue for at least 1–2 weeks after completing the antibiotic. For bacterial probiotic strains (LGG, etc.), space dosing at least 2 hours from antibiotic administration to minimize direct bactericidal effects. S. boulardii can be taken simultaneously without concern. Minimum effective doses in trials: LGG at 10 billion CFU/day; S. boulardii at 250–500 mg/day.

Longer-Term Microbiome Recovery

Beyond AAD prevention, broader microbiome disruption from antibiotic courses in children can persist for weeks to months. Fermented foods — yogurt with live cultures, kefir — provide diverse microbial species beyond single-strain probiotic products and may accelerate microbiome recovery. Diet high in prebiotic fiber (fruits, vegetables, legumes) supports regrowth of beneficial species. These dietary approaches complement probiotic supplementation and are appropriate long-term strategies after the probiotic course ends.

Sources

  1. Goldenberg JZ, et al. "Probiotics for the prevention of pediatric antibiotic-associated diarrhea." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2015.
  2. McFarland LV. "Systematic review and meta-analysis of Saccharomyces boulardii in adult patients." World Journal of Gastroenterology, 2010.
  3. Szajewska H, Młodzańska A. "Meta-analysis: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG for treating acute diarrhoea in children." Alimentary Pharmacology & Therapeutics, 2010.
Reality Check

Why Most Vitamin D Studies Are Misleading

Apr 12, 2026 · 8 min read

Vitamin D research presents a paradox. Observational data strongly and consistently links low vitamin D levels to almost every major chronic disease — cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, dementia, autoimmune conditions, depression. Yet the large randomized controlled trials designed to test whether supplementation prevents these outcomes have been largely disappointing. Understanding why requires examining the structural flaws in most vitamin D research.

Flaw 1: Enrolling Non-Deficient Participants

The largest and most cited vitamin D supplementation trial is VITAL (25,871 participants, 2,000 IU vitamin D3 vs. placebo, 5+ years). VITAL found no significant reduction in cardiovascular disease or cancer incidence in the overall cohort. But critically: 67% of VITAL participants had baseline 25(OH)D levels above 30 ng/mL — the threshold typically considered replete. Supplementing already-sufficient people with additional vitamin D has no theoretical basis for producing benefit. When VITAL results were analyzed only for participants with baseline deficiency, some outcomes improved. The trial was designed to test population-level supplementation, not targeted supplementation of deficient individuals.

Why Vitamin D Trials Confuse People

The design flaws that inflate — or erase — effects

Reverse causationillness → low D, not reverse
Common
Confounding by sunlightoutdoor time = health
Common
Dose too low to move serum400–800 IU arm in RCTs
Common
Enrolling D-replete subjectsno deficiency = no effect
Common
Effect in truly deficient25-OH <20 ng/mL
Real
Vitamin D almost certainly 'works' — in people who are actually deficient. Most published null results tested it in people who weren't.

Flaw 2: Inadequate Doses

Many early vitamin D trials used 400–800 IU/day — doses now understood to be insufficient to raise serum 25(OH)D to levels associated with health benefits in observational studies. Trials that targeted serum levels above 40–60 ng/mL (requiring 4,000–8,000 IU/day in many individuals) tend to show stronger results. The dose-response relationship matters, and most trials have used doses far below what is needed to move most deficient individuals into the target range.

Flaw 3: Too Short Duration

Chronic disease outcomes take decades to develop. A 2-3 year supplementation trial may be insufficient to detect impact on cancer incidence or cardiovascular events, even if vitamin D status is genuinely causally related to these outcomes. The VITAL trial’s 5-year duration is among the longest — and even that may be inadequate to see effects on outcomes with 10-20 year latency periods.

What the Better Evidence Shows

When these design flaws are corrected — targeting deficient individuals, using adequate doses, measuring achieved serum levels — the picture improves. A 2019 meta-analysis restricted to trials achieving serum 25(OH)D above 40 ng/mL found significant reductions in cancer mortality. VITAL sub-group analyses show reduced cancer mortality in normal-weight participants. The D-HEALTH trial (60,000 IU monthly, 5 years) showed significant cardiovascular benefits. Vitamin D's biology remains plausible; most trials are just poorly designed to detect its effects.

Sources

  1. Manson JE, et al. "Vitamin D supplements and prevention of cancer and cardiovascular disease." New England Journal of Medicine, 2019.
  2. Garland CF, et al. "Meta-analysis of all-cause mortality according to serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D." American Journal of Public Health, 2014.
  3. Scragg R, et al. "Effect of monthly high-dose vitamin D supplementation on cardiovascular disease in the vitamin D assessment study." JAMA Cardiology, 2017.
Breakthrough

Saffron for Depression: Better Than You Think

Apr 12, 2026 · 7 min read

Saffron (Crocus sativus) is best known as an expensive culinary spice used in paella and risotto. It is considerably less well-known as one of the most clinically supported natural antidepressants, with a body of randomized trial evidence that substantially outperforms most herbal remedies and competes favorably with pharmaceutical antidepressants in mild-to-moderate depression. The disparity between clinical evidence quality and public awareness is striking.

The Clinical Evidence

A 2023 meta-analysis by Hausenblas et al. reviewed 23 randomized controlled trials (1,567 participants) examining saffron for depression and anxiety. Key findings: saffron significantly outperformed placebo in reducing depression scores (standard mean difference −0.99, a large effect); when compared head-to-head with antidepressants (fluoxetine, citalopram, imipramine), saffron showed equivalent efficacy (SMD −0.11, non-significant difference); and saffron produced significantly fewer adverse effects than pharmaceutical comparators. The effect sizes are larger than those seen in most supplement depression trials and comparable to the effect sizes of antidepressants over placebo.

Active Constituents and Mechanisms

Saffron’s active components are safranal and crocin — the carotenoid pigments responsible for its color and flavor. Research indicates these compounds inhibit serotonin reuptake (similar to SSRIs), inhibit NMDA receptor activity, and have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in the brain. The multi-modal mechanism may partially explain why it shows equivalent efficacy to single-mechanism drugs: it works on several pathways simultaneously. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation has also been observed, which is associated with antidepressant response.

Dose and Form

Clinical trials consistently use 30 mg/day of saffron extract standardized to safranal content — either as a single 30 mg dose or as 15 mg twice daily. This dose is not achievable from culinary use (cooking saffron destroys active compounds, and the quantities used in cooking are too small). Standardized supplements are necessary. Onset of effect in trials is typically 4–6 weeks, consistent with antidepressant timelines.

Who It’s For

Current evidence supports saffron as a first-line consideration for mild-to-moderate depression, particularly for individuals who prefer non-pharmaceutical interventions, those who have experienced significant side effects from SSRIs (particularly sexual dysfunction), or those who cannot access psychiatric care. It is not appropriate as sole treatment for severe depression or bipolar disorder. Always discuss depression treatment with a physician before substituting any supplement for prescribed medication.

Sources

  1. Hausenblas HA, et al. "Saffron (Crocus sativus L.) and major depressive disorder: a meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials." Journal of Integrative Medicine, 2013; updated analysis 2023.
  2. Lopresti AL, Drummond PD. "Saffron (Crocus sativus) for depression: a systematic review of clinical studies and examination of underlying antidepressant mechanisms of action." Human Psychopharmacology, 2014.
  3. Akhondzadeh S, et al. "Comparison of Crocus sativus L. and imipramine in the treatment of mild to moderate depression." BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2004.
Guide

The Traveler’s Supplement Kit: Evidence-Based Picks

Apr 12, 2026 · 8 min read

Travel creates specific physiological stressors — circadian disruption, novel pathogen exposure, dietary changes, and altered activity levels — that create specific supplement applications. Unlike most of the supplement market, several travel-related uses have strong, specific clinical evidence. This guide covers the interventions with the best evidence-to-cost ratio for travelers.

Melatonin for Jet Lag

Melatonin is the most evidence-backed supplement for jet lag. The Cochrane review (9 trials) concluded that melatonin is “remarkably effective in preventing or reducing jet lag” when taken at destination bedtime (0.5–5 mg). Lower doses (0.5–1 mg) work as well as higher doses for circadian shifting while producing less morning grogginess. The key timing: take it at your destination’s local bedtime for 3–4 days after arrival. Eastward travel (harder) benefits more than westward travel (easier). Do not take melatonin on the plane — take it at local bedtime after arrival.

Probiotics for Traveler’s Diarrhea Prevention

Traveler’s diarrhea affects 20–50% of visitors to high-risk regions. The most robust evidence is for Saccharomyces boulardii — a 2005 meta-analysis found it reduced traveler’s diarrhea incidence by approximately 21%. Starting 5 days before travel and continuing throughout the trip is the studied protocol. Lactobacillus acidophilus and the Lactobacillus rhamnosus + Bifidobacterium longum combination also have supportive evidence. The absolute risk reduction is modest; these probiotics reduce risk but do not eliminate it. Continue standard food and water safety practices.

Zinc Acetate for Cold Prevention

Air travel exposes travelers to crowded, recirculated air conditions that increase upper respiratory infection risk. Zinc lozenges (zinc acetate, 13–23 mg elemental zinc) taken within 24 hours of cold symptom onset reduce duration by approximately 33% based on Cochrane analysis. For prevention, the evidence is less consistent, but zinc supplementation at 15–25 mg/day during high-exposure periods may reduce incidence modestly. Zinc gluconate and sulfate show weaker evidence than zinc acetate specifically.

Vitamin D for Immune Function

Travel often involves reduced sun exposure (winter destinations, indoor activities) and potential dietary shifts. Maintaining vitamin D sufficiency (1,000–2,000 IU/day) supports immune function and is well tolerated. Not a dramatic intervention but a sensible baseline.

What Not to Bother With

Activated charcoal for food safety: ineffective for bacterial toxins, blocks medication absorption. Oregano oil for infection prevention: no human trial evidence. “Immune booster” mushroom blends: no specific evidence for travel-related immune support. Anti-altitude sickness supplements (ginkgo, etc.): evidence is weak and conflicting; acetazolamide (prescription) is effective if high-altitude illness is a genuine concern.

Sources

  1. Herxheimer A, Petrie KJ. "Melatonin for the prevention and treatment of jet lag." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2002.
  2. McFarland LV, et al. "Meta-analysis of probiotics for the prevention of traveler’s diarrhea." Travel Medicine and Infectious Disease, 2007.
  3. Hemilä H. "Zinc lozenges and the common cold: a meta-analysis comparing zinc acetate and zinc gluconate, and the role of zinc dosage." JRSM Open, 2017.
Safety Alert

Raw Thyroid Glandulars: The Dangerous Supplement Trend

Apr 12, 2026 · 7 min read

Raw desiccated thyroid glandular supplements — made from freeze-dried or dried animal thyroid tissue, typically bovine or porcine — are sold over the counter in health food stores and online as supplements for energy, metabolism, and thyroid support. Unlike prescription desiccated thyroid (such as Armour Thyroid), these products are not regulated as drugs, not standardized for hormone content, and not subject to the medical oversight that thyroid hormone replacement requires. They contain real thyroid hormones: primarily thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3).

The Hormone Content Problem

The FDA tested multiple raw thyroid glandular supplements in 2014 and found detectable levels of T4 and T3 in products that, as dietary supplements, are not supposed to contain active pharmaceutical hormones. T3 in particular is potent — prescription T3 (liothyronine) is dosed in micrograms precisely because small dose variations produce significant physiological effects. Supplement products are not subject to the same potency testing as pharmaceuticals. ConsumerLab and independent testing have found significant variation between stated and actual thyroid hormone content in glandular products.

Clinical Risks

Excess thyroid hormone causes iatrogenic hyperthyroidism: heart palpitations, atrial fibrillation (a dangerous arrhythmia), osteoporosis (T3 accelerates bone resorption), insomnia, tremor, anxiety, and weight loss. These effects occur even in people with normal thyroid function taking supplemental thyroid hormones. Case reports of atrial fibrillation, thyrotoxicosis, and severe cardiovascular events from OTC thyroid glandular supplements have been published in clinical literature. The American Thyroid Association explicitly warns against the use of these products.
