  1. Nordio M, Proietti E. "The combined therapy with myo-inositol and D-chiro-inositol reduces the risk of metabolic disease in PCOS overweight patients compared to myo-inositol supplementation alone." European Review for Medical and Pharmacological Sciences, 2012.
  2. Unfer V, et al. "Myo-inositol effects in women with PCOS: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials." Endocrine Connections, 2017.
  3. Facchinetti F, et al. "Results from the international consensus conference on myo-inositol and D-chiro-inositol in obstetrics and gynecology." Gynecological Endocrinology, 2019.
Guide

Chondroitin: Modest but Real Cartilage Protection Over Time

Apr 6, 2026 · 7 min read

Chondroitin sulfate is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan that, alongside collagen and aggrecan, forms the structural scaffold of articular cartilage. Oral chondroitin has been controversial in the US but remains a first-line osteoarthritis treatment in European guidelines (ESCEO, EULAR) — a difference driven largely by product quality and study design, not by irreconcilable evidence.

The Structure-Modifying Question

Unlike NSAIDs, chondroitin has shown disease-modifying effects in multiple MRI and radiographic trials. The 2017 CONCEPT trial (Reginster et al.) randomized 604 knee OA patients to pharmaceutical-grade chondroitin sulfate 800 mg/day, celecoxib 200 mg/day, or placebo over 6 months. Both active treatments outperformed placebo on pain and function, with chondroitin showing comparable efficacy to celecoxib. Longer trials (2 years) have shown slowing of joint space narrowing — a structural endpoint — with chondroitin that is not seen with NSAIDs.

Why Some Trials Fail

Much of the negative chondroitin literature, including parts of the GAIT trial, used low-purity or low-molecular-weight product that is functionally different from pharmaceutical-grade chondroitin sulfate. The US supplement market carries wide quality variation — some products contain 50% or less of labeled content. Europe requires a prescription-grade form (chondroitin sulfate sodium, 800 mg daily) that consistently outperforms supplement-grade material in head-to-head trials.

Combining with Glucosamine

The MOVES trial (2016) compared glucosamine 1,500 mg + chondroitin 1,200 mg to celecoxib in severe knee OA and found equivalent pain reduction at 6 months. The combination is more commonly used than either alone, though the incremental benefit of combining versus single-agent is modest.

Safety and Limitations

Chondroitin is well-tolerated, with rare GI upset. Onset of effect is slow — most trials show meaningful improvement by 2–3 months, with maximal benefit at 6 months. It is not appropriate for acute flares. Patients with severe OA (bone-on-bone) are unlikely to respond meaningfully; the intervention is most useful in mild-to-moderate OA where cartilage remains.

Sources

  1. Reginster JY, et al. "Pharmaceutical-grade chondroitin sulfate is as effective as celecoxib and superior to placebo in symptomatic knee osteoarthritis: the ChONdroitin versus CElecoxib versus Placebo Trial (CONCEPT)." Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2017.
  2. Hochberg MC, et al. "Combined chondroitin sulfate and glucosamine for painful knee osteoarthritis: MOVES trial." Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2016.
  3. Singh JA, et al. "Chondroitin for osteoarthritis." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2015.
Guide

Glucosamine HCl vs Sulfate: Does the Form Actually Matter for Joints?

Apr 5, 2026 · 6 min read

Glucosamine exists in two primary supplemental forms: glucosamine sulfate (GS) and glucosamine hydrochloride (GH). The form distinction has generated strong opinions, particularly because European guidelines explicitly favor glucosamine sulfate — while most US retail bottles contain glucosamine hydrochloride. The underlying chemistry and clinical data suggest the form difference is real but frequently misunderstood.

What the Molecules Actually Deliver

Both salts dissociate in the stomach and deliver free glucosamine to circulation. The practical difference is per-gram elemental glucosamine: glucosamine sulfate is typically stabilized with potassium or sodium chloride, meaning a 1,500 mg dose of "glucosamine sulfate" actually delivers about 1,178 mg of free glucosamine. Glucosamine hydrochloride is more concentrated: 1,500 mg of the HCl salt delivers about 1,250 mg of free glucosamine. If dosing is matched on elemental glucosamine, bioavailability is equivalent.

Why Sulfate Trials Outperform

Two long-term European trials (Reginster 2001; Pavelka 2002) showed that glucosamine sulfate at 1,500 mg/day over 3 years reduced joint space narrowing and delayed progression to joint replacement. Similar trials with glucosamine hydrochloride — notably portions of the US GAIT study — were negative. The difference is likely not the sulfate ion itself but rather the prescription-grade European formulations (Rottapharm) used in the sulfate studies, versus mixed-quality supplement-grade HCl used elsewhere.

The Sulfate Hypothesis

Some researchers argue the sulfate moiety itself provides a substrate for proteoglycan synthesis, but human pharmacokinetic studies show that oral sulfate contributes only minimally to joint tissue sulfate pools. Dietary sulfur amino acids (methionine, cysteine) dwarf any contribution from glucosamine sulfate. The clinical edge of sulfate products is better explained by formulation quality and consistent dosing than by a unique sulfate-mediated mechanism.

Practical Takeaway

If you have access to a stabilized, pharmaceutical-grade glucosamine sulfate product (common in Europe), that has the strongest trial record. In the US, a reputable glucosamine HCl product at 1,500 mg delivered glucosamine is a reasonable alternative. Avoid multi-ingredient "joint complex" products with sub-therapeutic glucosamine doses — these deliver less than half the studied amount and produce correspondingly smaller effects.

Sources

  1. Reginster JY, et al. "Long-term effects of glucosamine sulphate on osteoarthritis progression: a randomised, placebo-controlled clinical trial." Lancet, 2001.
  2. Pavelka K, et al. "Glucosamine sulfate use and delay of progression of knee osteoarthritis: a 3-year, randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind study." Archives of Internal Medicine, 2002.
  3. Clegg DO, et al. "Glucosamine, chondroitin sulfate, and the two in combination for painful knee osteoarthritis (GAIT)." New England Journal of Medicine, 2006.
Guide

Melatonin Dosing: Why 0.1 mg Often Outperforms 10 mg for Sleep

Apr 4, 2026 · 8 min read

Melatonin is the most over-dosed supplement in America. A 2023 analysis in JAMA of 25 melatonin gummies found actual content ranged from 74% to 347% of label. Simultaneously, the typical retail dose (3–10 mg) is 10–100× higher than what the pineal gland naturally produces at night. MIT sleep researcher Richard Wurtman's dose-response work from the 1990s established that physiologic doses (0.1–0.3 mg) reliably restore endogenous melatonin levels and improve sleep onset, while higher doses desensitize receptors and often produce inferior outcomes.

The Dose-Response Reversal

In Wurtman's controlled trials at MIT, 0.3 mg produced peak plasma melatonin within physiologic range and improved sleep latency. At 3 mg, plasma melatonin exceeded normal nighttime levels by 10× and remained elevated well into morning — blunting next-night endogenous production and producing a morning hangover effect. Higher doses did not produce better sleep; they produced persistent receptor saturation and worse circadian alignment.

When Higher Doses Do Help

There are specific contexts where 1–5 mg is appropriate: jet lag travel, shift work sleep disorder, and certain pediatric neurodevelopmental conditions under clinical supervision. For routine sleep onset issues in healthy adults, 0.3 mg is a better starting dose with a cleaner next-morning profile.

Timing Matters More Than Dose

Melatonin is a circadian signal, not a sedative. Its effect on sleep onset is modest (about 7–10 minutes in healthy adults); its effect on circadian phase-shifting is substantial. For sleep onset, timing is 30–60 minutes before bedtime. For advancing a delayed sleep phase (night owls), taking 0.5 mg 4–6 hours before desired bedtime shifts the circadian clock earlier with each successive night.

Extended-Release vs Immediate-Release

Immediate-release melatonin addresses sleep onset. Patients whose primary complaint is sleep maintenance (waking at 3am and unable to return to sleep) often do better with a low-dose extended-release formulation, as blood melatonin falls rapidly with immediate-release products. Prolonged-release melatonin 2 mg is approved as a prescription product for insomnia in adults over 55 in Europe (Circadin), where endogenous melatonin declines with age.

Safety Tail

Melatonin is remarkably safe acutely, but chronic high-dose use in children has been associated with delayed puberty signals in animal models and remains incompletely studied long-term. For adult sleep onset, start with 0.3 mg 30 minutes before desired sleep. Increase only if no effect after two weeks. Most users never need more.

Sources

  1. Zhdanova IV, et al. "Melatonin treatment for age-related insomnia." Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2001.
  2. Cohen PA, et al. "Quantity of melatonin and CBD in melatonin gummies sold in the US." JAMA, 2023.
  3. Ferracioli-Oda E, et al. "Meta-analysis: melatonin for the treatment of primary sleep disorders." PLoS One, 2013.
Breakthrough

Lactobacillus gasseri: The Probiotic Strain for Weight and Women's Health

Apr 3, 2026 · 7 min read

Most probiotic trials use generic multi-strain products and produce correspondingly generic outcomes. A handful of specific strains, however, have well-characterized clinical effects tied to documented mechanisms. Lactobacillus gasseri — particularly strains BNR17 and SBT2055 — is one of the better-documented examples, with controlled trials in adiposity reduction and gynecological health.

The Snow Brand Weight Trial

A 2013 double-blind RCT published in the British Journal of Nutrition by Kadooka et al. randomized 210 overweight Japanese adults to a fermented milk containing L. gasseri SBT2055 (10^10 CFU/day) or placebo for 12 weeks. The active group showed a 4.6% reduction in visceral fat area, 1.8% reduction in waist circumference, and modest reductions in body weight and BMI. Effects reversed after cessation, suggesting the mechanism is ongoing modulation of gut microbiota rather than permanent change.

Mechanism: Postbiotic Lipid Modulation

L. gasseri produces conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and bile salt hydrolase, which together alter lipid absorption and fecal fat excretion. It also reduces intestinal inflammation markers (serum LPS, TNF-α), which contribute to metabolic dysfunction in obesity. The effect size is modest — comparable to a well-executed diet intervention sustained for 8 weeks — but the safety and cost profile are favorable.

Bacterial Vaginosis and Urogenital Health

L. gasseri is one of the dominant species in healthy vaginal microbiota, and strain-specific formulations (often combined with L. rhamnosus) have shown benefit in recurrent bacterial vaginosis and urinary tract infections. A 2016 systematic review in the Journal of Lower Genital Tract Disease found that oral and vaginal probiotics containing L. gasseri or L. rhamnosus reduced BV recurrence by roughly 50% at 3–6 months.

Strain Specificity Matters

Not all L. gasseri strains produce the weight or urogenital effects seen in these trials. The studied strains are SBT2055 (weight), BNR17 (insulin sensitivity), and LN-101 / CRL-1320 (urogenital). Buying a generic "L. gasseri probiotic" without strain identification may miss the effect entirely. Reputable products name the specific strain on the label.

Sources

  1. Kadooka Y, et al. "Effect of Lactobacillus gasseri SBT2055 in fermented milk on abdominal adiposity in adults in a randomised controlled trial." British Journal of Nutrition, 2013.
  2. Kim J, et al. "Lactobacillus gasseri BNR17 supplementation reduces the visceral fat accumulation and waist circumference in obese adults: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial." Journal of Medicinal Food, 2018.
  3. Hanson L, et al. "Probiotics for treatment and prevention of urogenital infections in women: a systematic review." Journal of Midwifery & Women's Health, 2016.
Myth

Desiccated Beef Liver Supplements: Ancestral Trend or Legit Nutrition?

Apr 2, 2026 · 7 min read

Desiccated beef liver capsules have exploded in popularity among fitness and "ancestral" communities, marketed as a concentrated source of bioavailable vitamin A, B12, iron, choline, and copper. The marketing is not wrong about nutrient density — liver is the single most nutrient-dense food by caloric unit. The question is whether the capsule delivery vehicle and realistic dosing preserve those advantages, and whether the alleged benefits extend beyond what a standard multivitamin provides.

What's Actually in the Capsules

A typical serving of 6 capsules of freeze-dried beef liver (about 3 grams of powder, equivalent to ~15 g fresh liver) provides roughly: vitamin A 3,000–5,000 IU (retinol form), vitamin B12 15–20 µg, riboflavin 0.5 mg, iron 1–2 mg (heme iron), copper 1–2 mg, and choline 100–150 mg. This is a meaningful contribution for B12 and copper, but the iron dose is too small to treat deficiency, and the choline is one-fifth of the adequate intake.

The Retinol Advantage

Unlike multivitamins that often use beta-carotene, liver provides preformed retinol — directly usable by the body without requiring conversion (which is 15–45% efficient and impaired by common polymorphisms in the BCO1 gene). For people with poor carotene conversion, retinol from liver or egg yolk is functionally more useful than plant-source vitamin A.

The Toxicity Ceiling

Exceeding 10,000 IU/day of preformed retinol chronically is associated with bone demineralization and hepatotoxicity. People who combine liver capsules with vitamin A-fortified foods and a multivitamin can easily exceed the tolerable upper intake. Pregnancy is the highest-risk context: retinol is teratogenic above 10,000 IU/day. Conservative use is 1–3 capsules/day rather than the 6–8 some products suggest.

Compared to a Multivitamin

A well-formulated multivitamin delivers similar or greater amounts of the same nutrients at far lower cost and with more consistent dosing. Liver's real advantage is the food matrix — nutrients bound to endogenous proteins that the body evolved to process. Whether capsule-encapsulated freeze-dried powder preserves this advantage over tablet-form synthetic nutrients is plausible but not rigorously tested. Eating fresh liver once every 1–2 weeks accomplishes the same nutritional goal for most people at negligible cost.

Sources

  1. Green R, et al. "Vitamin B12 deficiency." Nature Reviews Disease Primers, 2017.
  2. Lietz G, et al. "Single nucleotide polymorphisms upstream from the beta-carotene 15,15-monooxygenase gene influence provitamin A conversion efficiency in female volunteers." Journal of Nutrition, 2012.
  3. Penniston KL, Tanumihardjo SA. "The acute and chronic toxic effects of vitamin A." American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2006.
Breakthrough

Bergamot Citrus Extract: Italy's Answer to High Cholesterol

Apr 1, 2026 · 7 min read

Bergamot (Citrus bergamia) is a hybrid citrus fruit native to the Calabria region of southern Italy. It contains a unique profile of polyphenolic flavonoids — particularly brutieridin and melitidin, the only known natural statin-like inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase. Over the past decade, Italian and Australian researchers have published a growing series of trials demonstrating meaningful lipid improvements with standardized bergamot extract (BPF, Bergamot Polyphenolic Fraction).

The Lipid Profile Evidence

A 2019 meta-analysis of 12 controlled trials (Lamiquiz-Moneo et al.) found that bergamot extract at 500–1,500 mg/day reduced total cholesterol by 25–30 mg/dL, LDL by 15–20 mg/dL, and triglycerides by 30–50 mg/dL, while raising HDL by 5–10 mg/dL. Effect sizes rivaled or exceeded low-dose statins in some trials, with more favorable effects on HDL and triglycerides than statins typically produce.

Mechanism: Multi-Target

Brutieridin and melitidin inhibit HMG-CoA reductase competitively — the same mechanism as statins, but more weakly per molecule. Bergamot's additional benefits likely come from AMPK activation (improving glucose and lipid metabolism), PON1 activity enhancement (protecting HDL from oxidation), and reduced hepatic de novo lipogenesis. The multi-target profile may explain why the combined effect is meaningful despite modest HMG-CoA reductase inhibition.

Statin Adjunct or Alternative

A 2013 trial by Gliozzi et al. showed that adding bergamot 1,000 mg/day to rosuvastatin 10 mg produced greater LDL lowering than rosuvastatin 20 mg monotherapy — making bergamot an option for statin dose reduction in patients experiencing muscle side effects. As monotherapy, bergamot is reasonable for patients with mild-to-moderate hypercholesterolemia who prefer not to take statins, though it should not replace statins for established cardiovascular disease.

Standardization and Safety

Effective products are standardized to 40% polyphenols or contain a defined "BPF" fraction. Generic "bergamot extract" without standardization may deliver inconsistent potency. Bergamot is well-tolerated, with rare GI upset. Because it affects lipid metabolism, patients should monitor lipids at 8–12 weeks and inform clinicians before using it with prescription lipid-modifying drugs.

Sources

  1. Mollace V, et al. "Hypolipemic and hypoglycaemic activity of bergamot polyphenols: from animal models to human studies." Fitoterapia, 2011.
  2. Lamiquiz-Moneo I, et al. "Effect of bergamot on lipid profile in humans: a systematic review." Critical Reviews in Food Science and Nutrition, 2020.
  3. Gliozzi M, et al. "Bergamot polyphenolic fraction enhances rosuvastatin-induced effect on LDL-cholesterol, LOX-1 expression and protein kinase B phosphorylation in patients with hyperlipidemia." International Journal of Cardiology, 2013.
Guide

Hydroxocobalamin vs Methylcobalamin: Which B12 Form Is Actually Best?

Mar 31, 2026 · 7 min read

Vitamin B12 exists in four forms relevant to supplementation: cyanocobalamin (synthetic), hydroxocobalamin (natural bacterial form), methylcobalamin (one active form used in methylation), and adenosylcobalamin (the other active form used in mitochondria). Marketing around "active" methylcobalamin has created the impression that it is the only correct choice. The clinical data are more nuanced.

How the Body Handles Each Form

All oral B12 forms are absorbed via the intrinsic factor pathway or, at high doses, passive diffusion (about 1% of dose). Once absorbed, the body interconverts between methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin as needed via MMACHC and other enzymes. Cyanocobalamin must first have its cyanide group removed and be reduced to cob(II)alamin — a minor metabolic step that is efficient in healthy adults but inefficient in smokers (who consume the cyanide detoxification capacity on tobacco smoke) and certain rare enzymatic disorders.

Hydroxocobalamin: The Underrated Workhorse

Hydroxocobalamin is the natural bacterial form and the preferred parenteral B12 in Europe. It has the longest plasma half-life of any B12 form (about 6 weeks versus 1 week for cyanocobalamin), binds plasma proteins tightly, and is the gold-standard injection form for pernicious anemia. Oral hydroxocobalamin is less common in the US but is available and clinically interchangeable with methylcobalamin for most purposes.

When Methylcobalamin Specifically Helps

For diabetic or alcoholic neuropathy, methylcobalamin at high doses (1,500–5,000 µg/day) has shown some specific benefit over cyanocobalamin in a handful of trials, possibly reflecting its direct role in methylation pathways critical to myelin maintenance. The practical effect size is modest. For general B12 repletion in deficiency, all four forms work; the binding and storage matter more than the form.

MTHFR and the Methyl Debate

MTHFR polymorphisms affect folate metabolism, not B12 metabolism directly. The recommendation to use methylcobalamin specifically for MTHFR carriers is not strongly evidence-based. What does help MTHFR-variant individuals is using methylfolate (5-MTHF) instead of synthetic folic acid. B12 form selection is secondary.

Practical Recommendation

For routine supplementation, any of the four forms at 500–1,000 µg/day is sufficient. For documented deficiency, methyl- or hydroxocobalamin at 1,000–2,000 µg/day is appropriate. For neuropathy, methylcobalamin at higher doses has modestly better evidence. Avoid getting anxious about B12 form selection — it is one of the lower-stakes decisions in supplementation.

Sources

  1. Thakkar K, Billa G. "Treatment of vitamin B12 deficiency — methylcobalamine? cyancobalamine? hydroxocobalamin? — clearing the confusion." European Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2015.
  2. Sun Y, et al. "Efficacy of methylcobalamin on peripheral nerve function in patients with diabetic peripheral neuropathy: a meta-analysis." Diabetes Research and Clinical Practice, 2018.
  3. Obeid R, et al. "Vitamin B12 supplementation: a systematic review of various oral and parenteral formulations." Nutrients, 2015.
Guide

TTFD/Allithiamine: The Fat-Soluble Thiamine That Gets Into the Brain

Mar 30, 2026 · 6 min read

Thiamine (vitamin B1) is water-soluble and actively transported into cells via the THTR1 and THTR2 transporters. When these transporters are saturated, near the 5 mg single-dose ceiling, additional oral thiamine is excreted without effect. Two lipid-modified derivatives — TTFD (thiamine tetrahydrofurfuryl disulfide, "allithiamine") and benfotiamine — bypass this limitation and allow much higher intracellular thiamine loading.

TTFD's Origin and Mechanism

TTFD was developed in Japan in the 1960s after researchers noted that Japanese garlic and rice bran extracts produced persistent thiamine-like effects not explained by water-soluble thiamine alone. The lipid-soluble disulfide form passively diffuses across cell membranes, including the blood-brain barrier, and is reduced intracellularly to free thiamine. Clinically, TTFD produces tissue thiamine concentrations unattainable with conventional thiamine.

Neurological Applications

TTFD has been studied in conditions marked by impaired cerebral thiamine metabolism: pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency, subacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy (Leigh syndrome), and chronic fatigue spectrum disorders. Case series and small open-label trials have reported meaningful symptom improvement in subsets of patients, though randomized evidence is limited. The fact that CNS thiamine deficiency is under-recognized in dysautonomia, POTS, and some migraines has led to wider clinical use than the trial data alone would warrant.

Benfotiamine: The Diabetic Complications Cousin

Benfotiamine (S-benzoylthiamine O-monophosphate) is another lipid-soluble thiamine derivative with stronger evidence in diabetic neuropathy. A 2012 meta-analysis showed meaningful improvements in neuropathy symptoms at 300–600 mg/day over 6 weeks. Unlike TTFD, benfotiamine does not cross the blood-brain barrier efficiently — it acts primarily in peripheral tissues.

Dosing and Caveats

TTFD is typically used at 50–200 mg/day with fat-containing meals. A transient "paradoxical reaction" — worsening fatigue or anxiety for 1–3 weeks — is described in individuals with chronic low thiamine status as cellular metabolism rapidly repolarizes. Starting at very low doses (10–25 mg) and titrating helps avoid this. TTFD is well-tolerated long-term but has a mild sulfurous odor noted by some users.

Sources

  1. Lonsdale D. "A review of the biochemistry, metabolism and clinical benefits of thiamin(e) and its derivatives." Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine, 2006.
  2. Volvert ML, et al. "Benfotiamine, a synthetic S-acyl thiamine derivative, has different mechanisms of action and a different pharmacological profile than lipid-soluble thiamine disulfide derivatives." BMC Pharmacology, 2008.
  3. Stracke H, et al. "Benfotiamine in diabetic polyneuropathy (BENDIP): results of a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical study." Experimental and Clinical Endocrinology & Diabetes, 2008.
Breakthrough

5-Aminolevulinic Acid: The Mitochondrial Compound for Blood Sugar Control

Mar 29, 2026 · 7 min read

5-Aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) is a non-proteinogenic amino acid and the rate-limiting precursor for heme biosynthesis. It is produced in mitochondria from glycine and succinyl-CoA, and is the foundational building block for hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochromes, and catalase. In recent years, 5-ALA supplementation (usually paired with iron for stoichiometric support of heme synthesis) has gained attention for its effects on glucose metabolism and cellular energetics.

The Japanese Trials on Glucose Tolerance

Several randomized controlled trials from Japan and Bahrain have examined 5-ALA + sodium ferrous citrate on prediabetes and type 2 diabetes. A 2013 trial by Higashikawa et al. gave 50 mg 5-ALA + 57 mg sodium ferrous citrate daily for 12 weeks, finding reductions in HbA1c (−0.15 percentage points) and fasting glucose in prediabetic adults. A 2017 Bahraini trial showed similar effects in type 2 diabetes when added to standard care. Effect sizes are modest but consistent.

Mechanism: Mitochondrial Cytochrome Loading

5-ALA supplementation increases heme synthesis, which supports cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) and other mitochondrial cytochrome proteins. Improved electron transport efficiency enhances cellular ATP production and may improve insulin sensitivity by addressing mitochondrial dysfunction in skeletal muscle and pancreatic beta cells — a documented feature of type 2 diabetes. The iron co-supplementation is necessary because 5-ALA without iron can deplete body iron stores during the synthesis of heme.

Emerging Use Cases Beyond Glucose

5-ALA is also the active compound in photodynamic therapy for actinic keratosis and certain cancers (it accumulates preferentially in malignant tissue and is activated by light). Oral dosing for general health is at much lower concentrations than PDT protocols. Preliminary work examines 5-ALA for mitochondrial disorders and long COVID-associated fatigue, though controlled trials are not yet conclusive.

Safety

At supplement doses (25–100 mg/day), 5-ALA is well-tolerated. Temporary photosensitivity has been reported at higher doses. Patients with porphyria or hemochromatosis should avoid 5-ALA due to heme pathway and iron metabolism concerns. Co-dosing with ferrous citrate is standard; excess iron intake should be monitored.

Sources

  1. Higashikawa F, et al. "5-aminolevulinic acid, a precursor of heme, reduces both fasting and postprandial glucose levels in mildly hyperglycemic subjects." Nutrition, 2013.
  2. Al-Saber F, et al. "The safety and tolerability of 5-aminolevulinic acid phosphate with sodium ferrous citrate in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus in Bahrain." Journal of Diabetes Research, 2016.
  3. Hara T, et al. "5-Aminolevulinic acid in combination with ferrous ion reduces adipocyte differentiation and lipid accumulation in 3T3-L1 cells." Plant Foods for Human Nutrition, 2018.
Guide

CoQ10 Ubiquinone vs Ubiquinol: When Should You Upgrade?

Mar 28, 2026 · 7 min read

CoQ10 exists in the body in two interconverting redox states: ubiquinone (oxidized) and ubiquinol (reduced). Supplements are available in both forms, and ubiquinol products typically cost 2–3× more. The marketing claim is that ubiquinol is "pre-converted" and therefore more bioavailable. The reality is more conditional.

How Absorption Actually Works

Both ubiquinone and ubiquinol are absorbed via passive diffusion and chylomicron transport — a process highly dependent on dietary fat. Taking either form without a meal containing fat reduces absorption dramatically. In healthy adults under 50, ubiquinone absorption is efficient, and the liver reduces circulating CoQ10 to ubiquinol (which is the predominant plasma form regardless of what you took).

Where Ubiquinol Wins

In adults over 60 or 70, the reductase enzymes that convert ubiquinone to ubiquinol decline in activity. Several studies have shown that at matched doses, ubiquinol produces higher total plasma CoQ10 in older adults (60–80% higher than ubiquinone in some comparisons). In statin-treated patients with muscle symptoms, ubiquinol at 100–200 mg/day has outperformed ubiquinone in small trials. In congestive heart failure, the Q-SYMBIO trial used ubiquinone 300 mg/day — but more recent trials increasingly specify ubiquinol at 200 mg/day.

Where Ubiquinone Is Fine

For healthy adults under 50 taking CoQ10 for general mitochondrial support or migraine prevention (at 100–400 mg/day), ubiquinone is perfectly adequate. Ubiquinol offers no clinically detectable advantage in this population, and the cost difference is not justified by the data.

Formulation Issues

Ubiquinol is chemically unstable and re-oxidizes in air, so quality-controlled softgels with inert atmosphere packaging matter. Poorly manufactured ubiquinol products may contain significant oxidized content, negating the form advantage. Reputable manufacturers provide third-party verification of ubiquinol stability.

Practical Rule

Under 50 and healthy: ubiquinone. Over 60 or with statin-associated muscle symptoms, heart failure, or documented CoQ10 deficiency: ubiquinol. In both cases, take with the fattiest meal of the day and be patient — plasma levels reach steady state in 2–3 weeks.

Sources

  1. Mortensen SA, et al. "The effect of coenzyme Q10 on morbidity and mortality in chronic heart failure: results from Q-SYMBIO." JACC: Heart Failure, 2014.
  2. Langsjoen PH, Langsjoen AM. "Supplemental ubiquinol in patients with advanced congestive heart failure." BioFactors, 2008.
  3. Hosoe K, et al. "Study on safety and bioavailability of ubiquinol (Kaneka QH) after single and 4-week multiple oral administration to healthy volunteers." Regulatory Toxicology and Pharmacology, 2007.
Guide

L-Leucine: The Amino Acid That Flips the Muscle-Building Switch

Mar 27, 2026 · 7 min read

Leucine is one of three branched-chain amino acids, but it occupies a unique regulatory role: it is the primary molecular trigger for muscle protein synthesis via activation of the mTORC1 signaling pathway. Reaching a threshold concentration of plasma leucine ("leucine threshold") after a meal is what initiates a bout of muscle-building. Protein quantity and source matter precisely because they determine whether that threshold is crossed.

The 2.5–3g Threshold

Studies by Luc van Loon, Stuart Phillips, and others have converged on a leucine requirement of about 2.5–3 grams per meal to maximally stimulate muscle protein synthesis in young adults. This corresponds to roughly 20–25 grams of high-quality protein (whey, eggs, meat) or 35–40 grams of plant protein. Older adults ("anabolic resistance") require roughly 40% more leucine per meal to achieve the same effect — closer to 4 grams.

Leucine Supplementation vs Protein

Adding 2–3 grams of leucine to a suboptimal protein meal (under 20 g protein) restores maximal muscle protein synthesis in older adults. This is clinically useful for individuals with poor appetite, early satiety, or plant-based diets where reaching leucine thresholds per meal is harder. In contrast, adding leucine on top of a meal that already contains 30+ grams of high-quality protein produces no additional benefit — the threshold has already been crossed.

BCAAs vs Leucine Alone

Branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) supplements contain leucine, isoleucine, and valine in a typical 2:1:1 ratio. For muscle protein synthesis, the isoleucine and valine contribute little; leucine does the work. BCAA supplements are essentially low-dose leucine supplements with extra cost. If you want leucine, buy leucine — unless you are seeking BCAA-specific effects on central fatigue during endurance exercise (a separate and weaker application).

The EAA Question

Leucine alone cannot build muscle without all nine essential amino acids being available. In fed conditions with adequate protein intake, this is never an issue. In fasted or under-fueled conditions, supplementing leucine alone can temporarily activate mTOR without providing substrates for synthesis, which is inefficient. Essential amino acid (EAA) blends avoid this limitation.

Practical Dose

2–3 g leucine added to meals that fall short of 20 g high-quality protein. 3–5 g post-workout if protein timing is delayed. Do not exceed 10 g/day — high leucine loads can crowd out other BCAAs at shared transporters, theoretically producing imbalances.

Sources

  1. Wall BT, et al. "Leucine co-ingestion improves post-prandial muscle protein accretion in elderly men." Clinical Nutrition, 2013.
  2. Churchward-Venne TA, et al. "Supplementation of a suboptimal protein dose with leucine or essential amino acids: effects on myofibrillar protein synthesis at rest and following resistance exercise in men." Journal of Physiology, 2012.
  3. Phillips SM. "A brief review of critical processes in exercise-induced muscular hypertrophy." Sports Medicine, 2014.
Safety

Vinpocetine: Brain Booster or Unapproved Drug? The FDA Ruling Explained

Mar 26, 2026 · 7 min read

Vinpocetine is a semi-synthetic derivative of vincamine (from Vinca minor). It has been sold as a prescription drug for cognitive impairment in Eastern Europe (Cavinton) since the 1970s, and simultaneously as an over-the-counter nootropic in the US. In 2019, the FDA clarified that vinpocetine does not meet the legal definition of a dietary ingredient and should not be sold as a supplement — creating a regulatory limbo that continues to shape the US market.

The FDA Position

The Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (1994) allows supplements to contain substances marketed as dietary ingredients before that date. Vinpocetine was first synthesized and marketed as a drug, not as a food ingredient, and the FDA concluded it never qualified as a dietary ingredient. In 2019 the agency formally stated that vinpocetine products marketed as supplements are "adulterated" under the Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act. Enforcement has been inconsistent, and products remain widely available.

What the Clinical Data Show

Randomized trials of vinpocetine at 15–30 mg/day in vascular cognitive impairment have shown modest improvements in cognitive test scores and cerebral blood flow. Mechanisms include PDE1 inhibition, sodium channel modulation, and cerebral vasodilation. In healthy adults without cognitive impairment, the effect on memory or attention is small and inconsistent — the evidence supports a legitimate but narrow clinical role, not a general nootropic benefit.

Pregnancy: The Serious Concern

A 2019 NIH review of vinpocetine's reproductive safety concluded that vinpocetine can cause miscarriage and fetal harm at doses comparable to recommended supplemental doses, based on animal data and mechanistic considerations. The FDA specifically warned that vinpocetine should not be used by women who are or could become pregnant. Labels rarely make this warning prominent.

What the User Should Know

Vinpocetine is pharmacologically active and not a typical supplement ingredient. Adults considering it should treat it like a medication: consider interactions with blood thinners (it has mild antiplatelet activity), avoid during pregnancy, and discuss with a clinician if taken chronically. The narrow clinical utility (mainly vascular cognitive impairment in older adults under medical supervision) does not justify broad OTC use by healthy adults seeking memory enhancement.

Sources

  1. US Food and Drug Administration. "FDA statement on warning for women of childbearing age about possible safety risks of dietary supplements containing vinpocetine." 2019.
  2. Szakall S, et al. "Cerebral effects of a single dose of intravenous vinpocetine in chronic stroke patients: a PET study." Journal of Neuroimaging, 1998.
  3. Szatmari SZ, Whitehouse PJ. "Vinpocetine for cognitive impairment and dementia." Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2003.
Breakthrough

Acacia Fiber: The Gentle Prebiotic That Even Sensitive Guts Tolerate

Mar 25, 2026 · 6 min read

Most popular prebiotic fibers — inulin, FOS, GOS — are rapidly fermentable and produce gas, bloating, and urgency in a substantial minority of users, particularly those with IBS. Acacia fiber (gum arabic, from Acacia senegal) is structurally a complex arabinogalactan polysaccharide that is fermented slowly and distally along the colon, producing the prebiotic benefits without the rapid gas generation.

The Low-FODMAP Friendly Prebiotic

A 2008 trial in Applied and Environmental Microbiology (Calame et al.) showed that 10 g/day of acacia fiber for 4 weeks increased fecal Bifidobacteria and Lactobacillus counts comparably to inulin, while producing significantly less flatulence and abdominal discomfort. A 2012 IBS trial found that acacia fiber reduced symptom severity and normalized bowel function in IBS-D and mixed-type IBS without exacerbating gas symptoms. This profile makes it one of the few prebiotics tolerated on low-FODMAP protocols.

Constipation and Stool Quality

Acacia fiber is moderately water-soluble but forms a viscous gel that improves stool consistency without the osmotic effect of psyllium. For chronic idiopathic constipation, 10–20 g/day produces measurable improvement in stool frequency and Bristol score within 2–3 weeks. Unlike stimulant laxatives, it has no tolerance issues with long-term use.

Metabolic and Satiety Effects

Studies in obese adults have shown modest reductions in BMI, waist circumference, and postprandial glucose with 30 g/day over 6 weeks. The satiety mechanism is likely delayed gastric emptying and increased production of short-chain fatty acids (particularly butyrate) that signal fullness through GLP-1. Effect sizes are modest compared to pharmacological weight interventions but are clinically meaningful as dietary adjuncts.

Practical Dosing

Start with 5 g/day mixed into water, coffee, or food (it is tasteless and dissolves clear). Increase gradually to 10–20 g/day over 2 weeks to minimize transient bloating. Acacia fiber does not interact significantly with medications but, like any fiber, should be dosed at least 2 hours apart from medications where absorption timing matters.

Sources

  1. Calame W, et al. "Gum arabic establishes prebiotic functionality in healthy human volunteers in a dose-dependent manner." British Journal of Nutrition, 2008.
  2. Min YW, et al. "Effect of composite yogurt enriched with acacia fiber and bifidobacterium lactis on gut function in healthy adults with functional constipation: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial." World Journal of Gastroenterology, 2012.
  3. Babiker R, et al. "Effects of gum arabic ingestion on body mass index and body fat percentage in healthy adult females: two-arm randomized, placebo controlled, double-blind trial." Nutrition Journal, 2012.
Myth

Fucoxanthin: What the Brown Seaweed Weight Loss Research Actually Shows

Mar 24, 2026 · 7 min read

Fucoxanthin is a carotenoid found exclusively in brown seaweeds (Undaria pinnatifida, wakame; Laminaria, kombu). It gained attention around 2010 after Japanese animal studies showed that fucoxanthin increased UCP1 expression in white adipose tissue — essentially inducing "browning" of white fat and increasing thermogenesis. This mechanism is attractive, and marketing picked it up aggressively. Human data have not caught up to the marketing.

The Abidov Trial

The most-cited human trial is a 2010 study by Abidov et al. in Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism that randomized obese non-diabetic women to fucoxanthin 2.4 mg/day combined with pomegranate seed oil. After 16 weeks, the treatment group lost 5.5 kg versus 1.5 kg in placebo. The effect is larger than most pharmacological interventions. However: this trial was industry-funded, used a proprietary combination product, and has not been independently replicated. Subsequent smaller trials with fucoxanthin alone have shown more modest effects.

Mechanism vs Human Bioavailability

Rodent studies use doses equivalent to 50–100 mg/kg of fucoxanthin, which would translate to 3–7 grams per day in humans — far beyond what any commercial product provides. Human oral bioavailability of fucoxanthin is only 0.1–1% of dose, with most converted rapidly to fucoxanthinol (the active metabolite). Standard supplement doses of 1–4 mg fucoxanthin produce plasma concentrations that may be too low to meaningfully activate UCP1 in adipose tissue.

What Fucoxanthin Probably Does

The replicable human effects of fucoxanthin supplementation are modest: slight improvements in liver fat in NAFLD patients, small reductions in fasting insulin, and antioxidant activity. These are worthwhile but not the transformative weight loss marketing implies. For weight management specifically, evidence is weaker than for established tools (diet, exercise, GLP-1 agonists for those who qualify).

Safety Notes

Fucoxanthin is generally well-tolerated but is concentrated from brown seaweeds which can carry iodine. High iodine intake can disrupt thyroid function in susceptible individuals. Products standardized to fucoxanthin content with controlled iodine are preferable. Avoid combining with thyroid medications without clinical supervision.

Sources

  1. Abidov M, et al. "The effects of Xanthigen in the weight management of obese premenopausal women with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and normal liver fat." Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2010.
  2. Maeda H, et al. "Fucoxanthin from edible seaweed, Undaria pinnatifida, shows antiobesity effect through UCP1 expression in white adipose tissues." Biochemical and Biophysical Research Communications, 2005.
  3. Hu X, et al. "Fucoxanthin: a promising compound for human inflammation-related diseases." International Journal of Molecular Sciences, 2022.
Guide

Ferulic Acid: The Plant Antioxidant in Your Coffee Worth Supplementing?

Mar 23, 2026 · 6 min read

Ferulic acid is a hydroxycinnamic acid found at meaningful concentrations in whole grains (especially brown rice and wheat bran), coffee, and many vegetables. It is one of the most abundant plant phenolic compounds in the Western diet, contributing substantially to total dietary antioxidant capacity. Supplemental ferulic acid has become popular as a longevity and skin-health ingredient, though the oral evidence lags the topical evidence.

Topical Evidence Is Stronger

The best-replicated human application of ferulic acid is dermatological. Duke University dermatology research in the 2000s showed that adding 0.5% ferulic acid to topical vitamin C and vitamin E products stabilizes the formulation and roughly doubles its photoprotective effect. The "C+E+Ferulic" combination remains a standard in dermatology. This is a cosmeceutical application with meaningful evidence, distinct from systemic oral supplementation.

Oral Bioavailability

Oral ferulic acid is well-absorbed (absolute bioavailability ~50%) but rapidly glucuronidated and sulfated, resulting in short systemic exposure. The metabolites retain some antioxidant activity. Doses used in human trials range from 250 to 1,000 mg/day, typically showing modest reductions in markers of oxidative stress (malondialdehyde, 8-OHdG) and improvements in endothelial function in small studies.

Metabolic and Cardiovascular Signals

Small trials in type 2 diabetes have shown improvements in fasting glucose, HbA1c, and lipid markers with 500–1,000 mg/day over 8–12 weeks. A 2018 trial combined ferulic acid with curcumin and showed meaningful reductions in LDL and triglycerides. Effect sizes are modest compared to pharmacological options but consistent with its role as a dietary antioxidant at supra-dietary doses.

Where It Fits

Ferulic acid is a reasonable supporting antioxidant in a broader polyphenol strategy (alongside rosmarinic acid, quercetin, resveratrol). It is unlikely to produce dramatic effects as monotherapy. For skin, topical combinations are better-supported than oral supplementation. For systemic effects, dietary intake from coffee, whole grains, and polyphenol-rich produce is the foundation; supplementation is additive rather than foundational.

Sources

  1. Lin FH, et al. "Ferulic acid stabilizes a solution of vitamins C and E and doubles its photoprotection of skin." Journal of Investigative Dermatology, 2005.
  2. Bumrungpert A, et al. "Ferulic acid supplementation improves lipid profiles, oxidative stress, and inflammatory status in hyperlipidemic subjects: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial." Nutrients, 2018.
  3. Mancuso C, Santangelo R. "Ferulic acid: pharmacological and toxicological aspects." Food and Chemical Toxicology, 2014.
Guide

Honokiol: The Anxiolytic Compound Hidden in Magnolia Bark

Mar 22, 2026 · 7 min read

Honokiol is a bioactive lignan extracted from the bark of Magnolia officinalis, used for over a thousand years in Traditional Chinese Medicine for anxiety, sleep disturbance, and digestive complaints. Modern pharmacology has identified honokiol as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, distinct from the benzodiazepine site. This gives it anxiolytic effects without the dependence and tolerance associated with benzodiazepines.

Mechanism: Non-Benzodiazepine GABAergic

Honokiol binds to GABA-A receptors at a site separate from benzodiazepine, barbiturate, or alcohol binding sites. This position confers anxiolytic and mild sedative effects without the amnesia, motor incoordination, or respiratory depression associated with classical GABA-A drugs. Preclinical studies also identify interactions with adenosine receptors and sigma-1 receptors, potentially contributing to broader neuroprotective effects.

Clinical Trials

Several small human trials of standardized magnolia bark extract (containing honokiol and magnolol) have shown reductions in perceived stress, cortisol, and mild anxiety symptoms at 250–500 mg/day of the combined extract (typically standardized to 2% honokiol, 1% magnolol). A 2013 trial combined magnolia bark with phellodendron in overweight premenopausal women and showed significant reductions in transient stress and salivary cortisol over 4 weeks. Effects are modest but reproducible.

Sleep and Cancer Research

Honokiol shortens sleep latency and increases non-REM sleep in animal models, with some preliminary human data supporting sleep onset improvement. In oncology research, honokiol has been investigated for potential direct antitumor activity through NF-κB inhibition and apoptosis induction, but these findings are preclinical and should not inform supplement use in cancer patients.

Dosing and Interactions

Standard use is 250–500 mg magnolia bark extract 1–2 times daily, with honokiol content of 10–20 mg per dose. Onset is within 30–60 minutes, duration 4–6 hours. Because of GABAergic activity, honokiol potentiates alcohol, benzodiazepines, and other sedatives — these combinations should be avoided. It should not be used in pregnancy and should be discontinued 2 weeks before surgery.

Sources

  1. Woodbury A, et al. "Neuro-modulating effects of honokiol: a review." Frontiers in Neurology, 2013.
  2. Kalman DS, et al. "Effect of a proprietary Magnolia and Phellodendron extract on stress levels in healthy women: a pilot, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial." Nutrition Journal, 2008.
  3. Talbott SM, et al. "Effect of Magnolia officinalis and Phellodendron amurense (Relora) on cortisol and psychological mood state in moderately stressed subjects." Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition, 2013.
Breakthrough

Kaempferol: The Flavonoid Activating Autophagy and Longevity Pathways

Mar 21, 2026 · 7 min read

Kaempferol is a flavonoid found in high concentrations in kale, spinach, capers, broccoli, endive, tea, and saffron. Over the past decade, kaempferol has attracted attention in longevity research for its ability to activate autophagy — the cellular recycling process that declines with age — and to modulate SIRT1, AMPK, and NRF2 signaling pathways associated with healthspan.

The Autophagy Signal

A series of cell and animal studies have shown that kaempferol induces autophagy through mTORC1 inhibition and TFEB (master regulator of lysosomal biogenesis) activation. In C. elegans, kaempferol extends lifespan by roughly 15% through this mechanism. Mammalian data are more limited but include reduced beta-amyloid accumulation in Alzheimer mouse models, reduced adipose tissue inflammation, and improved glucose tolerance.

Human Epidemiology

Large observational cohorts (including the Nurses' Health Study and EPIC) have associated higher dietary kaempferol intake with lower cardiovascular mortality, lower pancreatic and gastric cancer risk, and slower age-related cognitive decline. Effect sizes in observational data are 15–30% risk reductions at the highest intake quintile, comparable to other flavonoid classes. Causality cannot be established from observational data alone, but the consistency across populations is notable.

Bioavailability Challenges

Like many flavonoids, kaempferol has poor oral bioavailability (1–3%) due to rapid glucuronidation. Food matrices and gut microbiota substantially modify absorption. Co-ingestion with fats, piperine, and quercetin can enhance bioavailability. Formulated products using phytosome or liposomal delivery achieve 5–10× higher plasma concentrations than crystalline kaempferol.

Dosing and Practical Considerations

Typical supplemental doses are 50–200 mg/day of kaempferol or, more commonly, kaempferol-rich plant extracts (saffron, ginkgo, kale concentrate). Dietary intake of 10–15 mg/day is achievable through 1–2 servings of kaempferol-rich vegetables. Kaempferol mildly inhibits CYP enzymes; caution in patients on narrow-therapeutic-index drugs. Because evidence is strongest for food-based intake, kaempferol is best positioned as part of a polyphenol-rich diet with supplementation as an optional add-on.

Sources

  1. Calderon-Montano JM, et al. "A review on the dietary flavonoid kaempferol." Mini-Reviews in Medicinal Chemistry, 2011.
  2. Filomeni G, et al. "Neuroprotection of kaempferol by autophagy in models of rotenone-mediated acute toxicity: possible implications for Parkinson's disease." Neurobiology of Aging, 2012.
  3. Knekt P, et al. "Flavonoid intake and risk of chronic diseases." American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2002.
Myth

Pine Pollen: Testosterone Booster Claim vs Allergy Risk Reality

Mar 20, 2026 · 6 min read

Pine pollen — particularly from Pinus massoniana and Pinus sylvestris — has been sold in the wellness market as a natural testosterone booster since roughly 2010. The marketing rests on two observations: pine pollen contains trace amounts of plant sterols structurally similar to testosterone (testosterone, androstenedione, DHEA), and traditional Chinese medicine has used pine pollen in tonic formulations for centuries. The gap between those facts and the testosterone-boosting claim is substantial.

The Actual Androgen Content

Analytical studies measuring pine pollen androgens find testosterone concentrations of 0.1–0.8 µg per gram of pollen. A daily dose of 2–5 grams (typical of powder products) would deliver 0.2–4 µg of testosterone — approximately 1/1,000th to 1/10,000th of the daily endogenous production in an adult male. Any systemic effect from this amount is pharmacologically implausible, and studies testing pine pollen on serum testosterone have been negative.

What Pine Pollen Does Provide

Pine pollen is genuinely nutrient-dense: it contains amino acids, B vitamins, zinc, magnesium, and bioactive flavonoids including rutin, quercetin, and kaempferol. This makes it a reasonable nutritional supplement for antioxidant support and general nutrition. The problem is only the testosterone marketing — the underlying product is a decent food-form multinutrient, not a hormone product.

Allergy Risk

Pine pollen is a known respiratory allergen, and ingestion can trigger allergic reactions in sensitized individuals. Tincture products (alcohol extracts) remove most protein allergens; powder products retain them. Individuals with tree pollen allergies should avoid pine pollen supplements or, at minimum, start with very low doses of a tincture form.

The Honest Use Case

If you like the idea of a food-form polyphenol + trace nutrient supplement and are not allergic to pine pollen, it is reasonable. If you are taking it to raise testosterone, the evidence does not support that use. For testosterone support, the interventions with measurable effect are resistance training, sleep optimization, weight loss in overweight men, vitamin D if deficient, and zinc if deficient. Pine pollen does not displace any of these.

Sources

  1. Saden-Krehula M, et al. "Testosterone, epitestosterone and androstenedione in the pollen of Scotch pine P. sylvestris L." Experientia, 1971.
  2. Xi SY, et al. "Anti-aging and antioxidant activities of pine pollen polysaccharides extracted by different methods." International Journal of Biological Macromolecules, 2020.
  3. Mao GX, et al. "Protective role of a new polysaccharide extracted from Lonicera japonica Thunb. in mice with ulcerative colitis induced by dextran sulphate sodium." Biomed Research International, 2020.
Myth

Taking NAD+ Directly vs NMN and NR: Why the Precursors Win Every Time

Mar 19, 2026 · 7 min read

As NAD+ research has entered mainstream longevity discussion, a predictable retail response has emerged: NAD+ itself is being sold in oral capsules, sublingual troches, and patches. The premise — take the molecule you want to raise, skip the precursor step — sounds obvious. In practice, NAD+ is the one form of the molecule you specifically do not want to ingest orally.

The Molecular Size Problem

NAD+ is a dinucleotide with molecular weight ~663 Da, well above the ~500 Da threshold for typical passive intestinal absorption. It is also highly polar and charged at physiological pH. Human pharmacokinetic studies show that oral NAD+ is extensively degraded by CD38 and CD73 enzymes in the gut and plasma, yielding nicotinamide (NAM) and ribose as breakdown products. Essentially, you pay for NAD+ and absorb nicotinamide at roughly the efficiency of taking nicotinamide directly — at a fraction of the cost.

Why Precursors Are Superior

NMN (nicotinamide mononucleotide) and NR (nicotinamide riboside) are specifically designed to bypass this degradation. NR uses dedicated nucleoside transporters in cells and is efficiently phosphorylated to NMN intracellularly. NMN has its own dedicated transporter (Slc12a8 in some tissues, debated in others) and is converted to NAD+ by NMNAT enzymes. Both precursors produce measurable increases in tissue NAD+ at practical oral doses. Direct NAD+ does not.

The IV and Sublingual Question

Intravenous NAD+ bypasses the digestive problem and does deliver systemic NAD+, though at high cost and with rapid clearance. Sublingual NAD+ troches are intermediate — avoiding gastric degradation but still facing blood and tissue enzymes. The pharmacokinetic data on sublingual NAD+ are not compelling compared to sublingual NMN, which has more supportive human data.

Cost-Per-Precursor-Equivalent

A 300 mg dose of oral NAD+ typically costs $2–4 per day. A 300 mg dose of NMN or NR, which produces a far more meaningful rise in NAD+, typically costs $1–2 per day. The premium pricing of direct NAD+ products is not matched by performance in the clinical data.

Practical Recommendation

For oral supplementation to raise NAD+, choose NR or NMN at 250–1,000 mg/day. Direct oral NAD+ is not a useful upgrade. IV NAD+ is reserved for specific clinical contexts and carries its own cost and safety considerations.

Sources

  1. Rajman L, et al. "Therapeutic potential of NAD-boosting molecules: the in vivo evidence." Cell Metabolism, 2018.
  2. Trammell SA, et al. "Nicotinamide riboside is uniquely and orally bioavailable in mice and humans." Nature Communications, 2016.
  3. Yoshino J, et al. "NAD+ intermediates: the biology and therapeutic potential of NMN and NR." Cell Metabolism, 2018.
Guide

Folic Acid vs Folate (5-MTHF): Why Your MTHFR Status Changes the Answer

Mar 18, 2026 · 8 min read

Folic acid is the synthetic oxidized form of folate used in food fortification and most multivitamins since the 1998 US mandate. L-methylfolate (5-MTHF, also called methylfolate or 5-methyltetrahydrofolate) is the biologically active form that participates directly in methylation. The debate over which form to supplement is one of the most actively discussed in nutritional genomics, and the answer depends meaningfully on MTHFR genotype.

How the Body Handles Folic Acid

Folic acid must be reduced in two steps by dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) to produce tetrahydrofolate, which is then methylated to 5-MTHF. Human DHFR has relatively low capacity for folic acid reduction, and doses above ~400 µg regularly produce detectable levels of unmetabolized folic acid (UMFA) in plasma. UMFA is not directly usable and may interfere with folate receptor function. Long-term health effects of chronic UMFA are debated; the precautionary position has pushed many clinicians toward methylated folate.

The MTHFR Variants

MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase) converts 5,10-MTHF to 5-MTHF. The C677T variant (present in ~30% of the population heterozygous, ~10% homozygous) reduces enzyme activity by 35–70%. In homozygous T/T individuals, folic acid supplementation still results in elevated plasma homocysteine compared to methylfolate supplementation — a meaningful functional difference. For these individuals, 5-MTHF is pharmacologically superior.

Preconception and Pregnancy

The neural tube defect prevention evidence underlying folate recommendations was established with folic acid, and folic acid remains the evidence-based choice in pregnancy for women with normal MTHFR status. For women with MTHFR variants or a history of pregnancy loss, 5-MTHF at 400–800 µg/day is a reasonable substitute supported by European obstetric guidelines. Total folate intake (fortified food + supplement) matters more than form in most cases.

Depression and Methylation

L-methylfolate at 7.5–15 mg/day has FDA medical food approval as an adjunct to SSRIs for treatment-resistant depression (Deplin). The mechanism involves methyl group donation for neurotransmitter synthesis. MTHFR variant carriers who have had inadequate SSRI response often show improvement with adjunctive methylfolate. This is a specific clinical application, not a general recommendation.

Practical Recommendation

For general supplementation in MTHFR-normal individuals: folic acid is acceptable at 400 µg/day. For MTHFR variant carriers, pregnancy with prior losses, or treatment-resistant depression: 5-MTHF at 400–800 µg/day (or therapeutic doses for depression under clinician supervision). Avoid high-dose folic acid (>1,000 µg) chronically without reason.

Sources

  1. Scaglione F, Panzavolta G. "Folate, folic acid and 5-methyltetrahydrofolate are not the same thing." Xenobiotica, 2014.
  2. Obeid R, et al. "Is 5-methyltetrahydrofolate an alternative to folic acid for the prevention of neural tube defects?" Journal of Perinatal Medicine, 2013.
  3. Papakostas GI, et al. "L-methylfolate as adjunctive therapy for SSRI-resistant major depression: results of two randomized, double-blind, parallel-sequential trials." American Journal of Psychiatry, 2012.
Guide

L-Theanine + Caffeine: Exact Doses, Timing, and Why the Combination Works

Mar 17, 2026 · 7 min read

L-theanine is an amino acid unique to tea leaves (Camellia sinensis) and a handful of mushroom species. Alone, it produces mild relaxation without sedation. Combined with caffeine, it produces one of the most reliably documented cognitive enhancement effects in nutritional pharmacology — focused attention with reduced jitter, at predictable doses.

The 2:1 Ratio

Haskell et al. (2008) first systematized the evidence: 100 mg L-theanine + 50 mg caffeine produced faster reaction times, improved accuracy on attention-switching tasks, and reduced mind-wandering compared to either compound alone. Subsequent trials have replicated this with 200 mg theanine + 100 mg caffeine and variations up to 400 mg + 200 mg. The 2:1 ratio of theanine to caffeine is the most-studied and most-reliable combination. Ratios below 1:1 lose the relaxation effect; ratios above 3:1 show diminishing returns.

Mechanism: Opposing Actions on the Same Systems

L-theanine increases alpha brain wave activity (associated with calm, focused states), increases GABA, and modulates glutamatergic transmission. Caffeine antagonizes adenosine receptors, increasing alertness and reducing perceived fatigue. The combination produces sympathetic activation without anxiety — you get the alertness of caffeine without the activation of the stress response that normally accompanies it. Theanine also blunts the sleep-disrupting effects of caffeine taken in the afternoon.

Timing and Dosing

Peak effects occur 30–45 minutes after ingestion, with the combined effect lasting 3–4 hours. For study, work, or creative tasks: 200 mg theanine + 100 mg caffeine 30 minutes before the task. For afternoon energy without sleep disruption: 100 mg theanine + 50 mg caffeine before 2 PM. Tea already contains roughly a 2:1 theanine:caffeine ratio, which is why a cup of green tea produces calm focus while an equivalent caffeine dose from coffee can feel jittery.

Special Populations

L-theanine also shows benefit as a standalone anxiolytic at 200–400 mg, without the sedation of benzodiazepines or the dependence liability. It is safe in combination with most medications, but caffeine interactions (beta-blockers, fluoroquinolones, anticoagulants) apply normally. Individuals sensitive to caffeine can use theanine alone for mild stress reduction.

Sources

  1. Haskell CF, et al. "The effects of L-theanine, caffeine and their combination on cognition and mood." Biological Psychology, 2008.
  2. Owen GN, et al. "The combined effects of L-theanine and caffeine on cognitive performance and mood." Nutritional Neuroscience, 2008.
  3. Einother SJ, et al. "L-theanine and caffeine improve task switching but not intersensory attention or subjective alertness." Appetite, 2010.
Myth

Barley Grass vs Wheatgrass: What the Superfood Powders Actually Do

Mar 16, 2026 · 6 min read

Barley grass and wheatgrass powders have been staples of the "green superfood" category for decades, marketed as concentrated vehicles for chlorophyll, enzymes, and alkaline minerals. The marketing often conflates what is true about fresh grass juice with what is true about dried powder — and what is provable with what is extrapolated.

What's Actually in the Powder

A 10 g serving of quality barley grass or wheatgrass powder provides roughly: 3–4 g protein, 1–2 g fiber, 20–30% DV vitamin K, 10–15% DV vitamin A (as beta-carotene), modest amounts of iron, magnesium, and trace B vitamins, plus polyphenols and chlorophyll. This is a respectable nutritional contribution — equivalent roughly to a serving of dark leafy greens in concentrated form. The reasonable comparison is not to a multivitamin but to a serving of spinach or kale.

Chlorophyll: Not What You Think

Chlorophyll is marketed as an "alkalinizing" and "oxygenating" compound. In reality, dietary chlorophyll is largely broken down in the gut; intact chlorophyll does not enter circulation to any meaningful degree. What it does do is bind to dietary mutagens in the GI tract (fried-food carcinogens, aflatoxins), which is a real but modest benefit. The alkaline/oxygen marketing has no physiological basis.

Enzymes: Denatured in Powder Form

Fresh grass juice contains enzymes that are sometimes credited with therapeutic effects. Dried powders have largely inactive enzymes — they are denatured by drying and further by stomach acid. The nutritional value of dried grass powder comes from the vitamins, minerals, fiber, and polyphenols, not from enzymes.

Barley vs Wheatgrass: A Near Tie

Nutritionally, the two are similar. Barley grass tends to be slightly higher in vitamin B1 and chlorophyll; wheatgrass tends to be slightly higher in vitamin C and selenium. Both are typically gluten-free when harvested before grain formation, but cross-contamination during processing is common — look for explicit gluten-free certification if relevant.

Where They Fit

Grass powders are reasonable as a convenience food for people struggling to eat enough vegetables, not as therapeutic supplements with specific clinical effects. For $1–2 per serving, you can get most of the nutrients from a salad or smoothie at much lower cost. The honest marketing position would be "a convenient way to add greens to your diet" — which is true — rather than "alkalinizing, oxygenating, enzyme-rich superfood" — which is largely marketing.

Sources

  1. Lahouar L, et al. "Therapeutic potential of young green barley leaves in prevention and treatment of chronic diseases: an overview." American Journal of Chinese Medicine, 2015.
  2. Mujoriya R, Bodla RB. "A study on wheat grass and its nutritional value." Food Science and Quality Management, 2011.
  3. Egner PA, et al. "Chlorophyllin intervention reduces aflatoxin-DNA adducts in individuals at high risk for liver cancer." PNAS, 2001.
Breakthrough

Oat Beta-Glucan: The Supplement with the FDA's Own Cholesterol Health Claim

Mar 15, 2026 · 7 min read

Oat beta-glucan is a soluble fiber extracted from the cell walls of Avena sativa. It is one of the few supplements permitted an FDA-authorized health claim: "3 grams of soluble fiber from oatmeal daily, in a diet low in saturated fat and cholesterol, may reduce the risk of heart disease." That authorization rests on roughly 40 years of consistent clinical data showing modest but reliable LDL cholesterol reduction at practical doses.

The Evidence Base

A 2014 meta-analysis of 28 randomized controlled trials (Whitehead et al.) found that 3 g/day of oat beta-glucan reduced LDL cholesterol by 0.25 mmol/L (~10 mg/dL) and total cholesterol by 0.30 mmol/L on average. Effect sizes are larger (15–20 mg/dL LDL reduction) in hypercholesterolemic patients. The FDA health claim was first authorized in 1997 and has withstood multiple evidence reviews since. The effect is smaller than statins (30–50% LDL reduction) but larger than most dietary interventions.

Mechanism: Bile Acid Sequestration

Beta-glucan forms a viscous gel in the small intestine that binds bile acids, preventing their reabsorption and forcing the liver to pull cholesterol from circulation to synthesize replacement bile acids. The mechanism is similar to prescription bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine) but gentler on GI function. Fermentation of residual beta-glucan in the colon produces short-chain fatty acids that further reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis.

Viscosity and Molecular Weight Matter

Not all oat beta-glucan is equivalent. The cholesterol-lowering effect depends on the viscosity of the gel formed in the gut, which is a function of molecular weight. Minimally processed oats and quality beta-glucan extracts retain high molecular weight; heavily processed products (extruded cereals, some bars) may have degraded beta-glucan that produces less effect at the same gram count. Products specifying molecular weight on the label are preferable for clinical use.

Beyond Cholesterol

Beta-glucan also produces meaningful reductions in postprandial glucose excursions, improves insulin sensitivity modestly, and supports satiety. These secondary benefits are additive to the cardiovascular effect. The same 3 g/day dose addresses all three endpoints.

Practical Use

3 g/day is the target dose, which equals roughly 70 g of rolled oats, 1.5 cups of cooked oatmeal, or a single scoop of a concentrated beta-glucan supplement. Take with a meal for best viscosity effect. Expect 6–8 weeks to see LDL change on a lipid panel. Beta-glucan is extremely safe, with transient gas or bloating the only common side effect, which resolves with continued use.

Sources

  1. Whitehead A, et al. "Cholesterol-lowering effects of oat beta-glucan: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials." American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2014.
  2. Ho HV, et al. "The effect of oat beta-glucan on LDL-cholesterol, non-HDL-cholesterol and apoB for CVD risk reduction: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised-controlled trials." British Journal of Nutrition, 2016.
  3. US Food and Drug Administration. "Food labeling: health claims; soluble dietary fiber from certain foods and coronary heart disease." Federal Register, 1997.
Safety

Melanotan II and Peptide Tanning: The Illegal Compounds Behind Gym Blackmarkets

Mar 14, 2026 · 7 min read

Melanotan II is a synthetic analogue of alpha-melanocyte stimulating hormone (α-MSH) originally developed by the University of Arizona in the 1980s as an investigational drug for pigmentation disorders. It was never approved for human use anywhere in the world. Despite this, melanotan II is widely sold through online peptide suppliers and gym-based blackmarkets as an injectable tanning and libido-enhancing compound. The safety profile justifies its unapproved status.

What It Actually Does

Melanotan II is a non-selective melanocortin receptor agonist. Activation of MC1R in melanocytes stimulates melanin production, producing a deep tan without UV exposure. Activation of MC3 and MC4 receptors in the central nervous system produces the "side effects" often marketed as benefits: increased libido and spontaneous erections (mediated by MC4). The drug was investigated clinically for erectile dysfunction under the name bremelanotide (now approved as Vyleesi for premenopausal HSDD in women) at much lower doses and with more receptor selectivity.

The Documented Harms

Case reports and case series in dermatology and emergency medicine journals describe: eruption of new and rapidly growing melanocytic nevi, one-sided darkening of existing moles (asymmetric hyperpigmentation), kidney infarction, priapism, rhabdomyolysis, and acute encephalopathy. A 2018 case series in JAMA Dermatology documented melanoma in patients using melanotan II — though the causal relationship between melanin stimulation and melanoma risk remains debated, the rapid mole changes are alarming and warrant dermatologic evaluation.

Product Quality

Because melanotan II is unregulated, product purity varies enormously. Independent mass spectrometry analyses of blackmarket peptides have found inconsistent dosing, bacterial endotoxin contamination, and wrong compound entirely. Injectable products carry additional sterility risks when self-administered outside medical settings. Abscesses, cellulitis, and systemic infections have been documented.

The Legal and Medical Reality

In the US, melanotan II is not FDA-approved for any indication and is not legal to market for human use. In the UK, Australia, and most of Europe, it is similarly restricted. Possession for personal use exists in legal gray zones in some jurisdictions but distribution is typically illegal. Medical providers have no protocols for monitoring people using melanotan II and no approved route to help with adverse events.

Safer Alternatives

For skin pigmentation effects, sunless tanners (dihydroxyacetone) and bronzers are cosmetic tools with no systemic risk. For libido, evidence-based pharmacological options (PDE5 inhibitors for erectile function; bremelanotide for female HSDD) are available through licensed medical channels with safety monitoring. The appeal of melanotan II is understandable; the risk profile is not justified for either indication.

Sources

  1. Habbema L, et al. "Risks of unregulated use of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone analogues: a review." International Journal of Dermatology, 2017.
  2. Langan EA, et al. "Melanotropic peptides: more than just 'Barbie drugs' and 'sun-tan jabs'?" British Journal of Dermatology, 2010.
  3. Brennan R, et al. "Acute neurological complications associated with use of melanotan II." Journal of Medical Toxicology, 2015.
